Lady of Crows? What do you recall?
		 What do folks in Stonetop say awaits
us, beyond the Last Door? What do
they say in/among the __?
		 What do you think awaits us? Why?
		 Who among the dead do you miss most?
		 What ghost story scared you the most
as a child?
		 When did you first see someone die?
		 Who was the first person you murdered?

69

Death and the undying

Death’s Door
When the PCs are dying and they make
the Death’s Door move (Book I, page 245),
they see the Last Door and the Lady of
Crows. The move prompts them to describe
it, but feel free to ask questions.
		 What does the Door look like? Big and
grand, small and humble, or what?
		 Is it flung open, or just ajar?
		 What appears to lie beyond?
		 Where does the Lady stand? What
does she wear? What adorns her brow?
		 What is she like? Imperious? Friendly?
Sad? Smug? Inscrutable? Or what?
		 Does she speak? What is her voice like?
What does she say?
		 What detail catches your attention,
that you’d never have expected?
		 How much of this will you remember?
As for the afterlife—the land beyond the
Last Door—its true nature is a mystery.
But it is home to dool spirits (page 83),
gwyllgi (page 84), and the shades (page
85) of the dead. Some of those shades,
when called, are eager to stay in the land of
the living. So for some, it would seem, the
afterlife is not a pleasant place at all.

70

Death and the undying

The Lady of Crows
She has many titles. Carrion Queen and
Black-Feather Woman. The Kindly One.
The Keeper of Secrets. She Who Dances
on the Field of Battle. None know her true
name, if she even has one. Most call her
simply: the Lady of Crows.
Her nature and demeanor are for you and
your table to decide, but ask/consider:
		 What is her role? Psychopomp? Judge?
Ruler of the afterlife? Or what?
		 What does she want? What pleases her?
		 Is she worshipped as a god? If so, what
are her observances (page 184)?
		 What is her origin said to be?
		 What is her relationship to the Pale
Hunter (page 292)?
		 How do tales of her differ among __?
What is notably the same?

Death and the undying

Haunted sites
These are places where the undead linger, or
where the Last Door stands ajar. If the site
itself has yet to be established, combine the
terrain in which it’s located with a feature.
1d12
1-3

feature

A creepy (un)natural landmark (a
dool tree, a skull-shaped rock, etc.)
4-5
A liminal place (a pass, a bridge, a
tunnel, a gate, a crossroad, etc.)
6-8
A tomb/barrow/cemetery, left by
the locals or their ancestors
9-10 An abandoned home/fort/steading
11-12 A ruin of the Makers (page 264) or
the ﻿Barrow Builders (page 34), or
a lingering sign of their presence

Once the site’s physical nature is established, pick or roll a theme (page 68),
how long it’s been haunted, and the origins of the haunting.
1d12

how long

1

Since the days of the Makers (page
264), at least
2-3
Since the Time of Cataclysm
(page 413), when the Makers fell
4-5
Since the Barrow Builders (page
34), after the Makers’ fall
6-7
For generations, longer than the
living remember
8-9
For decades, within living memory
10-11 For the past decade at most
12
Not long at all, a recent change

The PCs are exploring Three Coven Lake (page 438). They’ve found an ancient Stone
Lord (page 382) workshop carved into a bluff above the lake. I think it’s haunted! The
nature of the site is established, so I don’t roll for a feature. I roll a 6 for theme (“ignoble
death”), a 6 for how long (“for generations”), and a 4 for origins (“the death of a few,
intimate and intense”). Because deaths were involved, I roll another 5 for the cause of death
(“exposure/cold/thirst/starvation”). I ponder a bit, and come up with this:
A century ago, some adventurers explored these chambers. A great slab of stone fell into
place, trapping them in a side chamber. A grate in the ceiling (far out of reach) provided
enough fresh air and rainwater to sustain them for several grueling weeks, but one of them
murdered and ate his fellows before slowly wasting away.
His soul lingers as a wraith (page 79), tethered
to his bones.

Dool trees
Dool trees often grow in or near
haunted sites. A grove stands near
the Crossroads (page 270), and
lone dool trees are often found near
Barrow Builder tombs (page 34).
They are thick and knobby and
multi-stemmed. The bark is papery,
the color of bleached bone. The Pale
Hunter (page 292) shuns them, and
they are often home to dool spirits
(page 83) if nothing else.
The leaves of a dool tree are deep
purple, almost black, shot through
with pale white veins. Dried leaves
can be steeped to make a bitter tea,
which brings on a death-like sleep
from which some folk never wake.

Death and the undying

A blackened splinter
crude, magical, fireproof
Undead who see it are drawn to it, or else
deeply agitated by its presence. When you
touch the splinter to an undead entity, they
see the Last Door yawning open before
them. Roll 4d6. If the total is greater than
the entity’s current HP, they are drawn
through the Last Door and leave this world
for good. Otherwise, they are transfixed for
a few moments before the Door slams shut.
Regardless, the splinter vanishes.
A spindle of fine thread
magical
A length of fiber, maybe 50 feet long, spun
by a ghost from all the hair that ever fell
from her daughters’ heads. The thread is
light, and fine, and almost unbreakably
strong, but it can be handled only by one
who has seen the Last Door. Only the edge
of a silver blade can cut it. It can be used
to bind a ghost fast, if you’re clever and can
handle the thread yourself.
A pale wooden sword
, close, crude, magical
Carved from a dool tree with a silver knife,
and polished with oils infused with moonlight. The grip is worn smooth but the
“blade” is chipped and notched, unlikely to
stand up to iron or bronze.
When you wield the sword against a
manifest undead spirit, you can hurt it
(deal damage normally). If you reduce it to
0 HP, the manifest form dissipates but the
spirit can reform at its tether normally.

A tattered standard
, magical
Threadbare, ancient, but remarkably
well-preserved. Embroidered with a
stylized aurochs whose horns encompass
the sun. It fills you with hope and pride
to look upon it, as well it should. For this
was the standard of the Heol-Tarv, a heroic
band who gave their lives in hopeless battle
against the Anan Gllo (page 81), securing their people’s escape.
When you unfurl the tattered standard and
hold it aloft for all to see, you and your allies
are immune to supernatural fear and unshaken by undead or otherwise unnatural foes.

Death and the undying

Ghosts
Ghosts are the souls of the dead, those
who linger in this world. They vary wildly
in personality, motives, and capabilities,
but all have the undead and spirit tags, and
a move that reflects them manifesting a
presence in the world. They are animated
by more than biology (+4 HP) and lack
vital organs (+3 HP, +1 armor).
Consider the context in which they are
encountered. If not already established,
pick or roll for their cause of death (page
73). Then pick or roll for what they need
to move on, their manifestation, and 1-6
abilities and/or weaknesses. Establish their
tether (page 77). Let all these elements
inform their story and their stat block.
1d12
1-2

what they need

Kindness/patience/persuasion, a
guide to the Last Door
3-4
Justice (or vengeance) served
5-6
A proper burial
7
A reunion with a loved one
8
The truth, unearthed/acknowledged
9
A task completed; an oath fulfilled
10-11 Their tether, destroyed or unmade
12
To be put down, like a rabid dog

Death and the undying

Tethers
Ghosts are bound to an object or place.
Pick or roll for the tether itself, and how
tightly bound the ghost is.
1d12

tether

1-4
Their corpse/remains/bones/hair
5-6
The place where they died
7
(Part of ) their grave/pyre/altar/etc.
8
The weapon that killed them
9
An item they once cherished
10
A prominent place from their life
11-12 The focus/source of whatever magic
binds them
1d12

how tightly bound

1-2
Can’t leave its immediate presence
3-6
Must stay on the premises/site
7-10 Can roam a few miles; returns daily
11-12 Can wander far and wide, but must
return periodically

If a ghost’s manifest form is reduced to
0 HP, they dissipate and return to their
tether. They usually won’t manifest before
the next sunset; if they do, their max HP
are halved.
If a tether is destroyed, then the ghost
might… (pick 1)
…		 fade away, utterly destroyed;
…		 pass through the Last Door (now, or
after some final act);
…		 devolve into a wraith (page 79);
…		 possess a corpse, as a pyped (page 82);
or
…		 cling to their current host/form, but
fade utterly if it is destroyed.
Decide based on the ghost’s history, power,
and relationship to their tether.

Death and the undying

The Gwraig Wen
Solitary, spirit, undead, forlorn
HP 19; Armor 1 (lacks organs)
Damage bare hands d6 w/disadvantage
(hand)
Special qualities bound to within a few
miles of her bones; can’t form new
memories; leaves no footprints
Instinct to drag others into her search
ä Manifest in moonlight as a pale,
bedraggled woman
ä Ask for help, insist on it
ä Grow ever more agitated, flickering
and vibrating as she does
ä Vent her rage with a deafening wail,
d10 damage (near, area, loud, reload,
ignores armor)
ä Vanish when no one’s looking
Those who ply the Great Wood (page
200) tell an old tale of a bride, newly
married to a handsome trapper. He failed
to come home one night, and she unwisely
entered the forest to find him, falling into
a deep pond and drowning. Ever since, her
ghost has roamed the woods on moonlit
nights: wet and shivering, desperate and
pleading, certain that her husband is still
out there.

78

The Ghostly Legion
Horde, spirit, undead, organized,
terrifying, hoarder, violent
HP 10; Armor 1 (lacks organs)
Damage ghostly short sword or spear d6
w/advantage (hand, close)
Special qualities roams far and wide, but
tethered to the cave; shuns daylight;
can only touch the world through
violence; upsetting to/afraid of dogs
Instinct to lift their curse
ä Manifest a spectral form (harmed
only by silver, salt, or a loyal dog)
ä Ogle a mortal as they eat or drink
ä Threaten violence, carry through
ä Force a mortal to carry their treasure
They were a band of southern mercenaries,
come north in search of treasure. They
heard tell of a hdour (page 234) and his
hoard, trapped him in his cave, and slew
him. Then they divvied up the loot and left.
Only later did they learn why the Hillfolk
fear to kill a hdour. For he had cursed them
all. Cursed their souls to linger after death,
doomed to endless hunger and endless thirst.
Doomed to fear the sun, to be hounded like
the dogs they were. Cursed, indeed, until
every piece of the far-flung loot they stole is
returned to the hdour’s cave.

Death and the undying

Specter
					Solitary, spirit, undead, stealthy,
terrifying, cruel
HP 23; Armor 1 (lacks vitals)
Damage telekinesis d10+2 (hand, close,
near, grabby, forceful)
Special qualities bound to its tether; one
or more weaknesses (page 76)
Instinct to torment the living
ä Create eerie environmental effects
(bleeding walls, green flames, etc.)
ä Manifest a form of flitting shadow
(harmed only by silver or salt)
ä Manifest a nightmarish physical form
ä Speak through an unconscious or
catatonic victim
A specter is a soul consumed by hatred and
envy, cruelty and rage. Maybe they were a
sadistic murderer in life, or a victim whose
death was so traumatic that they lash out
at any living thing. Maybe they were a pining ghost whose loved ones rejected them,
or a fettered soul long- and cruelly-bound,
now set free. Regardless: they are now a
thing of spite and vengeance, wanting only
to punish those with the temerity to live.

Wraith
Solitary, spirit, undead, stealthy,
terrifying
HP 19; Armor 1 (lacks organs)
Damage life drain d10 (hand, ignores armor)
Special qualities bound to its tether
(usually), powerless in daylight
Instinct to feast upon living souls
ä Manifest a form of shadow and cold
(harmed only by silver and salt)
ä Hurl itself at the living
ä Suck vitality from its prey
Wraiths are cursed with endless hunger.
They are damaged souls, wounded by dark
magic, another wraith, or the dehumanizing effects of starvation or addiction. They
are little more than beasts, driven by the
emptiness within, with only the vaguest
sense of who they once were.
Other ghosts might become wraiths when
their tethers are destroyed. If they linger in
the world, they find themselves filled with
gnawing need, and must sustain themselves
on the living. These unbound wraiths are
weaker, but often form packs with others of
their kind (group, vicious instead of stealthy,
13 HP each, d8 damage, afraid of firelight).
When a wraith reduces you to 0 HP and
you survive, your soul is wounded. Reduce
your max HP by 4. Until your soul is
healed, your instinct becomes “Hunger: to
fill the emptiness inside you with excess,”
and should you roll a 6- on Death’s Door,
you die and become a wraith (a monster in
the GM’s control).
Other ghosts
		A cinderwraith (page 166)
		The Quiet Twins (page 396)
		Phantom riders (page 45)
		Ferocedes Ogran (page 347)
79

Death and the undying

Revenants
Revenants are the living dead, corpses
animated by a soul, a demon, or foul magic.
They vary wildly in personality, motives,
and capabilities. They are undead, animated
by more than biology (+4 HP).
Consider the context in which a revenant is
encountered. If not yet established, pick or
roll for their cause of death (page 73). Then
pick or roll for what they were brought back
by, their visage, and 1-6 abilities and/or
weaknesses. Let all these elements inform
their story and their stat block.

Death and the undying

Anan Gllo
					Horde, undead, corrupted, tireless,
fearless, terrifying
HP 11; Armor 5 (resilience, armor), 1 vs.
bronze or fire
Damage weapon d6 (tags by weapon) or
crushing grip d4 (hand, grabby)
Special qualities weaken over time
(see below)
Instinct to follow orders
ä Advance implacably
ä Unnerve foes with their silence, their
empty eyes
ä Fight on despite crippling injury
The Hillfolk (page 228) speak of dark
times, when the Things Below (page 412)
gave a gift to a petty chief. Depending on
who tells the tale, it was a patch of loose
soil or a deep pool of water, the hollow of a
dool tree or a great cauldron of rusted iron.
Regardless: a corpse placed within this vessel would rise as one of the Anan Gllo, the
Empty Dead. The chief raised an undead
army, and set out to make themselves king.

Dialeddwr
					Solitary, undead, terrifying, implacable
HP 20; Armor 4 (resilience), 0 vs. silver
or fire
Damage choking grip or powerful blows
d8+2 w/advantage (hand, grabby, forceful)
Special qualities cannot open doors, eat,
or drink
Instinct to avenge
ä Pass for alive, except in sunlight
ä Unerringly track a target
ä Lock eyes with someone, show them
the meaning of fear
ä Bask in moonlight to regenerate
even terrible wounds
Sometimes, when a great wrong is done,
when a good person is cruelly slain, when
the killers go unpunished… well, sometimes they come back. Called back by a
prayer or perhaps a spell. Or sent back
by their fellow victims. Or guided back
by one of the Lady’s own crows. They dig
themselves free of their graves, reborn and
whole. And vengeance shall be theirs.

Something interesting: The chief ’s power
was broken when a hero entered the vessel
while still alive, giving their life to destroy
it. A number of Anan Gllo linger, though,
inert and waiting.
Something useful: An Anan Gllo regains
all its HP when it either drinks fresh blood
or re-enters the vessel; it cannot otherwise
regain HP. And every dawn, an Anan Gllo
loses 1 HP. At 0 HP, it becomes inert until
its HP are restored.

Death and the undying

Pyped
Solitary, undead, terrifying, craven
HP 16; Armor 1 (speed)
Damage bone-snapping grip d8+2 (hand,
grabby, forceful)
Special qualities burned by silver (+1d4
damage); harmed by salt (1d8 damage,
hand, close); foul stench; sustained by
bone marrow
Instinct to avoid the Last Door
ä Move with a surprising, jerky speed
ä Jumble up its memories with those
of its host & those whose bones
it’s eaten
ä Possess a new corpse, if it can/must
When an u
﻿ ntethered ﻿ghost (page 76) or a
shade (page 85) possesses an empty corpse,
it becomes a pyped. They are wretched
things, all rotting flesh and lurching
movements, hungering for sweet marrow,
scavenging on carrion and graves. But hunger can make them bold. Bold enough to
hunt the weak and sick at the edge of a
village, or a lone traveler caught unawares.
Pypeds rise only from corpses that are
relatively intact, and which have not
undergone proper funeral rites. If a pyped
is destroyed, the spirit within will seek a
new, viable corpse. A shade must find one
before the sun banishes it. An untethered
ghost must find one before its memories
fade and it becomes a wraith (page 79).

Ulliam Unlucky
					Solitary, undead, stealthy, terrifying, loyal
HP 16; Armor 0
Damage whatever’s at hand d8 w/disadvantage (tags vary)
Special qualities burned/blinded by daylight; chill aura
Instinct to pine for his family
ä Keep out of sight, or try to
ä Get frustrated, agitated, angry
ä Lose his temper and drain heat from
someone, d10 damage w/advantage
(hand, close, reach, ignores armor)
Ulliam Unlucky dragged his family from
Marshedge (page 274) up to Gordin’s
Delve (page 190), in search of a better
life. But things never did work out. Last
time he went out prospecting, he got lost.
Then the blizzard rolled in. And that’s how
Ulliam died.
But say what you will about Ulliam, he
loved his wife and kids. Still does. Dragged
his frozen self home to see ’em again. He
knows they can’t see him like this, but he
can’t keep himself from lurking. And when
some lech comes sniffing around his wife,
or some fool notices him and makes a
fuss… well, there’s only so much a man can
take. And that little spark of warmth he
draws out of them? Well, he’s starting to
acquire a taste.

Dool spirit
Group, spirit, terrifying, stealthy, devious
HP 13; Armor 1 (lacks organs)
Damage feast on fears d6 w/disadvantage
(hand, close, ignores armor)
Special qualities powerless in bright light;
bound to its tree or a victim’s bones
Instinct to taste sweet, delicious fears
ä Sense a victim’s doubt and worries
ä Shape sound and shadow to unnerve
and frighten
ä Manifest as its victim’s fears (harmed
only by one who masters their fear)
ä Bind itself to a victim’s bones
Dool spirits hail from beyond the Last
Door. They are not undead, for they never
lived, but are native to the afterlife. They
sometimes slip into this world, haunting
the dool trees (page 73) from which
they get their name.
When a dool spirit takes a liking to a victim’s fears, it leaves its tree and binds itself
to that victim’s very bones. It proceeds to
haunt them, and only them, driving them
mad with fear. The spirit remains bound
to that victim’s bones even in death, until
a new dool tree sprouts where those bones
are buried, and the spirit can haunt the
living once more.

Gwyllgi
Solitary, spirit, cautious, stealthy,
terrifying

(hand, grabby, forceful)

manifest to feed

blazing red eyes

night and lay a death curse on them
(see below)

damage (reach, area, ignores armor)
A gwyllgi is a harbinger of death, a being
from beyond. A necromancer might call
one forth to send against their foes. Or,
one might slip through the Last Door
when dark magic or the death of many
cause it to swing wide open.
Death curse
Threat (affliction)
Instinct to crank up tension and dread
ä Cause an accident or coincidence
ä Drive them to anxiety, desperation
ä Push them towards a bad decision
ä Get them, in the end
When a gwyllgi curses a victim, they know
they will die before the next full moon.
Something interesting: A gwyllgi can
curse only one victim at a time, and it
manifests each night to feed on its victim’s
emotions.
Something useful: You can lift the curse
by destroying the gwyllgi’s form, banishing
it back through the Last Door.

Necromancer
Solitary, cautious, cunning, magical,
death-wise
HP 12; Armor 0 (or by worn armor)
Damage silver-alloy knife d8 (hand)
Special qualities can sense active spirits
Instinct to hoard secrets/knowledge
ä Call up, speak with, bind, or lose
control of an undead entity
ä Reveal or act on knowledge they
should not possess
ä Send the undead against a foe
ä Cast a death-related spell (reload)
‘Tis an odd sort, who would have truck
with spirits of the dead. But some are born
with the Sight, and a morbid curiosity. If
they can avoid getting themselves killed
by hungry ghosts or outraged locals…
well, the dead have all manner of secrets
to teach.
Necromancers often find themselves consorting with entities even darker than the
dead, and are prone to corruption (page
430) by the Things Below. They are likely
to possess one or more arcana (page 508),
which they do not hesitate to use. And
they are often attended by one or more
bound entities.

Death and the undying

Shade
Solitary, spirit, undead, cautious
HP 19; Armor 1 (lacks organs)
Damage none
Special qualities banished by daylight
Instinct (pick 1 or roll 1d6) 1: to refuse
to go back, 2: to fulfill its longings,
3-4: to bemoan its fate, 5-6: to return
to its rest
ä Manifest as a whisper and a dim
image, unable to affect the physical
world
ä Share what it knew in life
ä Reveal a secret learned in death
ä “Ride” a willing/prepared host
ä Possess an intact and uninterred
corpse, creating a pyped (page 82)
Shades are the souls of the quiet dead,
called back through the Last Door. Or
perhaps they are merely echoes of a dead
person’s personality, given shape by magic.
Either way, they might be called up at the
Crossroads (page 270), via an arcanum
like a strange skull and antlers (page 520),
or by some other form of necromancy.

Tomb-bog spirit
Solitary, spirit, terrifying, implacable
HP 19; Armor 1 (lacks organs)
Damage smothering grip d10+2 (hand,
forceful, grabby)
Special qualities harms only those whose
life is forfeit
Instinct to consume the dying breaths of
the condemned
ä Track down one whose life is forfeit
ä Wait until they are alone
ä Shape a hulking form from peat,
soil, and mud (takes half damage
from stabbing, slashing, or fire)
ä Whisper its victim’s crimes, freezing
them with fear
In Ferrier’s Fen (page 116), not far from
Marshedge (page 274), there lies a peat
mound where the town buries criminals
alive. Over the years, the place has given
birth to a spirit of death and punishment.
The spirit is young and eager, and can be
set to work upon criminals; see the grim
peat mound (page 511) minor arcanum
for details.

A shade can ride a living, willing host, or
one who has been properly prepared (with
arcane markings, by bearing an appropriate
object, via a potion, etc.). It can withstand
daylight while doing so, but only with great
effort. It experiences what its host experiences, and can communicate with its host
via only impulse and desire.
When a shade rides you and compels you to
act against your will, mark XP if you do as it
wants. If you resist, you are Defying Danger.

85

The Dread River
The Dread River is the eastern border
of the World’s End. It flows down from
the Whitefang Mountains (page 480),
through the North and South Manmarch
(pages 282 and 350), fed by numerous
creeks and streams along the way.

In its lower reach, the river itself is wide and
deep, never less than a quarter-mile across
and dozens—if not hundreds—of feet deep.
From afar, it appears placid and still. But it
flows with a terrible and relentless current.
The western shore alternates between wetlands, wide-open flood plains, and rugged
bluffs, ruins scattered all along its length. It
would be an idyllic place to live, except that
the length of the river is cursed.

Impressions
Always
		From afar: a sense of stillness
		As you get closer: the rush of the current
		The far shore: ruin-dotted, lonely,
mysterious
		Near the water: an uneasy feeling,
like anything could be in there—
watching, waiting, hungry
Spring
		The river swollen and flooded from
snowmelt, spilling out over its banks
		Swarms of flies, like clouds of smoke
		The smell of mud and rotting fish
Summer
		Brilliant green along the shores, sunlight
sparkling on water
		Endless chorus of waterfowl, starlings,
insects, current
		The occasional splash—just a fish, or a
spooked turtle, maybe?
86

Autumn
		Riot of autumn colors
along the shores, reflected
in the water
		Blankets of mist, hiding the river
but amplifying its rush and gurgle
		Honk and flap of waterfowl flying south,
or wheeling overhead before alighting
Winter
		White-capped trees and bluffs and slopes
		Ice growing out from the shores
		Slate grey water, more menacing than
ever with the promise of deadly cold

The Dread River

Questions
		 What famous treasure is said to lie in
the Ruins at Road’s End?
		 What once drove you to spend a night
encamped along the Dread River?
What evil did you encounter?
		 Who do you know who once saw the
Suileach, the Wurm with Two-Hundred Eyes? How did it change them?
		 Aside from empty ruins, what is
noticeably strange about the lands
across the river?
		 Does one cross the Dread River en route
to Lygos? If so, how? And what separates
the southern lands from those east of the
river? Mountains? A desert? A sea?

Hooks
		Bandits (page 355) or cultists (page
435) take up residence in ruins on the
Dread River’s shores.
		The PCs acquire a cursed treasure
(page 90), originally looted from the
Ruins at Road’s End.
		Some fool is mounting an expedition
to cross the Dread River.
		 People from east of the river
(page 88) land on the
western shore.

The Ruins at Road’s End
The Highway (page 270) runs from Barrier
Pass (page 26) through Marshedge (page
274) and ends at the Dread River. Where
the road ends, there was once a sizeable city, a
bustling hub of trade and learning.
The Tempest Lords (page 398) called
this city “Northernmost,” for it was
founded as their most far-flung outpost.
The Stone Lords (page 382) called it
Aavonkaer (“River City”). To the Green
Lords (page 210) it was Pazara (“The
Marketplace”). The Rime Lords (page
316) called it Jalampravahati (“Through
Where the Waters Flow”).
Now, though, the city is called the Ruins
at Road’s End. For when the Stone Lords’
servants rebelled and woke the Things Below (page 412), the Dread River belched
forth countless horrors. The city’s inhabitants were slaughtered in a single night.
Throughout the time of the BarrowBuilders (page 34), various warlords
and would-be sorcerer-kings claimed the
ruined city. They sought to recover the lost
artifacts of the Makers, or to harness the
city’s restless souls, or to bend the horrors
in the river to their will. Sometimes, they
even succeeded. But never for very long.
The ruins sprawl for miles along the shore,
and similar vestiges of the past are visible
across the river. The legs of a once-mighty
bridge still poke out from the water. Everyone agrees that great wealth and troves of
lore remain among the crumbling walls.
But everyone also agrees that those
old cellar holes and skeletal structures—along with anything dug
out of them—are cursed
(page 90).
87

The Dread River

East of the River

Terrain

Beyond the Dread River lie more ruins,
bluffs and woods, and gently sloping plains.
The Manmarchers (page 282) migrated
from east of the Dread River sometime before the Time of Cataclysm (page 413).
But if people dwell there still, they keep
well away from the shore. And if any have
ventured across the river and returned,
they’re keeping quiet about it.

Pick 1 or combine 2, or have someone roll
the Die of Fate (d12).

Who or what lies east of the Dread River?
That’s for you and your group to decide,
but perhaps one might find…
…		 the husk of a sprawling empire, home
now to only jackals and wolves;
…		 nomadic raiders, bloody-handed even
by the Manmarchers’ standards;
…		 descendants of the Makers (page 264),
greatly diminished, in isolated fiefs
…		 a people in thrall to an undying
sorcerer-king;
…		 a land of horrors, thoroughly tainted
by the Things Below (page 412);
…		 a barren land, dead and still, bereft of
spirits and magic;
…		 the Forest Folk (page 150);
…		 some combination of the above; or
…		 something else entirely.

Cursed object
When you remove something valuable
from the ruins that dot the Dread River, roll
(+nothing, just roll). If you have warded
yourself from the evil of this place, roll
with advantage. On a 10+, your theft goes
unnoticed, or at least unpunished; on a 7-9,
the GM will choose (or ask you to roll for)
a lesser curse; on a 6-, the GM will choose
(or ask you to roll for) a greater curse.
1d6

lesser curse

1

Food and drink become bland, then
off-putting, then vile (yet you must
still eat and drink)
Nightmares plague you and those
with whom you share a roof; restful
sleep is fleeting
Wild beasts attack you with unnatural frequency and focus; domestic
beasts are agitated in your presence
You age quickly, a year for
every month
You cannot abide the sound of
singing/the touch of loved ones/
food cooked by another/any gift
freely given/some other thing that
ties you to other people
You hear whispers and visions
(page 428), urging you to do things

2
3
4
5

6

The Dread River
1d6

greater curse

1

Horrors pursue you, intent on
dragging you and the item back
to the ruins, killing any who cross
their path. Destroy or bind them,
and more will come.
You contract a disease (page 122);
it merely makes you miserable, but
infects those nearby with a deadly
sickness.
Those around you find themselves
on edge, their emotions running
raw, and are subject to whispers
and visions (page 428). Unstable
folk quickly succumb.
You grow thin, haggard, and
unwell. Mark a debility each week,
which you cannot clear. Should you
die, you become a revenant (Book
I, page 150), tethered to the item,
obsessed with either protecting
it or returning it to where it was
found (your choice).
Wherever you go, plants wither,
food spoils, crops fail, beasts grow
barren and sickly.
Those who die in your presence are
unable to pass through the Last
Door. They linger as revenants
(page 80) or ghosts (page 76).

2

3

4

5
6

Tailor a curse’s specifics to the item, to
where and with what it was it found. Incorporate the themes and aspects of any relevant entities. And feel free to create entirely
new curses, riffing on the options above.
How does one free oneself of a curse?
Possibilities include…
…		 returning the item to where it was
originally found;
…		 destroying the item utterly: burning it,
melting it, tossing it into lava, etc.;
…		 packing it in salt and burying it at a
crossroads;
…		 driving a bronze spike through it, into
an ancient elder tree;
…		 ritually cleansing the item, binding the
curse to an appropriate vessel, and
burying/hiding the vessel somewhere;
or perhaps
…		 passing ownership of the item (and
thus the curse) on to another willing
party; they need not know it is cursed,
but must claim the item as theirs.

The Dread River

Luglfsk
Horde, small, amphibious, corrupted
HP 3; Armor 1 (hide)
Damage electric shock d6 (hand, reload), or
chew d4 (hand)
Special qualities lightning-proof
Instinct to feed
			ä Sense a creature’s electrical field
			ä Stun or paralyze with a touch, bzzzt
			ä Swarm a stunned creature
Each one is a few feet long, like a cuttlefish
crossed with a giant maggot, squirming
wet and gray, a face full of feelers whose
touch stings like lightning.
They spend most of their lives in the water,
congregating near power-crystals (page
389) and aetherium artifacts (page 404)
that have been claimed by the river. They
seem entranced by the passive energy, almost
lulled to sleep. But when they sense something more dynamic, something meatier, they
wriggle out of the water fast enough.
Some Hillfolk claim that luglfsk are just
tadpoles, and tell stories of larger, many-legged terrors that fling lightning from
face-feelers that stretch as long as a man
grows tall. But those are probably just
spook-stories to scare travelers. Probably.

92

Jellied horror
					Solitary, large, stealthy, amorphous,
			mindless
HP 19; Armor 1 (lacks organs)
Damage pseudopod d10+1 (reach, hand,
			grabby, messy, 1 piercing)
Special qualities immune to stabbing; takes
half damage from weapons unless
they’re forceful; acid-proof; harmed by
chalk/quicklime/similar chemics
Instinct to seep, to consume, to grow
			ä Lurk in water, moving slowly, seeking
or waiting for food
			ä Dissolve organic material—weapons,
clothes, flesh, etc.
			ä Engulf, d10+3 damage with advantage
(hand, messy, ignores armor)
Sometimes, the waters of the Dread River
overflow their banks and flood the cursed
ruins and tainted places. And sometimes,
when the floodwaters recede, they leave
behind… something. Something that just
isn’t right.

The Dread River

The Suileach
					Solitary, huge, magical, terrifying,
aquatic, corrupted
HP 26; Armor 2 (scales)
Damage jaws like a cave d10+7 (reach,
forceful, messy, 3 piercing) or thrashing
coils d10+5 (close, area, forceful)
Special qualities many-eyed; impossible
to surprise
Instinct to dominate the Dread River
			ä Sense an intruder upon its domain
			ä Transfix someone with one of its
myriad eyes (see below)
			ä Drag intruders to their doom
It’s a thing of legend. A great scaly wurm,
they say, hundreds of feet long, festooned
with eyes along its bulk. Hundreds of eyes,
so very many. They say it sees all that
happens along the river. They say it brooks
no tresspass, that it drowns or devours all
who encroach on its domain.
But that’s not entirely true. Some folks have
met the Suileach and lived to tell the tale,
haunted by what they saw reflected in its eyes.
When you stare transfixed into one of the
Suileach’s eyes, mark dazed and pick 1:
		You see yourself, unburdened. Name an
NPC whom you love; your heart grows
cold to them. Then, gain a new move
from your playbook.
		You see yourself as you might have been.
Change your instinct and swap one of
your playbook moves for another.
		You see your death, as it will occur. The
next time you would roll for Death’s
Door, don’t—you get a 10+. The
second time you would roll for Death’s
Door, don’t—you get a 6-.
		You see the truth reflected, darkly. The
GM will tell you something interesting
and useful, yet thoroughly disturbing.

Totnatter
					Solitary, large, stealthy, terrifying
HP 16; Armor 2
Damage venomous bite d10+1 (reach, close,
hand, 1 piercing)
Special qualities thermal vision; extraordinary sense of smell
Instinct to toy with its food
			ä Scent prey, track it down
			ä Rattle its tail, paralyzing prey with fear
			ä Pump them full of venom, then retreat
			ä Follow, waiting for its prey to collapse
These grey-white rattlesnakes grow to
absurd size, big enough to eat a boar, a
deer, or even a person. Manmarchers say
that they haunt the bluffs and ruins along
the river, waiting months, years, or even
decades between meals.
While most rattlesnakes rattle to warn off
threats or predators, totnatter use their
rattles to hunt. They delight in the terror
their rattle causes, and use it even when
they could just bite their prey unawares.
When a totnatter pumps its prey full of
venom, start the following countdown:
l		 Their hands shake, their bodies shiver

(mark weakened)

l		 They grow confused, thinking things

that aren’t true (mark dazed)

l		 They struggle to breathe, to keep going

(mark miserable)

l		 They are dying (roll for Death’s Door)

93

Fae

						An uprising began. Rebels
			 gutted tombs, burned plantations, put
their masters to the spear. Gangs of free
Fae roved the Great Wood, burning, liberating, recruiting. Gangs became armies.

Strange folk live in the Great Wood (page
200) and Ferrier’s Fen (page 116).
Wondrous and dangerous, beings out of
legend. The Forest Folk (page 150) called
them “Good Neighbors” and “Hidden
Ones,” and took pains to avoid insulting
them. They cautioned Stonetop to do the
same. And while the Forest Folk are gone,
the Fae remain.
The Green Lords (page 210) bred the
Fae long ago, to herd spirits and to weave
wondrous realms. They relied on the Fae to
manage their estates, to build the waystones (page 478), and, later, to create the
Green Lord’s idyllic afterlives.
Some Fae resented their servitude. Some
found loopholes in the promises and
oaths that bound them. Ringleaders arose,
creating webs of debt and obligation that
undermined their masters’ hold.
94

The Green Lords grew desperate. They
called on the Things Below (page 412),
corrupted themselves with unholy power.
They bred living weapons, hunted down
the rebels, and took terrible vengeance.
The uprising nearly failed, but the Green
Lords had drawn the ire of their fellow
Makers. The Stone Lords (page 382),
in particular, were horrified by the Green
Lords’ corruption. They worked a terrible
spell to shatter the heart of the Green
Lord’s domain, turning it into the ruined
bog that today we call Ferrier’s Fen. Then
they allied with the Fae, and waged war
against the Green Lords who survived.
After the siege of Vor Svetelik (page
468), the Fae found themselves adrift and
bereft of purpose. Many were no longer
bound by oath or obligation. Ringleaders
bickered and squabbled. Factions formed.
Armies disbanded. And the Fae became
the fractured people they are today.

The Fae

Themes
Choose or roll 1 (or 2 and combine).

The good neighbors
Fae and mortals cross paths, of course. The
Forest Folk descended from the Green
Lord’s mortal servants, and dwelt in the
Great Wood for centuries. Fae fought
side-by-side with the Stone Lords’ human
slaves. And some Fae, certainly, know of the
humans living on their borders: Stonetop,
Marshedge (page 274), the Manmarchers
(page 282), even some Whitefang villages
(page 483). There have always been dealings between them and us.
But Fae are not like us. They breathe magic. Time means little to them. They have
no base needs. They are driven by pride,
boredom, and obsession. They are shaped
by their debts, their obligations, and the
ancient scars of servitude and war.
Fae are dangerous to mortals. Maybe not
hostile (though some are), but alien. Some
admire our spark of life, our creativity and
passion. Some find our memories and our
emotions rich and heady. Some delight in
our dignity or our short-lived antics. Some
might even care about us. But they also
think no more of stealing an infant from
the crib than we would at picking a pup
out of a litter.

1d12

theme

1

Fluidity of time and space: stasis,
distortion, dilation, things where
(and when) they oughtn’t be
Enchantment, dreams and dreamlogic, the odd accepted as normal
Illusion/glamour, often hiding a
base or even sordid reality
The intangible made real; metaphor
made literal
Promises, deals, and loopholes;
debts and insults weighing heavy as
iron chains
Opaque etiquette, strange norms,
values upended, seeming amorality
Passions, whims, and excess; the
autonomy of others ignored
Extremes of beauty/ugliness,
blurred lines between them
Malleability of one’s looks, one’s
identity, one’s true and secret heart
Liminality: neither spirit nor flesh,
here nor there, this nor that, real
nor unreal
Deep ties to the natural world and
spirits of the wild (page 356)
Enslavement by the Green Lords
(page 210), the war against them,
the lasting scars left by both

Fae

Hooks

Lore

		A local family claims their infant has
been replaced with a Fae changeling.
		A widower wants a Fae to take away
the memories of his lost love.
		A hunter naively offers Stonetop’s
hospitality to a mischievous Fae.
		A zrajedak (page 223) is released
from stasis; terrified Fae seek help.
		An NPC is indebted to a Fae; it wants
what they cannot bear to give (a child,
their love, a marriage, etc.).
		A Fae falls in love with a villager, and
tries to live a mortal life.

Everyone in Stonetop knows that:

Questions
In general:
		 Who among you has lost a sibling or a
child, stolen from the crib?
		 Who in the village is said to have
Fae blood?
		 Who in town once made a deal with a
Fae? What did they lose? What did
they get in return?
		 What Fae being did you once meet
in the Wood? How did you get away
unscathed?
About any given Fae they meet:
		 What about their presence fills you
with awe/longing/sadness/joy/__?
		 What forgotten memory do they stir?
		 What detail about them will you
struggle to remember later?
		 What detail could you never forget?

96

		Strange, magical folk dwell in the
Great Wood
		They vary greatly in appearance, ability,
and disposition
		That they are strange, dreamlike, fickle,
wondrous, dangerous
One might know:
		Details of a given Fae: behaviors,
demeanor, abilities, limits, names, etc.
		The specifics of Fae nature (page 98)
		That iron is anathema to them
		Proper etiquette for dealing with one
		Ways to protect yourself from them

dangers they pose, specific examples
		Tales of the Wandering Market
		That they served the Green Lords

		The specifics of their history, their
origin, their rebellion
		The nuances of their politics, debts,
relationships, obligations
		How to find the Wandering Market
		The metaphysics of
their existence

Fae

Names
Fae “names” tend to be titles, nouns, or
descriptive phrases or borrowed from those
they’ve met—simple, whimsical, ominous,
or grandiose as befits their nature.
Mix, match, and riff on words related to
plants and beasts and rocks, features of
the landscape, natural phenomena, colors,
mundane objects, emotions or emotional
activities, titles, relations, and local names.
Some simple names: Acorn, Cobweb, Hob,
Jackdaw, Kettle, Lark, Moth, Quills, Rill,
Robin, Stewpot, Trillium, Uncle Whiskers,
Whistler, Wobbles, Yellowtail
Some whimsical names: Bellweather,
Bitterlips, Moonbeam, Peasebottom,
Poppy o’ Twilight, Rainpetal, Stagthorn,
Thistledust, Whiteoak, Yellow Britches

Some ominous names: Bonekettle, Father
Longfingers, Grinning Nel, the Hangman,
the King of Dirges, Old Sorrows, the Pale
Sisters, Stonetooth, the Weeping Rose
Some grandiose names: Dawnwhisper,
Fireblossom, Breath of Thunder, Hare
Starblade, the Midnight Mare, Rain Upon
the Valley, Silverstag, the Storm King
Some Fae might go by many names, or
change their names over time. If a given
Fae is known to the locals, consider what
they call it in their local tongue. A Fae
named Moonbeam, for example, might be
called “Pelydrlloer” in Stonetop or “Mondstrohl” by the Manmarchers.
Perhaps each Fae has a true and secret
name, by which they can be commanded
and bound. If so, it must be their most
treasured and precious secret.

97

Fae

Fae nature
The Fae are living beings made of spiritstuff. They aren’t quite spirits, but interact
with spirits as if they were. They’re made
of flesh and bone, or close enough, but it is
strange, malleable, and resilient. They…
…		 need not eat or drink, but can enjoy
such fare;
…		 need not sleep, but often choose to;
…		 can suffocate or drown, but usually
recover when they can breathe again;
…		 feel no drive to reproduce, but might
enjoy sex, fall in love, sire or bear children, steal them from mortals;
…		 do not grow feeble with age, but can
grow bored, deeply set in their ways;
…		 cannot break a promise or an oath, but
are keen at finding loopholes; and
…		 feel the weight of debts and insults as
though they were heavy chains.
Injury
Fae are unnaturally tough (Armor 4) and
can heal any wound not caused by iron. A
Fae who suffers a “mortal” wound might
pass through the Last Door. But they
might also change their mind and come
back later, for Fae are fickle things.
Iron
Iron (including steel) is offensive to Fae.
Its presence insults them. It disrupts their
magic. It burns their skin, ignores their
Armor, leaves scars that never fade. Iron
can kill a Fae, truly kill them dead.
Black iron (page 307) is even worse. Its
presence unravels their magic. Its touch
doesn’t just burn, it suppurates their flesh
(+1d6 damage, ignores armor, messy). It
leaves vicious, gaping wounds that never
heal and that eventually turn them into
Hollow Fae (page 114).
98

Magic and the Fae
Fae are Fae, neither spirit nor mortal. As a
rule, magic that specifically affects “spirits”
or “mortals” does not apply to the Fae. They
are their own thing.
With that said, magic is fuzzy and each
Fae is unique. When in doubt, consider
what feels right in this particular case. A
ploy that would work in a fairy tale should
probably work against the Fae!
Illusions and glamours
Illusions are lies. They have no substance.
An illusionary chair bears no weight; the
illusion of a sword does not cut. A skillful
Fae might create the illusion of pain and
blood when you are cut with an illusionary
sword, but the wound has no lasting effect.
Glamour is more profound. It changes a
thing and how it interacts with the world,
at least until the glamour fades. Glamour
a rock into a chair and sit comfortably.
Glamour a stick into a sword and it will
cut, make you bleed real blood. When the
glamour ends, the wound remains.
Glamours are like poetry. One might
glamour an acorn into a gem, or fluttering
leaves into butterflies, or a braying mortal’s
head into that of a donkey. But one does
not just, say, glamour a spear into a puddle
of water. These things must rhyme!
Fae can see through each other’s illusions
and glamours, but as a rule they play along.
Doing otherwise can be insulting.
Glamour affects neither iron nor steel, and
the touch of either causes illusions to flicker and fade. The touch of black iron (page
307) dispels both illusions and glamours,
like soap dropped in oily water.

Fae
Language
Fae understand anyone they meet, and can
always make themselves understood. Many
speak in an affected manner (accented, in
verse, with odd sentence construction, etc.)
to amuse themselves, to annoy others, or
because that’s just how they think.

Insults and etiquette
Fae are also bound by insults, pride, and etiquette. An insulted Fae feels the need for
satisfaction like a mortal feels hunger, as a
gnawing and physical thing. Their culture
is full of little rules and points of etiquette
that mortals often stumble over.

Debts and promises
Fae are bound by debts, obligations, and
promises. This applies to mortals who treat
with them, too. A Fae might:

One might insult a Fae by…

ä		 Make an offer (fair or lopsided)
ä		 Politely decline or dissemble
ä		 Insist, ask for more, offer less
ä		 Call in a debt, use it as leverage
ä		 Make or demand a promise
ä		 Find/exploit a loophole
ä		 Trade a debt to another Fae
When a Fae is beholden to you, you
have advantage on any roll to resist or
defend yourself against it. When you
leverage a debt that they owe you or a
promise they’ve made, gain advantage
to Persuade or otherwise influence it.
When you are beholden to a Fae, you
have disadvantage on any roll made
directly against them (including damage rolls). When you fail to uphold a
promise you’ve made to a Fae, for any
reason, either you are beholden to
that Fae or they take it as an insult
(their choice).
When you pass through the Last
Door, all debts you owe and promises
you have made are null and void.

…		 rudely refusing an offer;
…		 failing to honor a debt or promise;
…		 taking what they think they own;
…		 making demands or threats without the
proper leverage;
…		 sneaking iron into their presence;
…		 failing to play along with their games;
…		 disparaging their honor/their power/
their cunning/whatever they value
about themselves; or
…		 accusing them of any of the above.
When the PCs deal with a Fae, that Fae
might:
ä		 Set the PCs up for a faux pas
ä		 Make an accusation
ä		 Act affronted, genuinely or as a ploy
ä		 Truly take insult
And if insulted, they might:
ä		 Demand satisfaction
ä		 Storm/scamper off
ä		 Offer their forgiveness, as a gift
ä		 Ignore all other rules of etiquette
ä		 Spread tales or rumors
ä		 Hold a grudge, lay plans for revenge
ä		 Resort to violence or magic

99

101

Fae

Fae domains
Fae domains are hidden realities, existing
between the physical and spirit worlds,
touching and overlapping both but part
of neither. Some are tiny, intimate, cozy.
Others are vast and grand.
Not every place where a Fae dwells is truly
a domain. Plenty of Fae squat in ruins
or caves or hollow trees, perhaps spruced
up with glamour but fully in this world.
Domains are different, set apart.
To create a Fae domain, start with the
real-world terrain it borders or overlaps
with. Most are found in the Great Wood
(page 200), in the Foothills (page 144),
or in Ferrier’s Fen (page 116); those in the
Fen are often corrupted (page 430). Many
are part of Green Lord ruins (page 214).
Once the real-world terrain is established,
pick or roll for its entrance. Interpret the
result in light of where it’s found.
1d6

entrance

1
2
3

A shimmer, a feeling, little more
Megalith(s)/a ﻿waystone (page 478)
A door/gate/opening, natural or
made, literal or just an impression
A ring of toadstools or some other
distinctive pattern of flora (perhaps
useful or valuable, page 458)
A path/a climb/a descent/a fall
Only active at certain times (in
moonlight, at sunset, in winter, etc.)
and roll again

4
5
6

If an entrance in the Great Wood is at
least 10 years old, it’s likely to be marked
by Forest Folk glyphs (page 152).
102

For the domain itself, pick or roll for a
theme (page 95), the domain’s original
purpose, the anchor that maintains it, and
1-2 elements. Maybe roll 1d6 to inform
its size and scale.
1d6

purpose

1
2
3

Containment: to imprison/preserve
Habitat: where Fae live/work/play
Community: a place to gather,
trade, gossip, show off, travel
Status: a monument, a place to
impose, someone showing off
Creativity: an expression, an exploration, a work of art
Pleasure: a paradise, a retreat, a
false afterlife, a playground

4

mundane place made giant, etc.
“Civilized” things, out of place: a
feast hall in a mire, a house carved
from a tree, a library in a cave, etc.
A real place, plucked from the
world and the passing of time

get lost in, always something new

The PCs are exploring a Green Lord’s tomb, approaching the burial vault in which the
mummified Maker lies. Its spirit lingers on in a Fae domain.
The terrain is established (the inside of the tomb). I choose a “door/gate/opening” for the
entrance and the purpose is clearly “pleasure… a false afterlife.” I roll a 5 for theme
(“promises, deals, and loopholes…”) and a 5 for the anchor (“rune-covered stone”). For
elements, I get 6 (“decay/squalor/disorder”) and 5 (“a vast and shifting place”).
The theme and elements prompt me to rethink the anchor. The domain is barely maintained by
despondent Fae servants, bound by ancient oaths. An endless series of dusty halls, rooms and
feral gardens, full of discarded wealth and surly Fae avoiding their master. The Green Lord’s
soul swings from boredom to manic fits, during which whatever Fae is at hand conjures
threadbare glamours of the Green Lord’s half-remembered deeds. An eternal paradise, indeed.
103

Fae
Entering domains
Some entrances work only at particular
times: under a full moon, during winter, at
sunrise or sunset, etc. This holds true for
both Fae and mortal alike.
Otherwise, Fae can freely use the entrance
of most domains, like you or I might open
a door. For mortals, it’s more involved.
Something interesting: A mortal might
blunder into a Fae domain by accident, or
follow a guide, or be drawn into one by a
mischievous or malevolent Fae. Or they
might fall asleep near an entrance and visit
the domain in their dreams. Iron or steel
might protect you, or not; you might just
find that all your iron was left behind in
the world.
Something useful: To enter a Fae domain
on purpose, you must leave behind all iron
and steel, plus one of the following:
		Follow the footsteps of a Fae guide
		Addle (open?) your mind with drugs/
alcohol/madness/intense emotion/etc.
		Have Fae blood in your veins
		Be able to see through Fae magic and
glamours, via Old Mother’s Wine
(page 509) or a similar effect

Locked entrances
Some entrances have special requirements
to open them, for both Fae and mortal
alike. These requirements might involve…
…		 singing/reciting/inscribing the
proper words;
…		 doing something dangerous, without
flinching (like running at the wall);
…		 doing something daft (like spinning ‘til
you fall);
…		 paying the toll (a secret, a story, a thing
of beauty, a promise, etc.);
…		 convincing the guardians to let you in;
…		 some variation or combination of
the above; or
…		 something else entirely.
The residents of a domain might ignore
the requirements of a locked entrance; the
creator of a domain almost certainly can.
I think my Green Lord’s afterlife must
certainly be locked. I imagine a grand
archway with a pair of powerful Fae
guardians watching over it, demanding
a toll: none can enter without giving an
oath of good behavior.

Fae domain hazards
		Enchanting, entrancing beauty
		Time passing unnoticed
		Dramatic, rugged, strange landscapes
		Things changing when they look away
		Getting lost
		Capricious weather
		Elemental spirits (page 360), awake
and active and full of opinions
		Illusions and glamours (page 98)
		Not being able or allowed to leave

104

Fae

Fluid time
Time passes strangely in Fae domains,
when it passes at all. Upon returning to
the world, one might find that no time has
passed, or that time has passed normally,
or that they’ve lost days, weeks, months,
or years. Tales tell of folk returning to the
world centuries later and crumbling into
dust, but those are just tales. Right?
Lost time most often afflicts those who:
		Eat Fae food or drink Fae drink
		Fall asleep
		Lose themselves to revels, work, etc.
		Insult/become indebted to the locals
		Disrupt a place held in stasis
Threaten PCs with lost time, for sure. Use
it to explain the absence of PCs whose
players have to miss some sessions. But
don’t have the PCs lose years or decades
unless you—and your players—really want
to shake up your game.

Some Fae domains
		A little door in a rocky outcrop, leading
to a dying crystal city of tiny Fae and
their talking vole subjects.
		A natural arch of hawthorne trees.
Beyond: a tree-lined hall, a runecarved hearth, endless drink & dance.
		A knot in a large dead tree. One must
recite a certain poem to enter. Inside
is a crowded theater, where callous Fae
call up long-dead shades to play out
their mortal dramas.
		A waystone (page 478) in the mire.
A crossroad of many Fae paths, oftused but one must pay a toll of beauty
to the wretched troll who guards it.
		A wide stream. Fall face-first into it
and enter a watery, dreamlike realm.
A catfish-like Fae arranges stones,
treasures, and lost memories into a
beautiful, ever-shifting mosaic.
		A circle of menhirs, entwined in
thorns. Can only be entered in moonlight. Inside, all the stones but one are
revealed to be Forest Folk (page 150),
bound in agony.

105

Fae

The Wandering Market
Little ties the Fae together, except for a
desire to show off. And for that, they need
an audience. A place to meet and gather. A
place to trade stories and wonders and lies.
They need the Wandering Market.
Questions
		 According to tales, when does the
Wandering Market convene?
		 If you could purchase any treasure from
the Fae, one wondrous thing, what
would it be?
		 What is your single most treasured
possession? Why do you value it so?
		 Who among you is most beautiful?
Has the best singing voice? Dances the
finest? Makes the best food?
Getting there
The Wandering Market is a Fae domain
(page 102), made anew by a different host
each time it convenes.

Impressions
		Sensory overload: crowds and barkers,
silks and spices, perfumes, feathers and
flowers, leering inhuman faces
		Wonders for sale: distilled friendship,
liquid silence, crystalline sorrow, etc.
		Snide comments about how this
Market pales to previous ones
		Squeals of delight at your smile, your
wry comment, your whistled tune
		How long have you been here, again?
Chance encounters
Pick or have someone roll the Die of Fate.
1d12

A Fae in mismatched finery, selling
stolen wares; rejects all offers
One Fae savagely beating a servant;
not a single eyebrow raised
A kindly green fruit-seller implores
the PCs to “sample” his wares
A troupe’s bedazzling performance:
fireworks, illusions, bored onlookers
A crone selling fine futures, fates
altered, prophecies (re)written
A hand (paw?) on your purse, deftly
attempting to “trade”
A tiny sub-market, in a beehive or a
stump; astir with bug-sized Fae
A scandalous, sensual revel in the
street; stepped around by most
Talking beasts, glum Fae, holloweyed children, collared and chained

Fae

Moves
TREASURE HUNT
When you scour the Wandering Market for
a solution, say what problem you’re hoping
to solve and roll +CHA: on a 7+, you find
someone offering just the thing, and can
Haggle for it. But before you do, on a 10+,
you must first pick 1; on a 7-9, you must
first pick 3:
		Someone else is trying to buy it (or
pretending to); you must Haggle with
disadvantage
		It’s not actually a solution itself, but
rather a way to acquire one
		It’s cursed, stolen, or otherwise
dangerous to use or possess
		To even find it, you’ve put yourself in
some Fae’s debt
HAGGLE
When you dicker with a Fae (anywhere,
not just in the Wandering Market), roll
+WIS. Roll with advantage if they like you,
and disadvantage if they wish you ill. On
a 12+, it costs but a pittance; on a 10-11,
pick 1; on a 7-9, pick 2; on a 6-, pick 3:
		The price is Value 2, at least
		The price is irreplaceable, cherished,
heartbreaking to lose
		The price is not truly yours to pay
		It involves a promise, condition, or
obligation, sure to cause trouble
		You end up beholden to them
Whatever the price, you’ve either agreed to
it or you’ve insulted the Fae and the deal
is off. Work together to figure out exactly
what happened. Reneging on the deal is
certain to insult the Fae and/or put you in
their debt (page 99).

. trade opportunities .
If the PCs get a trade opportunity
(from the Seasons Change move), the
Fae might offer the following. The PCs
still need to Haggle for it, though!
		An impossible task, like a whole
season’s worth of work done in
a night
		A secret, a memory, an answer to
a mystery, help with a sticky
problem, etc.
		Fantastical trade goods (Value 2
or 3): cloth-of-clouds, mead
brewed from dreams, dyes of
glamour, etc.
		A Fae artifact (page 108)
		An artifact of the Green Lords
(page 216), the Forest Folk
(page 154), the spirits of the
wild (page 364), or perhaps the
Things Below (page 424).

Fae

Moonlight raiment

					magical, beautiful, fragile

A gossamer robe, a joy against your skin.
When you wear the raiment in dim light,
you become more pleasing to the eye. If
normally ugly, you become plain. If plain,
you become beautiful. If beautiful, you become luminous, breathtaking, magnificent.
Alas, when the moonlight raiment is soiled
or torn while you wear it, mark a debility.
The debility cannot be cleared until the
raiment is cleaned or repaired using Fae
magic, or you burn it away into silvery ash.

pleasing to the spirits of plants, trees,

reflects how you see yourself
		A necklace of broken promises (fragile,

and infinite depth; fills the heart with
cathartic, yearning sorrow (fragile,
beautiful, Value 3)
		A bronze ring with an emerald the
size of an acorn; its wearer ages not,
but the years catch up all at once if
removed (beautiful, magical, Value 4)

A pot of “gold”
, magical
					
A small bronze cauldron, full of glittering
coins. At least, that’s what it seems.
When you fill the pot with small, natural
items (leaves, stones, acorns, etc.) and hide
it from light for a season, the contents are
glamoured to become a purse of gold
(Value 4).
Any “coins” removed from the pot revert to
their true form when…
…		 an honest merchant touches them;
…		 their current owner tells a lie;
…		 someone sees through the glamour;
…		 something dispels the glamour; or
…		 the new moon comes and goes

The Red Pennant
, magical, Blood l l l
					
A woolen flag hanging from a crossbar at
the end of a sturdy long spear. It’s tattered,
crimson, with a crudely embroidered boot
for a device. It hangs heavy. Like it’s wet.

If spending Blood results in you Ordering
Followers or Deploying, or another PC
Clashing, the roll has advantage. Each
time you spend Blood, those affected gain
some small, animalistic or stony trait (little
horns, weird eyes, crag-like wrinkles, etc.).

When you fly the Red Pennant in battle,
all who fight on your side feel bloodlust
boil within them. Hold 3 Blood. While the
battle rages, you can spend Blood 1-for-1
to will your allies to:

When you spend the last Blood or the battle
ends, the Red Pennant’s color fades and
a few (~1 in 6) of those manifested traits
will linger, especially in those who fought
hardest. It will not function again until you
soak it in the blood of fallen foes.

		Rush forward, driving back their foes
		Hold fast, making their foes pay for
every inch
		Fight savagely, giving no quarter

This is the battle standard of the Bloody
Boots (page 112), their most sacred relic.
Don’t expect them to just let it go.
A sturdy, banded box
					 , magical
6” by 4” by 3”, made of thick rowan wood
and banded with bronze. It’s not much to
look at. It slips easily from one’s mind.
When you let a drop of your blood fall into
the empty chest, name a promise you have
made, an oath you have given, or a debt
that you owe. Your blood becomes a bead
of lead as it falls into the chest, and the
promise, oath, or debt holds no power over
you as long as the chest is sealed.

Auntie Sallow
					Solitary, large, fae, magical, stealthy,
corrupted, terrifying
HP 16; Armor 4 (resilience), 1 vs. iron (bark)
Damage choking fingers/tendrils d10+1
(hand, close, reach, grabby)
Special qualities Fae nature (page 98);
takes +1d4 damage from bronze;
leaches color from surroundings; knows
whatever her moths know
Instinct to “protect” beauty and innocence
ä Disperse into an eclipse of moths
ä Find someone needing her “help”
ä Show them their fears/nightmares
ä Lure/drag them back to her home
ä Bury them in the cool safe mud,
forever
She appears out of the shadows, when the
fear rises up to take you. Tall and grim and
looming, draped in willow branches, moths
flitting all about. She reaches out a bony
hand. “Hush dear,” she whispers. “Hush.
Auntie Sallow is here to help.”
She was beautiful, once. The minder of a
sacred grove. A place of clean water, butterflies, and bees. But then came war. Fire
and poison and the sundering of earth.
Schemes and lies. Grief and endless pain.
’Twas in grief that L’bin’bozia (page 421)
found her. Gave her the strength to help.
To find the innocent who suffered, the
beautiful in despair. To offer succor, and
the safety of her master’s cool embrace.

Fae

The Bear of Winter
					Solitary, huge, fae, magical, hoarder,
devious, fierce, terrifying
HP 26; Armor 5 (resilience, ice), 2 vs. iron
(hide, ice)
Damage bite or maul d12+5 (close, hand,
reach, forceful, grabby, messy, 1 piercing)
Special qualities Fae nature (page 98);
immune to cold; aura of wintry weather, matching his mood
Instinct to protect his status and holdings
ä Weave ice and snow, glamours and
illusions, entire Fae domains
ä Send a minion (spirit, beast, or
indebted Fae) to do his bidding
ä Wield the wintry elements
ä Take the form of a scarred old man
or a storm of sleet and snow
ä Make use of a forgotten artifact
When winter snows blanket the Great
Wood, the Bear roams the land. He’s most
often met as a great and snuffling beast, big
as a house, icicles clinging to his fur. His
retinue follows: nervously, discretely, waiting upon his call. He visits the many Fae
who owe him favors and those who have
pledged him their support. None are happy
to see him. None dare turn him away.

The Four Chieftains
The chieftains are among the most
powerful of the Fae, personally and
politically. The Bear of Winter is one
of them. The others might include:
		The Warbler, who dwells in the
springtime canopy and fills the
world with song. An incorrigible
gossip, who pretends to be a fool.
Often goes incognito. Extremely
well-informed.
		The Crowned Stag, who troops
through deep summer woods
with their court and hangers-on.
Beautiful, proud, dashing, and
noble. Lusty, too, and prone to
fall in love.
		Longshadow, a stately old
tree-wife, bare of leaves. Found
brooding in the autumn forest, attended by skittish minions. Speaks
prophecy, lays subtle plans. Knows
that the war isn’t over.

The Bear, you see, is among the most powerful of the Fae. Snapped his own master’s
neck. Gobbled down its corpse. He freed
hundreds of Fae from servitude. He leveraged the debts they owed him to become
something of a feudal lord.
The Bear’s domain is a great cave, full of
hoarded riches and slinking servants. It has
many entrances and exits, each leading to a
different cave mouth in the world, but only
when that cave mouth is surrounded by
drifts of snow.

Fae

Blush-of-Dawn
					Solitary, fae, magical, beautiful, passionate
HP 12; Armor 4 (resilience), 0 vs. iron
Damage fiery touch d8 w/disadvantage
(hand)
Special qualities Fae nature (page 98);
radiant
Instinct to be adored
ä Sing a song to stir the blood
ä Inspire passion/jealousy/devotion
ä Steal away into their Fae domain,
perhaps with a new prize in tow
ä Set devotees to a task/challenge
ä Blaze brightly, blind onlookers
They were the shining jewel in some Green
Lord’s paradise, the bright beauty of a
rising sun made flesh. They were happy
and in love. (Or so they thought, for can a
being bred to love truly do so?)
Then their master’s tomb was plundered by
the rebels. Their world was upended. They
were bereft, vulnerable, alone and unloved.
They had no interest in freedom, only the
safe warm surety of other beings’ full regard. Fortunately, for them, such adoration
was easy to find.
Their domain is a stately pavilion in a
summer glade, forever in that moment
just before the dawn. It’s filled with
fawning victims of their charms: lovers
and would-be lovers, abducted children,
devotees. And on the outskirts: the castout wretches, those who’ve lost favor but
whose jealous adoration still feeds the
Blush-of-Dawn.

112

The Bloody Boots
					Group, small, fae, ugly, violent
HP 12; Armor 4 (resilience), 2 vs. iron
Damage oversized club d8 w/advantage
(close, crude) or iron-shod boots d8+2
w/advantage (hand, forceful)
Special qualities Fae nature (page 98);
tolerates iron; can’t start violence
Instinct to brawl, the nastier the better
ä Insult, mock, heap abuse
ä Get in your face, dare you
ä Leverage their unlikely strength
ä Stomp someone who’s down (messy)
A gang of hoodlums, with names like Hobnail, Spitkicker, Big Thunk, Tusk, and Grub.
Short, squat, brutes with lopsided limbs,
mismatched parts, and craggy skin. They
stomp about in oversized, iron-shod boots,
delighting in the disgust of other Fae.
Back in the day, they quarried stone for
their masters. Didn’t even mind it much.
But given the chance to fight and brawl
and spill all the blood they could, well, no
need to ask them twice. The Bloody Boots
were among the most feared and storied
troops of the war. First into the fray and
dyeing their Red Pennant (page 109) in
the blood of trodden foes.
And after the war? Well, you can’t have
thugs like them just rampaging about.
Some little schemer bought and traded for
every debt they ever had, then extracted a
promise from each: no starting fights. They
can finish a fight that someone else starts,
and, oh, will they enjoy it. But just about
every Fae still living knows better than to
give them that chance.

Grimstew
					Solitary, small, fae, magical, tough,
stealthy, cautious, ugly
HP 16; Armor 4 (resilience), 1 vs. iron
(thick fur)
Damage crushing grip d10+2 (hand,
grabby, forceful)
Special qualities Fae nature (page 98)
Instinct to fill the emptiness inside
ä Stalk a mortal, curious and lonely
ä Offer aid, in exchange for company
ä Perform an impossible labor
ä Grow when angered: +large, +4 HP,
+3 damage, +close, +reach
ä Get hungry, eat a companion

He dwells in a cave full of smoke and
bones, tending his stew pot and stewing.
He might venture out to fetch firewood,
water, or meat. And when he’s out, he
might cross paths with a mortal. In his
loneliness, he might forget just how poorly
things went last time.
When you Haggle with Grimstew (page
107), you can’t pick either of the first two
options. He wants company, not things.

Fae

Hollow Fae
					Solitary, fae, undead, confused,
terrifying, see below
HP 19; Armor 5 (resilience, lacks vitals),
1 vs. black iron
Damage life-drain d10 (hand, close, area,
ignores armor)
Special qualities senses life; surrounded by
inert death and ash; can be truly
destroyed only by black iron
Instinct to consume life-force
ä Moan, filling all with dread
ä Tear apart Fae magic
ä Pause in confusion when confronted
with who it once was
ä Open wide, drink in the vitality of
the living (inflicting debilities)
A Fae wounded by black iron (page 307)
will pray for death. For to survive such a
wound is a curse. They grow weak, then
confused, then hungry. Soon, they become
a walking husk, surrounded by death,
inhaling any source of life they find.
Hollow Fae are no longer vulnerable to
mere iron or steel. Debts and promises
no longer bind them. They will tear their
way free of any Fae domain. Only black
iron can destroy them, and what living Fae
would be fool enough to try?
When faced with such a terror, most Fae
choose to flee. A few might be so clever
as to trap it in some forgotten tomb. But
the cleverest, oh, the cleverest… they find
some fool mortal to handle the problem
for them.
A large Hollow Fae is tougher than shown
above: 23 HP and +1 damage, +reach. A
huge one is tougher still, with 27 HP, +3
damage, and +reach.

Fae

The White Billies
					Horde, fae, athletic, raucous
HP 7; Armor 4 (resilience), 0 vs. iron
Damage headbutt d6+2 (hand, forceful)
Special qualities Fae nature (page 98)
Instinct to suppress their grief
ä Drag someone into their revels
ä Get huffy if refused
ä Lash out if forced to face the past
Muscular folk with cloven hooves, birchwhite fur, and horns in every size and shape.
They seem so happy, their endless days
spent dancing and singing, drinking and
rutting, indulging in good-natured brawls.
But when you look closer, you see the scars.
You see the missing limbs, the broken
horns. You see, briefly, the eyes so full of
pain. But, no, no, it’s gone. Another dance!
Another round! Another bawdy song!

Tiny little things, winged and fluttering,
the size of mice or dragonflies. Weird little
things, too: mishmashes of fur and feather,
scales and chitin, gossamer and wings.
Beautiful, cute, or grotesque.
Some lived together under the Green
Lords’ rule, others came together after the
war. A surprising number were born into
freedom, unburdened by their elders’ scars.
You might find them keeping watch over
some corrupted site or crumbling Green
Lord ruin. Or you might come across one
of their teeming cities, hidden in a burrow
or a hollowed-out ancient tree. Or, if you’re
unlucky, you might encounter a gang of
them out roaming the woods or fen, bored
and cruel and looking for sport, and you’d
be just the thing.

When you join the White Billies in their
revels, lose 1d6 HP, mark a debility, and
roll +CON: on a 10+, you keep your wits
about you and learn something useful; on
a 7-9, you regain your wits later, drunk and
exhausted (mark another debility), with
some new excess about to start; on a 6-,
mark another debility and pick 1:
		You’ve upset them; real violence
is imminent
		You come to later, alone, and who
knows how long it’s been
		You’re enthralled, reveling endlessly
until someone rescues you

115

Ferrier’s Fen
Ferrier’s Fen lies between the Great Wood
(page 200), the Steplands (page 372),
and the North Manmarch (page 282).
It’s a huge wetland, 100 miles wide, 50
miles north to south, fed by the Stream
(page 396) the Sruth near Marshedge
(page 274), and many smaller creeks. It’s a
maze of firm ground, stagnant pools, shallow streams, and plunging drops into deep
deep water. And mud. So much mud.
Long ago, this area was the cradle of the
Green Lords’ civilization (page 210). But
when their servants rebelled and the Green
Lords embraced the Things Below (page
412), the other Makers (page 264) collapsed the region and shattered the Green
Lords’ civilization. It’s still a broken place,
oozing with old magic and corruption.

Impressions
Always
		Willow trees leaning over water
		Chunks of stone ruins, jutting from
			the mud
		Wobble and shake of peat bogs
			underfoot
		Sulphur smell of stagnant water and rot
		Some mundane thing but shockingly
wrong: a tree with weeping eyes, a
three-faced rabbit, a snake with stumpy
legs, a deer with a fawn growing from
its side, etc.
Spring
		Dead brown mixed with pale green
			 mixed with bright-colored flowers
		Squelch of mud, the shocking wet			 cold, its jealous grip on your feet
		Boisterous, rhythmic calls of frogs
			and toads
		Thick fog, muffling everything,
			shrinking the world
116

Summer
		Flies and midges, buzzing at your ears,
			nose, eyes, mouth
		Press and rustle and scrape of grass,
			sedge, and rush
		Butterflies and moths in every hue and
			 shape; fireflies like the stars at night
		Baking heat, cloying air, sweat rolling
			off of you
Autumn
		Yellow, brown, and gold of dying plants
			 below; dead slate of skies above
		That smell of rot, just overwhelming now
		Flocks of water fowl, their harsh
			 honking, flying in great Vs overhead
Winter
		Dead grass poking through a blanket
			of snow
		A quiet calm
		Gurgle and trickle of unseen water
		Crack and lurch as you put your weight
			on thin ice

Exploring the Fen
Ferrier’s Fen holds much of value, but it’s a
shifting, treacherous place.
Chart a Course
On any expedition into Ferrier’s Fen:
		 Choose 1:
		You need to bring a pouch of
bendis root (Value 1 in Marshedge)
per day, burnt constantly
		The way will be perilous, filled
with dangers
		 You need a knowledgeable guide, like a
fen-walker (next column)
		 You risk getting lost nonetheless
		 The terrain itself is treacherous; you
risk injury along the way
		 The way will be grueling; you risk
exhausting yourselves/your resources
		 You risk contracting fen blight
(page 123)
		 You risk drawing the attention of the
suarachan (except in winter)

Fen-walker
					Fen-wise, cautious, stealthy, observant
(and see below)
HP 8; Armor 1 (leather cuirass)
Damage knife d8 (hand), sling d6
			(near, reload)

			ä Spot/avoid a hidden hazard
