Ferrier’s Fen

The Willow Witches
Many tales are told of the weird sisters of
the Fen. There are always three, but they
might be old, young, or middling aged.
They might be tall and regal or twisted and
gnarled, dignified and poised or feral things
rooting through the mud. Sometimes, the
tale’s hero seeks them out. Other times, they
stumble upon the Witch’s abode.
All stories agree that the Willow Witches
have what you seek. An answer. An artifact
(or knowledge of where to find it). A way
to accomplish some impossible deed. Everyone knows that Sianna Ferrier learned
the secrets of the Fen from the Witches,
allowing Marshedge to be settled.
What the tales often fail to mention is:
there’s always a price. For Sianna Ferrier, the
Witches’ gifts were costly indeed: a child of
her blood, or the blood of those she settled
with, to be delivered every seven years.
The witches are, in truth, a single entity,
the inscrutable incarnation (page 300) of
a primordial being of knowledge, fate, costs
and consequences, greatly diminished and
dwelling in this broken land.
The Witches’ abode
The Willow Witches’ abode is rarely found
in the same place twice, and in every tale,
the details are different. Sometimes they
dwell in the hollow of a willow tree, the
space inside impossibly big. In other tales,
it’s a crude shack leaning against a tree. In
still others, they huddle around a cookfire in
a willow’s shade, no real shelter to speak of.
The surest way to find the Witches is to
seek out a fen-troll (page 123) and convince them to guide you. They all know the
way, though one should expect mischief.
120

Mind your manners
The Willow Witches observe the old ways
of hospitality. Any who approach peaceably
and invoke the guest-right are welcome to
eat their food and shelter under their roof
(such that it is). The Witches will do no
true harm unless the guest first violates the
rules of hospitality by…
…		 initiating violence;
… threatening the Witches or their guests;
… stealing; or
… insulting the Witches’ hospitality.
The Witches delight in provoking guests:
speaking in riddles, asking about shameful secrets, offering disgusting victuals or
shabby accommodations, showing glimpses
of horrors that are gone when one looks
again. The humble, patient, and wise do
well. Hot-headed, prideful fools do not.
The price
Whatever one asks of the Willow Witches,
they require something of similar value.
Tailor the price to the character. It’s not
about what the Willow Witches want or
what they’ll do with it. It’s about sacrifice.
It’s about what the seeker is asking for, and
what it’s worth to them.
The Witches might ask for…
…		 a promise, to be fulfilled now or later.
Renege, and calamity follows. “The
axiom of local contagion? Such fancy
words for so simple a thing. We can
teach you, yes, but you must swear to
never again open a door without its
owner’s permission.”

Ferrier’s Fen
…		 a possession, worth at least as much
to the asker as that which they ask for.
“You seek a weapon against the demon?
Ah, yes, the Twisted Spear! We can show
you where it lies, take you to it. But what
will you give us in return? That mantle
you wear, the one with teeth and the
hungry ghosts? Give us that.”
…		 something strange, fraught with potential consequence. “You seek to
appease the anger of the forest? Tsk tsk,
its anger cannot be quelled. But you
can throw off the scent. Merely chew
off your own shadow and let us dispose
of it. Hmm? No, of course you can,
here, let us show you…”
…		 a personal sacrifice, bloody and
visceral. “You wish to know what the
old runes say? Surely your right eye is
a bargain for such wisdom. Or the left.
We are not picky.”
…		 a personal sacrifice, something
intangible. Work with the player to
change their instinct, swap their stats,
or lose one of their moves as a result.
“You seek a way to defeat the King of
the North? We can tell you, yes. But it
will cost the memories of your father,
and the kindness he showed you.”
…		 a sacrifice, but of something not
truly theirs to give. “You want your
eye for beauty back? Hm. You choose
the price: your tall friend’s kind heart,
which you treasure so. Or your parents’
love. It’s beauty you seek, and like must
pay for like, after all.”

If the Witches like someone—if they’ve
been humble, well-mannered, and wise—
then they’ll be clear and upfront about the
costs and what they’re offering, and maybe
suggest another course of action. Otherwise, the Witches will be cagey about the
costs and all too willing to let them have
something for cheap if they know that it
will lead to ruin.
Willow Witch
				 Group, primordial, cautious, devious,
			organized, magical, all-wise
HP 16; Armor 4 (wards, resilience), 0 vs.
black iron
Damage gnarled fists and fingers d10+2 w/
			disadvantage (hand, forceful, grabby)
Special Qualities impossible to deceive
or surprise; divide damage taken equally
between the three witches (round down)
Instinct to enforce the old ways, the old rules
			ä Frustrate, annoy, or provoke someone
			ä Twist someone’s perceptions
			ä Vanish, be elsewhere
			ä Turn someone into a swamp creature
Tip: use a different voice and different
physical position (left, center, right) for
each witch, and shift between them as you
talk, sometimes mid-sentence.
When you press or entice the Willow Witches into changing their price or altering their
deal, don’t roll. They will not be budged;
the price is what it is.

Whatever the PCs ask for, it should be a
meaningful decision whether they pay the
price. Don’t ask for something you know
they’ll never give up, and don’t ask for
something they won’t miss anyhow.

Ferrier’s Fen

Dangers
Treacherous terrain
		Quaking bog: wobbly footing, some			 times thin enough to fall through
		Deep, sucking mud
		Slippery footing
		Thin ice cracking open
		Fallen logs or furrows hidden by grass
			or mud
		Thorns, spines, brambles
		Venomous snakes, underfoot or hiding
			in hollows
		Poisonous plants that cause rashes, blisters
		Fog and mist, or blanketing snow,
			 obscuring all of the above and worse
Swarms
		Midges or mosquitos—annoying,
			distracting, painful, diseased
		Leeches—clinging to anything that
			gets in water
		Starlings—obscuring, drowning out
sound, buffeting, possibly possessed by
a grwgnach (page 370)

Disease
					 Sickness can come from bad water,
bad air, tainted food, and blood-sucking
vermin. It’s especially risky to have open
wounds—in these conditions, it’s all too
easy for them to go rotten.
ä		 Show that they’ve been exposed/infected
ä		 Hit them with minor symptoms
			(mark miserable)
ä		 Have symptoms worsen (mark another
			debility)
ä		 Deny them rest
The best treatment for disease is rest, clean
food, water, and air, and treatment with
the proper herbs. Someone who pushes on
through serious symptoms is likely Defying Danger (the danger that being that
they get worse, or suffer complications).
To name a disease, or describe its symptoms,
mix and match 2-3 of the following:
ague, angry, black, blisters, blood, blue,
boils, breath, burning, chills, cold, cough,
cramps, crows, dry, eye(s), festering,
fever, fingers, fluttering, flux, foot,
itching, lung, midge, milky, mouth,
muddy, oozing, phlegm, pickled, pink,
pox, rash, retching, rot, running, runs,
scalp, shakes, shivers, skin, snot, sores,
spit, spots, sweats, tears, tick, toad, toes,
tongue, tremor, trots, warts, weed, weeping, welts, wet, wheezing, wound, yellow
Consider rolling the Die of Fate to see
how serious the symptoms are and how
hard the disease is to treat.
Something interesting: You know what
this disease is called, its symptoms and typical
course, maybe even what causes it. Something
useful: You know how best to treat it, what
herbs to use and where they might be found.

Ferrier’s Fen

Fen blight
					Threat (affliction)
Instinct to strip away its victim’s humanity
Those who spend significant time in Ferrier’s Fen might find themselves… changed.
Marshedgers tell many tales about what
causes the blight, and what will protect from
it, but even the most careful fen-walker will
sometimes start to feel the changes.
l		 Little things, easy enough to hide,

like warty skin, thinning hair, fetid
odor, a lengthening tongue, a taste for
bugs and snails
l		 More pronounced changes, growing
appetite, jealousy, a fear of being
discovered
l		 Growth spurts, increased strength,
more obvious mutations, resentment
towards the unafflicted
l		 Difficulty with language, unending
hunger, paranoia, urge to hide from sight
l		 Impending doom: Transformation
into a fen-troll (right)

Monsters

200), often mutated
		Fae (page 94), especially those who
			 delight in ugliness and misfortune

Things Below (page 432)

		A rotting pyped (page 82), living on

Fen-troll
					Solitary, large, corrupted, hoarder,
gluttonous, stealthy
HP 16; Armor 1 (thick hide)
Damage bone-snapping grip d10+3 (hand,
close, grabby, forceful), rending maw
d10+3 (hand, messy, 1 piercing)
Special qualities sharp senses, horrid odor,
ashamed of its visage
Instinct to fill its belly and jealously guard
			its hoard			
ä Approach a victim from cover of
		dark, or fog, or murky water
			ä Demand treasure to let them pass
					unharmed
			ä Probably try to eat them anyway
They were human once, corrupted by the
Fen and the foul forces flowing through it.
Little remains of that humanity, though:
a rough shape, half-forgotten words, a
terrible anger at what it has lost.
They prize finery and mundane goods,
anything that reminds them of their former life. Even more, they long for human
connection. But hunger, shame, and greed
usually lead them to lash out at any who
show them kindness.

(area, close)

Snaplilies

					
Damage snapping “jaws” 1d4 (hand, grabby)
Instinct to latch on and drag down
ä Blend in with a field of lily pads
ä Snap shut on a frog or bird,
		 disappearing before anyone sees
ä Snap tight on a limb, yank down
ä SNAP SNAP SNAP on someone
who trips and falls among them: 1d8
damage w/advantage (grabby, messy)
They blend right in, really. But when anything bigger’n a bug lands on one, SNAP,
just like a bear trap, the vine contracting
and pulling prey underwater. It’s a lethal
trap for a small creature. For a human?
Just a painful mistake. Unless you panic, of
course, or flail, or trip. So just, you know,
stay calm.
124

Suarachan hunters
					Group, stealthy, cautious, organized,
			amphibious
HP 6; Armor 1 (scaly hide)
Damage cudgel d8 (close, crude), snapping
tongue d6 (reach), poison blowgun
needles d6 (near)
Special qualities excellent night vision,
salt burns their skin
Instinct to bring home captives for sacrifice
ä Surround their quarry unnoticed
ä Leap a shocking distance
ä Club someone senseless on the head
ä Tie a fast knot
They’re tall as a short man, hunched on
too-long legs and too-long arms. A toadlike head juts forward from broad shoulders, the eyes entirely too big, the mouth
entirely too wide. They’re covered in moist
scales and horn-like growths, and draped
in belts and bandoliers of woven grass.
These are the suarachan most often encountered, the ones that raid Marshedge
and the ones that fen-walkers most fear.
Truly, they spend more time fishing and
hunting than seeking sacrifices, and they’re
just as happy to claim a deer as a man. But
deer are harder to catch, on the whole.

Ferrier’s Fen

Suarachan singers
					Horde, amphibious
HP 3; Armor 0
Damage fists and bite d6 w/ disadvantage
(hand), snapping tongue d4 w/ disadvantage (reach)
Special qualities excellent night vision,
salt burns their skin, mutations
Instinct to sing the eternal lullaby
ä Drown out all other noises with
		their song
ä Raise a shrill call of alarm
ä Scuttle away and hide
ä Keep a prisoner living, long enough
There are places in Ferrier’s Fen where horrors sleep, where the Things Below dream
unquiet. The air in these places thrums
with frog-song, so deep and so loud you
can barely hear yourself think. But it’s not
frogs singing. It’s the suarachan.
The suarachan believe that their songs keep
the Things Below quiet. And they hope
that their sacrifices—given in bulk in late
autumn—will keep their dark gods satiated
while the suarachan sleep through winter.
Most suarachan bear mutations: extra legs
(often twisted and useless), overgrown
or atrophied limbs, missing or extra eyes,
strange growths, etc. The most mobile
become hunters, but the chorus welcomes
any suarachan that can sing.

Suarachan soothsayer
					Solitary, corrupted, cunning, magical,
amphibious
HP 12; Armor 0
Damage poisonous skin d10 (hand),
snapping tongue d4 (reach)
Special qualities excellent night vision,
salt burns their skin, marked by the
Things Below (page 432)
Instinct to mollify the Things Below
ä Secrete various poisons: debilitating,
		hallucinogenic, deadly
ä Commune with outsiders via
		poison-induced visions
ä Use a gift of the Things Below
(page 432)
A few suarachan—one or two per chorus—have bright, colorful scales that ooze
with slime. They will often ingest their own
hallucinatory toxins, communing with the
horrors that lay sleeping, shushing them
and soothing them, and promising them
meals of screaming flesh, soon, soon.
The suarachan lack language, per se. They
can communicate basic concepts via their
songs, but true communication requires a
soothsayer’s touch.
When you are affected by the hallucinogenic poison of a suarachan soothsayer, you
can perceive spirits around you and speak
to those who are active, as well as others
affected by the poison. When the poison
wears off, mark a debility (dazed if it’s
available).

The Flats
Stonetop sits on the easternmost edge of
the Flats, a rolling tallgrass prairie that
stretches all the way to the Huffel Peaks
(page 236), the Foothills (page 144) and
the Steplands (page 372).

The prairie is dotted with small hills, outcrops, and the occasional copse of trees huddled against the ceaseless wind. Winding
streams cut through the soil. But mostly?
Just mile after mile after mile of grass.

Impressions
Always
		A southerly wind, ever present
		 Mountains looming on the far horizon
		 A sky so big it beggars the soul
		 Birds of prey, or carrion eaters, floating
high above
		 Clouds, always in motion: high and
wispy, or big and fluffy, or massive and
dark like an anvil ready to drop
Spring
		Patchworks of hopeful green, pale
white, dry yellow
		 Carpet of last year’s grass, folded flat by
wind and heavy snow
		 Soft crunch and pop underfoot
		 A damp chill in the wind, smell of rain
and wet soil
		 Chirp chirp chirp, warble
Summer
		Green and yellow stems topped with a
riot of flowers, rippling and bobbing in
the wind
		 The itch and scratch of nettles, thistle,
and stiff sandpaper stalks
		 Constant noise: birdsong, insect-buzz,
rustling grass
		 Relentless sun, unbroken by shade
		 A wall of rain, rolling
towards you

126

Autumn
		Crinkle, snap, and rustle with every
step, every movement
		 Stiff stalks, gold in the low sun,
nodding in the breeze
		 Lonely chitter of birds, unseen
		 Morning frost, or thick blankets of fog
Winter
		Snow billowing in the wind,
obscuring everything
		 A white expanse, still as death, poked
through with grassy stubble
		 The cold, wet, exhausting trudge
through waist-high drifts
		 The squeak of snow underfoot on a
bitterly cold day

The Flats

The Gwead
Villagers call the Flats-grasses the Gwead
(“the Weave” or “the Weaving”). It’s a
bewildering array of species. Some are
short and wispy, others thick and taller
than a tall man. They bloom in a myriad of
shapes and colors, and sport every manner
of prickle, thorn, and seedpod.
The Gwead will not be tamed. Burn the
grasses, plow the soil, tear things up by
their roots—it doesn’t matter. The Gwead
always come back, thicker and hardier
than before. And sometimes, it seems, the
grasslands take their revenge.

		 When was the last time anyone tried to
plant crops beyond the Old Wall?
		 What uncanny fate befell them?
Why can crops be managed within the Old
Wall (page 12), but not beyond? That’s
for you to decide. Maybe…
…		 magic tied to the Stone (page 12)
makes the land more pliable, easier
to tame;
…		 something buried beneath the Old
Wall keeps the Gwead at bay;
…		 something about the soil keeps the
Gwead from taking root; or
…		 whoever built the ruins on which
Stonetop was founded, they made a
pact with the spirits of the Gwead.

Questions
		 Who among you has ventured deep
into the Flats? What did you see there
that no one quite believes?
		 Aside from sticking to the roads, what
keeps the pack drakes at bay?
		 Which of you has seen a prairie fire up
close and personal?
		 What flower, root, seed, or stem that
can be found on the Flats is particularly useful? How so? Why is it so hard to
harvest?

Places
		The Makers’ Roads (page 270)
		The Ruined Tower (page 332)
		Titan Bones (page 448)

Pieces of the Ruined Tower
Scattered far and wide, for miles and miles

The Flats

Dangers
Hazards
		Nasty turn in the weather: lashing
wind, bitter cold, blistering heat, sleet/
hail/rain/snow; lightning
		 Lack of shelter, water, or wood for a
long-burning fire (try dried aurochs
dung, if you can find it)
		 Hidden holes/burrows, twisting an
ankle or worse
		 Getting lost (lack of landmarks, tall
grass and rolling terrain, hard to keep
to a straight line)
Prairie fire
					 The Gwead is prone to burning,
sparked by lightning, an errant flame, or
(if you believe the tales) Hillfolk intentionally setting the Flats ablaze. Early
spring, late summer, and early autumn are
particularly prone, especially after a few
weeks without rain.

130

Instinct to run unchecked across the land
ä Start with just a few crackling
		 flames, a column of smoke
ä Grow implacably, creeping outward
ä Smother the landscape in choking
		smoke (debilities)
ä Hit them with a wave of heat (d4
		damage)
ä Linger and burn for minutes or
		 hours (d6 damage, messy)
ä Tear a crimson line, faster than you
		 can sprint, bonfire-hot (d10 damage,
		 ignores armor, messy)
ä Run out of fuel, or get smothered
		by rain
Something interesting: To really spread
and burn hot, a prairie fire needs years of
built-up fuel, dry conditions, and steady
wind; even then, it can only burn at its
hottest for a short period of time.
Something useful: Seek the Makers’
Roads, or barren ground, or low-lying
patches that still hold some moisture. Failing that, seek patches of grass that burned
in the past year. Worst case: seek a narrow
point in the fire-head and run through it,
into the ash-fields behind.
Also: pray.

Aurochs
					Group, large, ornery, powerful
HP 14, Armor 2 (thick hide, tough)
Damage gore d8+5 (close, forceful, messy,
1 piercing), kick/trample d8+3 (hand,
forceful)
Instinct to spook or to get angry, in
			equal measure
			ä Send an attacker flying
			ä Stampede (area, forceful, messy)
			ä Wheel suddenly, with surprising
grace and speed
			ä Keep going past when it should be
dead or dying
Big, powerful, wild cattle with horns longer
than your arm and thicker than your hand
can grip around. Even the does have big
horns, and aren’t shy about using them.

They winter in the Steplands, in small
bands of 3-5 adults. In spring, though,
they come down to the Flats, form up into
herds of 30 or more, and graze and graze
and graze. They’ll break up into smaller
bands by midsummer, then head back into
the hills before first snow.
The Hillfolk hunt them, riding at the
flanks of a herd and picking off one or
two with arrows, keeping their damned
distance. You do not want to get too close
to one of these things, especially not after
you wound it.
A full-grown aurochs carcass (immobile),
properly butchered and transported back
to town, counts as 1 Surplus. A
pair of
aurochs horns is Value 1.
Bandit
				 Group, stealthy, cautious, devious
HP 6; Armor up to 2 (hides, shields)
Damage spears d8 (close, thrown) or
			slings d6 (near)
Instinct to survive at the expense of others
			ä Prepare a deception, to lure travelers
from the roads
			ä Spring an ambush when their prey
takes the bait
			ä Take captives or slit throats, so that
no one escapes to tell tales
			ä Flee into the grass if things go south
The magic of the Roads and the open terrain make a life of banditry far from easy,
but somehow, they crop up every few years.
Might be Hillfolk, disgraced or desperate,
but more often than not, they’re scoundrels
who’d rather bleed their fellows than scrape
by up in Gordin’s Delve.

Bandit chief

they’ve got

Most bandit gangs struggle to find victims
or shelter or both, and quickly die out or
disband. The clever ones last a few seasons,
but eventually folk catch wise and grow
wary, and the bandits’ schemes start to fail.
But every generation or so, a leader crops
up who’s cagey enough to turn a gang of
bandits into a serious threat.
What does the chief possess that makes
them such a threat? (pick 2 or 3)

Beznpol
Solitary, cautious, fierce
HP 12; Armor 3
Damage burrowing talons d10 (hand),
needle teeth d8 (hand, messy, 3 piercing)
Special Qualities acid-proof
Instinct to constantly search for food
ä Dig up serious quantities of soil
ä Bound out of the grass and onto prey
ä Spray a stupefying musk (reach, area,
		debilities)
			ä Curl up into a ball of hard, jagged
scales (4 armor)
Great big armored mustelids, like a cross
between a stoat, a badger, and a pangolin,
but like 6 feet long not counting the tail.
They roam the Flats digging up burrows
of just about anything, but they especially
love to stir up a hlomren (page 134) nest
and chomp chomp chomp. They’re far
from the top of the food chain, though. If
startled, they’ll spray musk and attempt to
flee. If cornered, they’ll curl up and hope
the attacker grows bored.
A skilled tanner can turn a pair of hides
into a magnificent
beznpol scale coat
(2 armor, warm, protects against acid,
Value 3). Hillfolk champions are known to
wear them.

The Flats

Frythanc
					Solitary, large, stealthy, terrifying
HP 16; Armor 0
Damage swooping talons d10+2 (hand,
close, forceful, grabby), beak d10 (reach,
disadvantage)
Special Qualities incredible eyesight,
terrible hearing
Instinct to wait for the right moment
			ä Soar on thermals, thousands of feet up
			ä Swoop down like a thunderbolt,
					snatching up prey
			ä Drag prey up to 30 or 40 feet with
					slow, mighty flaps—then drop them

Great scaly birds, wings like 40 feet across,
sky blue and white feathers on their
undersides to hide in the sky. They’re said
to roost in the Huffel Peaks but hunt the
Flats, snatching up the unwary—man,
woman, deer, horse—and dropping them
to the ground. The drop usually kills or
cripples its prey, and then it alights to dine
at its leisure.
They are, thank Tor, very rare, with only
one mated pair hunting the Flats at a time.
On the other side of the Huffel Peaks,
though… who knows?

The Flats

Hlomren
Horde, tiny, organized
HP 1; Armor 3 (carapace, size)
Damage acid-dripping mandibles d6-2
(hand, messy, 3 piercing), acid glob d6-2
(reach, messy, reload, 1 piercing)
Instinct to protect the colony
ä Burrow into/out of/through loose soil
ä Swarm over a threat
ä Burst with acid when killed, d4
		damage (close, area)
These red-shelled insects look like a cross
between an ant and a beetle, but the size of a
small cat. They form colonies a few-hundred
large, typically on higher ground. A colony
will harvest all the dead grass in a few miles,
hibernate through winter, and then relocate
in spring to a part of the Flats that hasn’t
burned or been harvested in a few years. They
mostly ignore people and other large animals,
but they’ll swarm if threatened.
Klezln
Solitary, large, stealthy
HP 16; Armor 3 (carapace)
Damage pincers d10+3 (hand, close, grabby)
Special Qualities senses vibrations,
			strong burrower

					(grabby)
ä Burst forth from the loose soil to
		 snag a victim
ä Drag it beneath the soil, to be
		 crushed and supped on at leisure
Great big beetles that live out beneath the
Flats, preying on large game like horses and
aurochs. The Hillfolk know what signs to
watch for, but they rarely tell outsiders. What
better way to keep the Flats free of fools?

Shellback drake
Solitary, cautious, hardy, patient
HP 16; Armor 3 (carapace, scales)
Damage snapping, bone-crushing bite d10
(hand, close, grabby, messy, 1 piercing)
Special Qualities nearly blind,
senses vibrations

ä Burrow into soil

Like a snapping turtle, but 6 feet long and
400 pounds, with big, badger-like claws.
Way faster than you think. It’ll dig itself
under a layer of soil, and wait long enough
for the gwead to start growing over it. Then,
when something comes near? SNAP!
An intact shell is worth quite a bit (immobile, Value 2). But good luck killing one
without cracking the shell.

Wild horse
					Group, large, cautious, swift
HP 10; Armor 0
Damage trample d6+3 (hand, close, forceful)
Instinct to panic
			ä Sniff trouble on the wind
			ä Run free!
			ä Vault over an obstacle
They roam the flats, in herds ranging in
size from a half-dozen to a couple dozen.
The larger herds will give rise to a ceffylwraig (page 368).

Sometimes, out on the Flats, you’ll see
what looks to be small hills in the distance.
I advise you to steer clear. They probably
aren’t hills, really, but huge piles of dirt that
a thunder drake has moved as it digs. And
hoo boy, you do not want to stumble into
one of them on their home turf.
Something interesting: Thunder drakes
eat mostly grass and shrubs, but they have a
taste for certain ores. They spend their time
grazing, sleeping, and digging up rocks.
Something useful: They particularly savor
the taste of copper, and relish aetherium
(page 404). If they find a source of it,
they’ll chew it right up and spend the next
few days belching lightning bolts.
When you are near a thunder drake when
it bellows, roll +CON: on a 10+, pick 1; on
a 7-9, pick 2; on a 6-, all 3.
		 Drop what you’re holding and cover
			your ears
		 Stagger back a few steps
		 You’re deafened and disoriented for a
			few moments

135

Fomoraij
The Green Lords (page 210) damned
themselves. Oh, they’ll tell you they had no
other choice. That their treacherous servants
drove them to it. That they knew not the
cost. That they were lied to by the Things
Below (page 412). But, no, they knew
what they were doing. They just didn’t care.
And why would they have cared? Had they
not lost everything? Had they not been
driven from their homes and hunted like
beasts, by the very servants they had created? By those who owed them everything?
So, yes damn you, the Green Lords made
their deals. They called up the darkness and
the poison. Let it flow through their
minds. Their flesh. Their lands.
And when not even that was enough to
turn the tides, when they were betrayed
again—this time by their fellow Makers
(page 264)—and they were corralled into
one last trap… well, what did they care?
What could they care? Let the traitors
have the surface. For now.
And so, twisted and mad with hatred,
they went below. Into the bosom of their
masters. There, they wait. They spawn new
horrors. They hate.
And one day, perhaps, they will return.

Weelimir, Yoanaw

Fomoraij

Dwellers beneath
The Fomoraij have dwelt beneath the
Great Wood for centuries. Their influence
can be felt, certainly, and they sometimes
send forth minions, but they have not seen
the surface since the fall of Vor Svetelik
(page 468).
To create a Fomoraij, start with 2 themes
from the Things Below (page 412).
Combine with a constraint that keeps it
from emerging, the nature of its lair, and
2-3 forms. Give it a name and perhaps
an ominous title. Write it up as a threat (a
villain, a beast, or a magical entity). If the
PCs are likely to fight it, also write it up as a
monster (see example on page 142).
1d12
1-2

constraint

Confinement: by accident, by artifice,
by its own size or nature (Instinct to
capture others, to escape)
3-4
Disdain: of the world above, of the
world that betrayed them (Instinct
to punish anything that reminds it
of its past)
5-6
Decay: of its mind, its power, its
sense of self (Instinct to lash out)
7-8
Fear: of the Fae, of the Makers, of
the much-hated light (Instinct to
see threats everywhere)
9-10 Obsession: with hoarding, experimenting, digging, etc., to the
exclusion of all else (Instinct to
pursue its obsession)
11-12 Patience: it waits, it gathers power,
it makes preparations (Instinct to
further its plans)

1d12

lair

1

A yawning cavern, flooded; dotted
with islands and unlikely structures
Endless rough-hewn tunnels, like
an ant hive
An inverted forest of roots, tubers,
fungus hanging from the ceiling
A series of underground streams,
rivers, lakes, all teeming with horrors
A deep, vertical shaft ending at a
vault of white stone
Tunnels and chambers, every inch
lined with bones
The petrified belly of a gargantuan,
primordial titan (page 303)
Vaulted tunnels lined with roots
of iron-infused wood, rusted and
bleeding
Limestone caverns, the walls pulsing with red crystal (page 424)
Limestone caves and columns, wet
and dripping, festooned with
slimy life
Strangely smooth tunnels coated in
warm, pulsing organic material
A vast chamber of white stone, a
mighty dead tree growing down
from the ceiling

7
8

Fomoraij lairs are always found underground and beneath the Great Wood (page
200), accessed via caves and Green Lord
ruins (page 214). The sites above are often
corrupted by the Things Below (page 412).

138

The PCs have encountered some horrors
in the Great Wood—some crinwin
oozing silver slime and wasps with
hallucinatory venom. I already decided
that these things were corrupted by the
Things Below, based on the themes of
“insanity/chaos/ruin/quicksilver” and
“corruption/disease/poison/vermin.”
Now I’m thinking that a Fomoraij is
behind them. I keep the two themes, and
roll a constraint of “confinement.” For
its lair, I roll “a series of underground
streams, rivers, lakes, all teeming with
horrors.” For its form, I get “too many
limbs/eyes/mouths,” and “a swarm of
smaller things” and “slimy/oozing/mucous.” I ponder, and come up with:
Yaarowslow the Many is trapped away
on an island in an underground lake,
fed by tiny rivulets of groundwater. It
has transformed its flesh into an oozing,
silvery nest, host to an endless swarm
of skittering and buzzing horrors that
serve as its eyes and hands and mouths.
For centuries, the vermin have burrowed through stone to reach the surface.
It has recently succeeded, and its silvery
spawn now taint a spring deep in the
Great Wood.

Fomoraij

Artifacts
Various treasures
		Preserved chimera corpse, mounted
for display (immobile, Value 1)
		A pouch of shimmering powder
(l l l l l doses, Value 2 to the right
buyer); ingest a dose and start compulsively telling secrets to those around you
		 A polished slice of agate the size of
a dinner table, bringing nightmares and
dark thoughts to those who linger in its
presence (immobile, Value 2)
		 A pair of what seem to be rubies
(Value 3) set as the eyes of a horrible
statue; they’re actually red crystals
(page 424) and impart a cruel bloodlust on whoever holds them.
Bloodshot amber
					 each, magical
1d4 fist-sized gems, riddled with red-black
intrusions.
When you drip blood onto an amber, name
a manifest spirit in your presence and roll
+CHA. If the spirit has an intact tether
(page 358), roll with disadvantage. On a
10+, the spirit is imprisoned in the amber;
on a 7-9, the amber starts to suck the spirit
in, but it may yet fight back; on a 6-, the
spirit wrests free and the amber shatters.
When you imprison a spirit in a bloodshot
amber, it is wracked with pain and cannot
manifest. Only one spirit can be imprisoned
at a time. When you touch the amber with
bare skin, you can communicate with the
spirit within and release it if you want (even
if you weren’t the one who imprisoned it).
When a bloodshot amber is found, it likely
contains a spirit that has been trapped in
agony for centuries.

Dream-lily pool
					 A raised pool of clear water. A
strange lily grows within, its tendrils
pouring over the lip and coating the floor,
hiding bleached bones. The tendrils rustle
when living things approach, and a gentle
wind promises prophecy and wisdom.
When you let a lily-tendril slither under
your skin, take 1d4 damage (ignores armor)
and fall deeply asleep. Roll +WIS: on a
10+, you dream of wisdom—ask the GM
any question and get a helpful, honest
answer, though one cloaked in symbolism; on a 7-9, you dream of wisdom (as
on a 10+) but also of hunger, desperation,
and want—mark a debility when you
wake; on a 6-, you are trapped in endless
nightmare—mark two debilities and you
shall not wake unless you find an exit or
someone rescues you from without.
Regardless, your dreams are clearly visible
in the waters of the pool. You get the sense
that something else may be watching, too.

					 Pick 1 or have someone roll.

Infected servant
					Fomoraij sometimes ensnare beings
of the world above, capturing them, luring
them in with promises of power, or compelling the weak-willed to attend them.
They then infect the victim, turning them
into extensions of their will.
Start with a base creature, and then pick
or roll for the nature of the infection, the
means of control, and up to 3 gifts. Interpret them based on what you know about its
master. Update the base creature’s tags, stats,
qualities, instinct, and moves accordingly.

infection

A bug/worm/vine, burrowed into
their brain/spine

A foul substance, injected/inhaled/
imbibed
5-6
A fungus/plant, growing under
their skin
7-8
A spirit possessing their body,
bound by intricate scars/tattoos
9-10 A mass of vermin/a single horror,
eating them from the inside out
11-12 One or more shards of red crystal
(page 424) embedded in their skull
1d12

means of control

1-2
3-4

By crushing their will
By eating away at what they
once were
5-6
By filling them with euphoria/
pleasure
7-8
By promising them power/immortality/revenge/a cure/etc.
9-10 By wracking them with pain
11-12 Roll 1d10 twice, or make up
something new
If you like, roll 1d6 for the extent of control.

Should a PC or an important NPC
become infected by a Fomoraij (or one of
their servants), write it up as a threat (an
affliction). If a PC willingly becomes an infected servant, consider creating a playbook
insert, using the Thrall for inspiration (see

I want a big scary servant of Yaarowslow the Many (page 142), a hagr
(page 208) that stumbled on the tainted
spring. I roll “a foul substance, injected/
inhaled/imbibed” and think of Yaarowslow’s silvery slime. I get “by eating
away at what they once were” as a means
of control, and imagine the silver slime
corrupting it from the inside, replacing its
blood and organs and overwhelming its
mind with voices and delusions. For gifts,
I pick “unnatural resilience/vigor/durability” and “a way to spread its infection
and/or its master’s influence.” I then roll
for a third gift and get “an area attack.”
I update the hagr’s stat block to reflect
these changes, and end up with this:
INFECTED HAGR

143

The Foothills
Two days northwest of S
﻿ tonetop, the rolling
﻿F lats (page 126) give way to hills, climbing
up into the ﻿Whitefang Mountains (page
480). The Highway (page 270) cuts through
these hills, leading to ﻿Barrier Pass (page
26). To the east, the hills wrap around the
﻿Great Wood (page 200).
The Foothills are rugged and forest-strewn,
crisscrossed with streams, dotted with
gorges and ponds and small lakes. In ages
past, it was a borderland between the Rime
Lords (page 316) and the Green Lords
(page 210), and later it was the extent of
the Barrow Builders’ reach (page 34).
Nowadays, it is mostly uninhabited, but
both Stonetop and Barrier Pass come here
to harvest timber.

Impressions
Always
		Hills rising up from the Flats, mountains
looming behind them
		 Young thin trees on the southernmost
slopes, getting bigger and older as you
press into the hills
		 Echoes of birdsong, beast-bellows,
tumbling rocks, raised voices
Spring
		Thin branches drooping with fresh
green leaves
		 Mud slipping and squishing underfoot
		 Surprising chill of shadowed vales,
where snow still lingers
Summer
		Muggy, humid heat
		 Sweat stinging your eyes as you hump
up another slope
		 Cold clear water from a gurgling
stream, tasting better than anything
144

Autumn
		Slopes turned red and orange and
yellow, then black and brown and gray
		 Plump squirrels and other critters,
busily foraging for winter
		 Cold, clammy mist lasting well into
the day
Winter
		Waist-deep snow drifts in the valleys
		 Frozen-over streams, water gurgling
ominously from below
		 Squeak of trees, swaying in the cold wind
		 Short, magical twilight as the sun
drops below the mountains

The Foothills

Questions
		 What striking difference do you notice
between the trees here and those in the
Great Wood?
		 Who typically leads Stonetop’s logging
trips up to the Foothills?

lurk in the Foothills?
		 What is said to draw its ire?
		 Who claims to have encountered it?
When, and how so?
		 Why do you/don’t you believe them?
		 What mundane dangers up there worry
you more than some legendary beast?

Hooks
		A logging expedition goes missing
		 Loggers stumble upon a ruin or
corrupted site

trip years ago, and now they reappear

Highway, blocking access to good timber
		An adventurer claims to know the
location of an old barrow in the Foothills, said to be laden with gold and
other treasure
145

The Foothills

Logging trips
A typical logging trip generates a wagonload of timber (Value 2), and requires:
		 A wagon and two horses;
		 ~20 able-bodied workers; and
		 Pulling Together over 9-12 days (for
travel and work)
Trips traditionally take place in autumn,
after the harvest is complete and only if
there are no more-pressing needs in town.
The Palisade steading improvement
requires ~1,000 logs of good timber (Value
3). A wagonload of timber (Value 2)
contains ~40-50 logs, meaning you need
~20-25 wagonloads. To gather that much
timber:
		 You need ~20 able-bodied workers, a
wagon, and two horses working for the
course of a season
		 You must talk to a few town elders, and
get their support
		 You risk accidents and attacks
		 You must spend 1 Surplus (to feed and
equip the loggers) and reduce Fortunes
by 1 (because of so many people and
resources being away for so long)
		 The steading must Pull Together over
the course of the season

149

Forest Folk

All the tales agree: the Folk were in the
Great Wood (page 200) long before
Stonetop was founded. They welcomed the
newcomers, or at least suffered them, but
demanded that villagers never fell a living
tree from the Wood.
The Forest Folk mostly kept to themselves.
But over generations, contacts were made.
Favors exchanged, friendships won, stories
shared. And more shared, too, judging by
the odd eyes that some villagers bear.
The villagers did not notice when the
Forest Folk left, not right away. Encounters
were never common. Then they became
rare. Then there were none at all.
Their absence echoes through the Great
Wood. Their crude glyphs can still be
found, carved into trees and boulders. Their
sacred places stand empty. And dangerous
creatures—things once hunted by the
Folk—now breed freely.
150

Names
Slavic-sounding, often diminutives:
Analiya, Asya, Avo, Boyko, Danja, Dimvo,
Dobri, Galya, Genko, Ivka, Inko, Kosta,
Lyubo, Milada, Milko, Mincha, Mitko,
Nadya, Neli, Penko, Polya, Radko, Raya,
Sajka, Sasho, Tsvetanka, Valya, Vasko

Questions
		 Who (among you/among your kin/that
you know) bears the strange eyes of the
Forest Folk? What do they look like?
		 How are folk with “the forest eyes”
treated in Stonetop? What about their
parents?
		 Did you ever meet one of the Forest
Folk? Aside from their eyes, which of
their inhuman features did you find
most disturbing?
		 It’s a mystery for sure, but what do you
think happened to the Forest Folk? Why?

Forest Folk

Lore
The Forest Folk’s ancestors were mortal servants of the Green Lords (page 210). They
lived nomadic lives among the trees, traveling
in small bands and living off the land.
Something interesting: The Folk would often commune with spirits of the wild (page
356), and sometimes had dealings with
the Fae (page 94). Maybe they know
where the Folk went?
Something useful: You know of a place
where the Forest Folk used to congregate,
not too far from Stonetop, or a Fae they
once dealt with frequently, or a sacred site
they revered—tell us about it! Also, tell
us why you think it’s a good place to start
investigating what happened to the Folk.

Where did they go?
That’s for you and your group to decide,
but possibilities include...
…		 they fled east, to escape the waking of
the Crombil (page 62);
…		 they went to fight the Fomoraij
(page 136), maybe at the behest of
the Fae;
…		 a servant of the Things Below (page
412) hunted them down, chased
them off;
…		 a curse transformed them into living
trees, though not all at once;
…		 a powerful Fae whisked them off into
its domain;
…		 they were ravaged by sickness; the
survivors left in search of a miracle; or
…		 their numbers were always few, and
they simply died out.

151

Forest Folk

Hooks

Glyphs and pictograms

		A hunter finds a mass grave of the
Forest Folk, from, oh, about 10 years back
		A cave is covered with glyphs and pictograms that reveal valuable lore, but
some of the glyphs are visible only to
those with the forest eyes
		 A trapper swears that they’ve seen one
of the Forest Folk, or signs of their
recent presence
		 60 years ago, Stonetop gave the Forest
Folk a precious gift, which they took
to one of the sacred sites; perhaps it’s
still there?

The Forest Folk left crude marks and
drawings throughout the Great Wood.
When the PCs encounter a point of interest in the Great Wood, and it was around
before the Folk disappeared, pick or have
someone roll the Die of Fate:

Forest eyes
Those who inherit the “forest eyes” see well
at night. They see by mere starlight as well
as most humans can see under a full moon;
they see by moonlight as well as most
humans can see at dusk.
Beyond that, certain plants, lichens, and
rocks shine with indescribable colors that
can only be seen with the forest eyes. The
Folk would sometimes be seen staring rapt
at a mundane grove or meadow, marveling
at some unseen beauty. Humans with the
forest eyes sometimes do likewise.
If a PC was born with (or develops) the
forest eyes, feel free to use these GM
moves on them:
ä		 Reveal glyphs, markings, or patterns
that only they can see
ä		 Distract them with colors and patterns
of impossible beauty

152

1d6 markings
1-2 None to be found
3
Hidden, incomplete/marred
4
Hidden, intact
5
Obvious but incomplete/marred
6
Obvious and intact
“Hidden” glyphs might be hidden intentionally, obscured by happenstance (vines,
growth, etc.), and/or made with pigments
that only the forest eyes can see.
To an outsider, these glyphs and pictograms seem like nonsense—arbitrary
shapes and crude, stylized drawings. But
the Forest Folk had an elaborate visual
vocabulary, and most of these markings are
rich with meaning.
A PC can Know Things to attempt
to discern the meanings of any such
glyphs, with disadvantage if they are
incomplete or marred. On a 7-9,
they discern a vague concept, like
“dangerous plants,” “bound spirit”
or “sorrow/loss”. On a 10+,
they glean the full meaning,
like “these plants are dangerous to eat” or “this
tree binds a spirit of
darkness” or “many
Forest Folk died
here at the hands
of a sorcerer.”

A wooden mask
					fragile, magical
Carved from pale elder wood in the shape
of a bird’s head, but with small horns and
fangs. The inside is chiseled with glyphs
like those that the Forest Folk used to make.
Something interesting: You recognize
glyphs for “beast,” “spirit,” “forest,” and…
“peace?” Or maybe “treaty.”
Something useful: When you wear
the wooden mask, beasts of the Great
Wood will do you no harm, though your
compan-ions have no such protection.
Perversions of nature are unaffected. If
you harm a beast of the Great Wood while
wearing the mask, it loses all power and
rapidly rots away.
Crystal knife
					hand, crude, magical
A blade of knapped quartz, with the hilt
wrapped in glyph-covered leather. It
gleams to those with the forest eyes.
When you wield the crystal knife as a
weapon, you can harm the manifest form of
any spirit, ignoring armor and dealing +1d4
damage. If the 1d4 comes up a 4, though,
the knife shatters into useless shards.

155

Forge Lords

Forge Lords were Makers (page 264)
who bent fire and metal to their will. They
built a sprawling civilization among the
Huffel Peaks (page 236) and on the
shores of the sea beyond. They wrought
wonders and traded with other Makers, in
what is now Gordin’s Delve (page 190).
They grew wealthy, proud, and strong.
A fire burned inside the Forge Lords. It fueled their magic, and also their passion and
creativity, their love of beauty and craftsmanship. Their desire to do better, to be
better. But this fire was also a curse. If left to
burn unchecked—if they gave in to ambition, jealousy, and greed—it could consume
them. Burn away their finer feelings, twist
them into terrible, fire-belching beasts.

156

To guard against this curse, the Forge
Lords lauded loyalty and generosity. They
observed strict rules of hospitality and
decorum. For centuries, it worked. Their
lands were hotbeds of politics and betrayal,
but bloody-handed murder was rare. Only
the most debased citizens lost themselves
to the Change.
Then came the Time of Cataclysm (page
413). Volcanoes belched forth lava
and ash. The Forge Lord’s principal city,
Moratia Rubra, was destroyed. So were
many of their mines, foundries, and estates.
Their society plunged into chaos. And
the Things Below (page 412) stoked the
flames of vengeance, greed, and paranoia.
Rivalry gave way to murder. Murder gave
way to slaughter. Only those who embraced the Change survived. And soon,
soon, the Forge Lords were gone. Only
draventao (page 170) remained.

Forge Lords

Themes
Pick, or roll 1d12.

theme

Aftermath of destruction
For example:
		Chimneys and bits of wall, sticking out
of rubble and tuff, charred and dusty.
		A mountainside, melted and smooth

Traces of industry

		A furnace, still warm from banked
coals, kept smoldering for centuries by
a groggy, mopey spirit.
		A rune-etched marble slab set before a
magma pool. Binds a spirit that manifests as a figure of molten rock. Helps
with metalwork in exchange for gossip.
		A crystalline matrix, home to a dozen
small flames that have burnt for centuries
without fuel. Opinionated blowhards, but
quite clever and craving novelty.
		A huge bronze statue of a boar, its
mouth agape. The spirit within
consumes the flammability of objects,
making them fireproof. But the statue
itself ignites with the slightest spark
and burns like hot oil.
		Small flame spirits, each bound into
a different glass sphere set into a wall
or fixture. Light up in the presence of
people, chat amiably.
		A cinderwraith (page 166) and the
bones it’s bound to, trapped behind a
masonry wall in some Forge Lord’s cellar

		A bronze statue of a regal figure, once
60 feet tall, found toppled, dented, and
buried in a valley.
		Another huge bronze statue of a regal
figure, still set upon its summit but
half melted.
		A portrait on a mountainside, a mosaic
made from millions of glass beads.
		A seamless tower of basalt, topped with
an open-faced onion dome of brilliant
brass, an eternal flame burning within.
		A marble slab, big as a house. One side
is covered with a bronze plaque, etched
with tight-packed calligraphic runes,
mostly illegible under all the lichen.

Mutra’s compound was once the showroom for some Forge Lord’s collected wealth. Outside,
it’s like the Treasury of Petra, in Jordan—columns before a recessed entrance and a looming
arch. The arch leads to a vast chamber with a lofty domed ceiling and numerous empty plinths,
each taller than a tall man. Mutra’s people have strung screens between the plinths to split the
chamber into barracks and storage and so forth. “Smaller” side chambers, carved further into
the rock, hold Mutra’s personal quarters—and his own not-so-modest collection.

		 Orichalcum
A mystical metal that flashes red with fire
and burns away the impure, bane to the
Things Below (page 412) and perversions
of nature. The Forge Lords knew its secrets—
how to mine it, smelt it, alloy it, and work
it—and it was treasured even in their day.
162

Consider rolling 1d6 to inform size, quantity, and/or scale.
Orichalcum often melts or vaporizes as it
burns away impurity. Proper alloying helps
prevent this, but even a well-made orichalcum spear might turn to slag as it strikes
down a corrupted foe.

Forge Lords

		 Anthracite
A lustrous, gray-black stone, a form of
purified coal. Catches fire like dry wood,
but burns hotter, longer, with very little
smoke. The Forge Lords used it to fuel
their furnaces and kilns, or even to just
heat their homes.
Some Ustrina (page 464) know the
secrets to making the stuff (cooking coal,
infusing it with flammability leached from
other objects), though they (probably?)
lack the numbers for extensive coal mining.
They occasionally trade anthracite in Gordin’s Delve, but only small amounts. The
Bosses or southern merchants (page 259)
would start wars to learn how it could be
mass produced.

Other materials
Many Forge Lords knew how to
make aetherium (page 404) and
invest it with special characteristics.
Indeed, they likely taught the Tempest Lords a thing or two.
A few Forge Lords could create
makerglass (page 388), or at least
shape it, but never with the skill and
deftness of the Stone Lords.
The Forge Lords prized black iron
(page 307). It can be worked only
with fire, strength, and skill—not
with magic—and so only master
smiths could work it.
A few Forge Lords dabbled with red
crystal (page 424), inspired by the
Things Below or just arrogant enough
to think they could control it.

An oversized ring
					 , beautiful, magical, Value 4
A fist-sized signet, large even by the Makers’
standards, made of orichalcum. It glows
like a dying ember, but is cool to the touch.
When anyone lies in the ring’s presence,
their tongue blisters and burns. The greater
the lie, the more damage is done.
Something interesting: This was the signet
ring of a Forge Lord, one of great wealth
and importance; the Ustrina (page 464)
often show keen interest in such things.
Something useful: This was the signet
ring of Omelor Xonane, a storied artificer.
Many of their works were said to be keyed
to this signet ring. Who knows what wonders it could unlock?

When you prick your finger and touch the
smaller mirror, it shows what is reflected in
the larger mirror until you break contact.
Your blood soaks into the surface, leaving
no stain.

Cinderwraith
					Solitary, large, spirit, undead, fiery,
terrifying
HP 23; Armor 1 (amorphous)
Damage blistering flames 1d10+1 (hand,
grabby, messy, ignores armor)
Special qualities fireproof, dormant
without fresh air to “breathe,” tethered
to its bones
Instinct to burn the living
ä		 Manifest a leering pillar of fire and
blackened hands (harmed only by
water or silver)
ä		 Melt silver or bronze, weaken steel
or iron, ignite wood, char flesh
ä		 Engulf a living being, char both
flesh and soul (deal damage with
advantage, inflict debilities, harm
even fireproof foes)
The Forge Lords burned with an inner fire,
a fire that threatened to consume them
even when they lived. And when they died
badly—slowly, desperately, betrayed and
full of helpless rage—that fire could linger
on. First it would burn away the Forge
Lord’s flesh, and then its spirit, and then
its soul. And then it would wait in the
bones, like heat in banked coals. Wait for
fresh air to give it new life, and fresh souls
to burn for fuel.

Metal constructs
					The Forge Lords could breathe life
into metal through clever workmanship,
spirit-craft, and magic. Many of these
wonders remain, though only a few still
function and those might function poorly.

Bronze colossus
Solitary, huge, construct, plodding,
implacable
HP 31; Armor 4 (metal, lacks organs)
Damage trample d10+5 (hand, close, area,
forceful) or crushing hands d10+5 (close,
reach, forceful, grabby) or hurled object
d10+5 (far, area, forceful, reload)
Special qualities fireproof, too big to hurt
with most weapons, vulnerable to
lightning
Instinct to defend its creator’s honor
ä		 Pretend to be “just” another statue
ä		 Come to life, screeching as it moves
ä		 Issue a booming challenge in a
forgotten tongue
ä		 Block their path
It stands nearly 60 feet tall, a muscular
Forge Lord cast in bronze. Similar statues
loom about, each one grand and imposing.
This statue, though, is shot through with
blueish veins. And it feels like it’s watching
you. Because it is.
The colossus guards the entrance to its master’s domain. If someone approaches honorably, speaking in the Forge Lord’s tongue,
it politely tells them to come again later, for
its master is away. Intruders, blowhards, and
thieves are dealt with forcibly.
Something interesting: Those blueish
veins are aetherium (page 404), and likely
what animates the statue. Which, alas,
means there’s no single weak-point. Maybe
you could harm this thing by drawing the
lightning from it.
Something useful: Or you could, you
know, just blast it with lightning and
overload it.

They’re not really shaped like spiders, but
that’s what they make you think of. Picture
a metal basket, holding gears and springs
and a glowing central core. Articulated legs
extend out, each ending in a little 3-clawed
pincer. They squeak as they move, their
many joints needing oil.
They were made to be little servants, extra
sets of hands to help around a house or
forge. Even now, they’re just trying to help.

Forge Lords

Iron hound
Horde, construct, undead, corrupted,
tireless, terrifying
HP 10; Armor 4 (metal, lacks organs)
Damage bite d6+2 (hand, grabby)
Special qualities fireproof, heat-sensing
Instinct to punish and torment
ä		 Follow the trail of its master’s
property
ä		 Fix its prey with a soul-chilling stare
ä		 Once it’s bitten down: crush bone,
shred muscle, tear apart (+messy,
3 piercing)
There was a Forge Lord who prized his
hounds as much as he prized his treasure.
When one would die, he’d capture its spirit
in red crystal (page 424) and set them
as the eyes in a crudely-wrought effigy.
If anyone would be so foolish as to steal
from him, he would loose his iron hounds
to punish not just the thief but all whose
hands had sullied his property.
That Forge Lord is long gone, but his iron
hounds remain. They’ve learned to leave
their master’s treasures lying about in plain
sight, and to give thieves a nice long head
start. For the more hands touch the stolen
goods, the more victims they can claim.

Singing sword
Solitary, small, construct, lonely, dutiful
HP 19; Armor 4 (quick, made of metal)
Damage cut, thrust, slash d10 w/advantage
(hand, messy, 2 piercing)
Special qualities flies, fireproof, blind,
heat-sense, bound to its hall
Instinct to defend its hall, to ease its
loneliness
ä		 Make a strange, ethereal song
ä		 Strike a warning blow, deft but
nonlethal
ä		 Respond enthusiastically to music
ä		 Block their path with a razor’s edge
This naked blade needs no wielder. It dances about the air, making music as it moves:
every bob a hum; every slice a whistle.
It was set here long ago, as guardian to
this crumbling hall. For ages it has kept its
vigil. Yet, this is not some mindless thing
waiting to cut down foes. It remembers
and yearns for the days in which this hall
was filled with light and laughter and—joy
of joys—music!
And so it waits. In the dark. Singing songs
to itself that have not been heard by any
living mortal ear. It waits, dutiful, and worries that it has misremembered the music it
once knew. It waits. In the dark. Alone.

Forge Lords

Draventao
Solitary, large, hoarder, paranoid,
terrifying, fierce
HP 22; Armor 4 (scales, steel plates)
Damage bite d12+3 w/ advantage (close,
reach, forceful, messy, grabby, 1 piercing)
Special qualities fireproof, bestial intellect,
slumbers for years at a time
Instinct to protect itself and its hoard,
from threats real or imagined
ä		 Lure or drive intruders into a trap
ä		 Attack with sudden, terrible
ferocity that makes even stout
hearts falter
ä		 Suck in air, stoking its inner flames
ä		 Breath sticky fire, d10+3 damage
(near, area, grabby, messy, reload,
ignores armor)
Picture a long and sinuous beast with
serpentine scales, curled upon a pile of
treasure, covered in a layer of long-cold
soot or ash. Bits of rusted steel protrude
from its flesh. Smoke drifts lazily from its
nostrils. Charred and jagged teeth poke out
from its sneering lips. An eye snaps open.
It fixes you with a glare like a red-hot coal.
Hard to believe that this thing, this beast,
was once a Maker. But it was. It was.
A draventao is what remains of a Forge
Lord. It survived the Time of Cataclysm
(page 413), when its rivals moved
against it, when its allies betrayed it,
when even its loved ones came for it
in the dark. It survived by Changing,
by letting its inner fire burn until its
heart was a blackened coal of wrath.
It shed its once-beautiful form. It
forced its servants to bolt living
metal onto its now-twisted flesh and
bones. Then it ate those servants.

170

Now, mostly, it sleeps. For years at a time,
it sleeps. It broods upon its treasure heap,
loathe to leave its secret, trap-strewn lair.
Only hunger draws it forth. Hunger, or vengeance to be had upon some brazen thief.

171

The Frozen Wastes
North of the Whitefang Mountains (page
480) lies a vast steppe, a windswept sea
of shortgrass, sedge, and lichen. No trees
grow here. The landscape is broken only by
rocky outcrops, low moraines, and kettle
lakes. It rarely snows and almost never
rains, but hail is somewhat common.
Caribou and mammoths graze the plateau,
preyed on by wolves and saber-toothed cats
and terrible things that crawl forth from
the lakes in summer. Bands of mammoth
herders roam in warmer months. But for the
most part, it’s an empty and forlorn place.

Farther north, the plateau gets even colder,
even windier, the steppe giving way to
tundra. Eventually, a mountain-like wall
of dark ice (page 322) blocks the way,
stretching across the horizon. The glacier is
the final working of the Rime Lords (page
316). It seals a rift in the world, and keeps
the worst of the Howling Curse (page
492) at bay. Mammoth herders come here
to quarry dark ice, slowly chipping the
glacier away.

Impressions
Always
		A landscape so wide and empty you
can see the horizon curve
		 The constant wind, whispering and
howling, tugging and lashing
		 Patterns forming in the windswept
grass or snow, or the growth of flowers,
like runes that fade just before you can
read them
		 The swell of anger, the urge to inflict
violence on your companions, as your
temper and patience fray
Spring
		Yellow and brown grass, splashed
with pale violet flowers
		 Fields of standing water, just inches
deep, reflecting the empty sky
		 Squelch and crackle of half-frozen
soil underfoot

172

Summer
		Days that last forever, the sun never
truly setting
		 Mix of bright green grass, red lichen,
white flowers
		 Swarms of insects, spawned from
muddy soil
		 Birds and ice drakes (page 491)
pecking at the soil or flocking to the sky
Autumn
		The world turned red and orange
and golden
		 Long shadows in the low, low sun
		 Sharp cold edge to the wind
		 The restless foraging of small animals
Winter
		Endless night, the sun barely rising
at noon
		 Thin carpet of frost and snow, shrubs
and dead grass poking out
		 The ceaseless, gnawing wind and cold
		 The sense that you’ve made a terrible
mistake in coming here

The Frozen Wastes

Traversing the Wastes
The Frozen Wastes are inhospitable in a
way that southerners can barely comprehend. On any expedition into the wastes:
		 You need a knowledgeable guide, like a
mammoth herder (page 177), who
knows the ways of this place
		 You need lots of firewood or dried
dung, for you’ll find no fuel and even in
the summer the “nights” can be freezing
		 The way will be grueling (from cold
and mud and bugs); you risk exhausting yourselves/your supplies
		 You need to watch out for predators,
like wolves (page 209) or mchebra-gris
(kleztigrs, basically, see page 379)
		 You need to watch out for kettle lakes,
easily mistaken for ice or shallow
standing water
		 You risk succumbing to the Howling
Curse (page 492), which taints much
of the Wastes

Questions

Frozen Wastes, blooming for a few days

about this place?

damned annoying?

do it?

The Frozen Wastes

Mammoth herders
Here’s a typical band:
Size hamlet (~15-30 souls)
Population +0
Prosperity -1
		 Trade with (pick 1 or 2)...
... other mammoth herders
... a farming valley
... a fortress-monastery
Resources
		 Mammoths (wool, ivory,
			bone, dung)
		 Sleds/sledges of bone, hide, timber
		 Hunting (wild mammoths, caribou,
hare, fish)
		 Gathering (timber, ivory, bone,
flint, herbs, dark ice (page 322)
Defenses +0
		 Mammoth-mounted warriors
		Hounds and hunting drakes
		Spears/javelins tipped with dark
ice, flint, bone, or bronze (crude)
About half of each band are youngsters,
with the rest being able-bodied adults.
Elders rarely allow themselves to survive
long enough to become a burden to the
others. A band typically keeps a herd of 10
to 20 adult mammoths (page 177), plus a
few young. They butcher their own beasts
only when desperate, keeping them instead
for wool, transport, and labor.

A band spends the warmer months on
the Frozen Wastes, but spend the colder
months sheltering in the caves, ruins, and
forests of the Whitefang Mountains (page
480). They’ll trade with valley farms
(page 484) or fortress-monasteries (page
486) in spring, before heading out into
the Wastes, or in autumn, before they
encamp for the winter.
Something interesting: Mammoth
herders tend to be impeccably polite, with
drawn-out, formal greetings and elaborate,
almost artistic ways of doing everything
from lacing up a parka to butchering a
beast.
Something useful: These rituals help
identify those afflicted with the Howling
Curse (page 492), who lack in patience
and good grace. Alas, this means that
mammoth herders interpret frustration or
bad manners as a sign of being accursed.

Names
Tibetan-inspired: Bhokhi, Cheyez, Dangyi,
Gamyang, Lhing, Jongash, Nayan, Ningo,
Nyayelar, Paing, Ramang, Samanz, Sewas,
Tawaro, Tsaru, Tsetse, Wasen, Yenphi

They have little to no use for finery.

Gods and religion
The village of Stonetop maintains a pavilion of the gods, an open-air structure with
shrines to four main gods:
		Aratis, the Lawkeeper (page 22)
		Danu, the Earth Mother (page 64)
		Helior, the Daybringer (page 224)
		Tor, the Rain-maker (page 452), the
Thunderhead, Slayer-of-Beasts
Tor is the most popular god in Stonetop, and associated with the Stone (page
12) itself, but all four gods receive their
proper respect. Depending on the PCs in
your game, some of the gods may be more
important than others. Aratis, for example,
will feature more prominently in a game
with a Judge PC than a game without one.
Other communities might worship some
or all of these gods, perhaps by different
names, perhaps with different aspects and
traditions. Or they might have different
gods entirely, gods unknown or
unworshipped in Stonetop.

178

Questions
		 Who tends the pavilion of the gods?
		 Are Stonetop’s gods part of a coherent
pantheon? Or a mix of gods from
different cultures?
		 What little gods are revered by your
family/in Stonetop/where you grew up?
		 What other gods are known in Stonetop? Do any have shrines in the pavilion?
		 Which god, if any, do you consider
your patron?

Gods and Religion

god arises in Marshedge (page 274) or
Gordin’s Delve (page 190), upsetting
the balance of powers there.
		 Someone offends their household
god(s); misfortune ensues.

179

Gods and Religion

True nature

Divine intervention

“God” is not a distinct category of being. If a
spirit or entity receives offerings and prayers,
and responds in kind, then it’s a god.

Gods shape the world in subtle ways:
through synchronicity, dreams, and omens,
or by acting in the spirit world. Even Tor,
who sometimes walks the world, limits
himself to testing mortals and bestowing
gifts and curses.

The origin of any given god is for you and
your table to decide. A god might be…
…		 one of the great spirits (page 300),
there at the dawn of time, or birthed
spontaneously from the fabric of existence, or made by the First Creators
(page 299) as a steward of the world;
…		 a Thing Below (page 412), or an emanation of such;
…		 a spirit of the wild (page 356), transformed through worship;
…		 the child of other gods, or of a god and
a mortal;
…		 a mortal, ascended/transcended/reborn;
…		 a mortal, revered in death;
…		 a spirit born from humanity’s beliefs;
…		 one of the above, but also part of a
duality/triumvirate; or
…		 one of the above, but died and reborn.

180

Every so often, though, a god will touch a
mortal and imbue them with divine power.
They then send that mortal forth as their
instrument in the world. Such mortals become prophets or saints, heroes or villains,
and help shape the world for generations
to come. If you have the Blessed, the Judge,
the Lightbearer, or the Storm-marked
Heavy among your PCs, they might well
be one of these god-touched mortals.
Should the PCs wish a god to intervene on
their behalf, they can petition them (see
next page). Should the PCs offend a god, or
oppose one, or otherwise draw its ire, write
that god up as a threat (a magical entity).

Gods and Religion

Religion
For most people in the World’s End,
religion is not about faith or morality. It’s
a practical matter. The gods are, and they
influence the world. You perform rites and
make offerings in order to gain their favor
and avert their wrath. Even priests and the
like aren’t necessarily devoted to the gods
they serve; they just know what the gods
expect and how to read their signs.
There are, however, some mortals who feel
a profound connection to their god(s).
Such devotees give offerings gladly. They
aspire to emulate their god(s), to heed their
teachings and spread their glory.
Devotees might form fellowships with the
like-minded, which in time can become
institutions: formal priesthoods, mystery
cults, temples, and the like. Such institutions can be powerful forces in the world.
They might preserve knowledge, legitimize
leaders, protect innocents, or otherwise stabilize societies. But they might also grow
corrupt, fat on power and privilege, run by
cynics rather than true devotees.

Petitioning the gods
Religion is an expected part of daily life.
You help in the fields, you clean your
house, and you give the gods their due.
There’s no extra benefit for performing the
usual rites or offerings. But if you fail to
do them, you invite disapproval from your
neighbors and misfortune from the gods.
PCs can seek the favor of the gods in order
to Bolster (Book I, page 524). This involves
time and effort, rites and offerings above
and beyond what’s typical and expected. Be
sure to ask the PC for details! What god(s)
do they petition? What sort of aid do
they seek? What offerings do they make?
Consider, too, the consequences. What
other work goes undone? How do NPCs
react? Etc.
Should a PC seek a specific boon from
the gods—a curse lifted, an injury healed,
a curse laid upon a foe, an answer or an
insight, etc.—then Make a Plan! Some
example requirements include:
		 You must learn the proper rites/offerings
		 You must obtain a specific sacred object
		 You’ll need the help of a particular spirit
		 You’ll need to wait for an auspicious
date/time
		 You must travel to a particular sacred site
		 The rites will take hours/day/weeks
		 You must make an appropriate sacrifice
(Value 2 at least)
		 You risk angering the god(s)
Choose requirements based on the PC’s
request, the god(s) they petition and how
they are typically worshipped, the PC’s
relationship to the god(s), and how active
the gods are in your particular game.

181

Gods and Religion

Little gods
These are the gods of households, of specific places or geographic features, of particular chores or crafts or trades. Many are just
elemental spirits (page 360) worshipped
at a sacred site (page 362). But others are
spirits closely tied to humanity.
Little gods mostly just do their thing, quietly watching over their people or places,
keeping things running or causing trouble,
as is their wont. A little god can:
ä		 Reveal what it knows, from its own
perspective, to those who can speak to it
ä		 Calm/annoy/frighten animals or children
ä		 Move/hide/reveal an unattended item
ä		 Express displeasure through accidents,
misfortunes, neglect
ä		 Fend off other spirits
Little gods manifest only to small children
or at the beck and call of someone like
a Blessed or spirit-talker (page 235).
When they do manifest, treat them more
like NPCs than monsters.
182

Here are some example little gods. Feel
free to modify them, expand on them, and
ask the PCs questions about them.
Ancestral spirits
					Group, spirit, protective, conservative
Instinct to protect their family’s honor.			
Manifests in dreams, as drafty whispers.
Expects an altar, prayers, incense/libations.
Notes Like grandparents who don’t quite
get you.

Instinct (roll 1d4) 1 = to languish;
2 = to run rampant; 3 = to show off;
4 = to give back.
Manifests as flower-folk and little creatures.
Expects first fruits, water, blood.
Notes Many voices talking over each other.

Instinct to keep secrets.
Manifests as an echo in the dark, as a
misty figure.
Expects water, snow, coins, wishes.
Notes Desperately wants to know how
various tales end(ed).

Gods and Religion

God of weaving
					Solitary, spirit, exacting, insightful
Instinct to reward diligence, to punish
perfectionism.
Manifests as a spider, as idle thoughts.
Expects one perfect bit of fabric to dwell in,
at least one minor flaw in everything else.
Notes A trickster (tangles yarn, snaps your
warp), sometimes a wise councilor.
God of whisky
					Solitary, spirit, secretive, grandiose
Instinct to take its sweet time.
Manifests as a vaporous figure.
Expects the head & tail of each batch.
Notes Passionate about flavors, smells.
Hearth spirit
					Solitary, spirit, friendly, nurturing
Instinct to belong.
Manifests as pops & whispers, as an
impish little person.
Expects warm greetings, friendly banter,
little offerings cast into the flames.
Notes Natters on quietly even if no
one’s present.

Household spirits
					Group, spirit, helpful, mischievous
Instinct to easily take offense.
Manifests as gnomish little folk.
Expects bowls of goat’s milk, bits of bread.
Notes Complain loudly to each other,
grumble about leaving if things don’t
improve.
Threshold spirit

Instinct to dislike outsiders.
Manifests as a grumpy little person.
Expects sprinkles of salt, poured-out
whisky, reverent touches.
Notes Crosses arms, puffs up, harrumphs.
See also the following elemental spirits
(pages 360-361):
		Beast wardens (for livestock)

the fields)

the Ringwall)

Gods and Religion
From there, envision the god’s specific
sphere of influence, how it is depicted, its
rites, its myths, and its true nature
(page 180).
To name your god, use one of these methods:
		 Pick a word associated with the god,
translate it into an appropriate language, then change it a little (“threshold” >> “trothwy” in Welsh >> “Troth”)
		 Pick a deity from actual mythology or
popular culture, and change a letter or
two (“Thor” >> “Tor”).

Then give them one or more titles,
kennings, or honorifics to reflect their
portfolio. Minor gods might have only a
title; “She Who Threads the Needle” might
go by no other name than that.
If this god will cause ongoing trouble for
the PCs or the village, write it up as a
threat (a magical entity).

The PCs are approaching Three Coven Lake (page 438), and I’ve decided that a group
of Hillfolk (page 228) called The White Hand patrol the area. Their god tells them to keep
outsiders away and to keep the horrors of the lake at bay. I consider having them worship
Helior, but decide to make up a new god instead.
I start by picking a theme of “bravery/justice/oaths/protection,” but also roll a second theme
for flavor. I get a 12, focusing on “fire/rebirth”. Then I roll a 6 for relevance (“minority within a larger people”), an 8 for standing (‘invoked only when needed”), and 8 for
observances (“pilgrimage/hermitage”). I could use a bit more, so I roll another observance:
5 (“fashioning of monuments”). I look at the possible true natures, ponder a bit, and settle
on this:
The White Hand worship Gyndorn of the Cleansing Flame,
a hero of the Hillfolk who led an uprising against the
Barrow Builders (page 34), and whose name the
Hillfolk invoke when facing unholy evil.
Gyndorn charged her followers with eternal
vigilance. She calls warriors and spirit-talkers
from all tribes to pass through a sacred flame and
be reborn as members of the White Hand. Members
of the sect follow a circuit around the lakes that
Gyndorn took herself, marking certain landmarks
with white handprints each time they pass.
And each band in the sect carries a consecrated
flame with them as they go.

185

The Golden Oak
On a clear day, sharp-eyed folk in Stonetop
can spot the Golden Oak, gleaming at the
horizon and dwarfing the trees around it.
According to the tales of the ﻿Forest Folk
(page 150), a hero buried at the roots of
the Oak can find their way back from
beyond the Last Door (page 70). But no
one from Stonetop has ever made it there
and back.

Questions
		 When was Stonetop’s last expedition to
the Golden Oak? Who were they
trying to save?
		 What other tales have you heard about
the Oak? How much of them do
you believe?
		 Who would have to die for you to risk
a journey to the Golden Oak?

Getting there
Something interesting: The Golden Oak
doesn’t quite exist in this world, at least not
anymore. It can only be reached through ﻿Fae
pathways, at great risk and personal cost.
When you travel towards the Golden Oak
in the mundane world, you never reach it.
Even if you were to fly above the trees,
it would remain always out of reach, its
bearing shifting.
Something useful: To reach the Golden
Oak, you must…
		 learn to walk the Fae paths (see way			stones, page 478);
		 follow the Fae paths to a specific set of
			waystones, those closest to the Oak;
		 find a secret crossing and face its
			 guardian, set there by the Lady of
			Crows herself (page 71); and
		 pay the Lady’s due to make the crossing.
186

When you pay the Lady’s due, choose 1 as
a group:
		Present a marker of the Pale Hunter’s
			favor (page 292), honestly earned
		Each of you must make a sacrifice.
			 Cross off a move you possess; it is gone
			 and you cannot regain it. (Followers
			 cross off a tag of the GM’s choice instead.)
		One of your number passes willingly
			 through the Last Door, their body
			 gone, never to be seen again.
When you make the crossing, you glimpse
the Last Door and what lies beyond. Tell

The Golden Oak
that tells you that something is making
you more angry, savage, and violent?

The savage curse
This was once a hub of the ﻿Green Lords’
civilization (page 210). When the F
﻿ ae
(page 94) rebelled and turned on their
masters, they claimed it for themselves. The
surviving Lords laid a curse of primal
savagery on the place. The rebel Fae
abandoned it and the ﻿Lady of Crows
(page 71) sealed it away.
When you are under the vast canopy of the
Golden Oak…
…		 and you Persuade using anything but
violence or threats, the best you can
get is a 7-9.
…		 and you give in to the savagery in
your heart, regain 2d8 HP and become
larger, more muscular, more bestial. Gain
advantage when you roll STR and disadvantage when you roll WIS until you
leave this place.
us what you see.

Setting the scene
The Golden Oak itself is enormous, so
big as to defy any sense of scale. The bark
is pale and luminous, and the glittering
canopy is distant as the sky.

…		 and you fight with no mercy,
hesitation, or remorse, roll damage
with advantage.
…		 and you seek to make something, heal
someone, or repair something, roll
+WIS: on a 10+, you can work as long

		 What detail do you notice that drives
home the fact that everything here is
huge and massive?
		 What here tells you this is a place of
primal, unchecked vitality?
		 What do you notice about yourself,

The Golden Oak

easily distracted, frustrated, or enraged.

Dangers
Pick 1, or have someone roll the Die of Fate.

An overwhelming urge to fight,

The Golden Oak

Treasures of
the Golden Oak
		A living leaf from the Golden
Oak can be made into a Goldleaf
Cloak (page 517).
		With proper tools and knowledge, the
bark can be harvested to craft weapons
or armor (2 armor, warm, cumbersome)
that is hard as iron and bane to undead
or things from beyond the Last Door.
		A
golden acorn, harvested in
autumn, can be planted elsewhere to
grow a Golden Sapling (see sidebar).

Buried at the roots
When you bury a corpse at the roots of
the Golden Oak, close to the trunk, roll
and add…
…		 +1 if the corpse has not yet started to rot;
…		 +1 if they died bravely in their prime; and
…		 +1 if their name is still well-known.

. steading improvement .
GOLDEN SAPLING
Requires all of the following, in order:
		 Retrieve an
acorn from the
			 boughs of the Golden Oak in
			late autumn
		 Plant the acorn in the fields
			near Stonetop, watering it with
			 the lifeblood of a great virile
			beast, like an aurochs bull
		 Nurture and protect the
			 sapling through 4 years of growth
When you mark all the requirements,
add “Golden Sapling” to the Resources
list and automatically mark the
Greater Harvest improvement.
Henceforth, when the steading
generates Surplus, even just 1, it
generates +1 Surplus.

On a 10+, they return from beyond the
Last Door when the sun next rises, whole
and healthy though likely shaken; on a 7-9,
they do not yet rise, but maybe if you give
it another night? On a 6-, they return, but
something else comes with them, something dangerous and dark, something now
tied to them in a deep and primal way.
