Primordial powers

The Seasons Change and the PCs get “interesting news.” I want to give them a lead on the
Ineffable Words (page 568), to be found in a primordial site in the Whitefang Mountains (page 480).
I roll a 4 on the Whitefangs terrain table (“waterlogged soil, frozen or muddy”) and a 9 for
a marker (“ruin of the Makers”). In the Whitefangs, that’s probably a Rime Lord ruin.
I roll a 10 for theme (“mythic times, the birth and death of gods and legends”) and an 8
for origins (“death of a primordial entity”). For features, I roll a 4 (“the shade of a dead
god…”), and pick “one or more artifacts of primordial power” (the Ineffable Words). I
create the Rime Lord ruin using the tables on page 320, and the dead god using the steps
on page 184.
I picture a high place, where a demon killed Nangbu—a goddess of writing, wisdom, and
prophecy. The Rime Lords carved a temple into the slope, to honor her and commune with
her shade, but avalanches buried the temple. What remains is an alpine meadow with a
few chunks of Maker-carved stone sticking out of muddy soil. There’s a stillness to the air, in
which one can sometimes hear a whispering voice.
For their interesting news, I think the PCs will acquire a scroll describing this temple
of Nangbu, and how the dead god’s shade dwells there and bestows gifts of wisdom and
prophecy upon petitioners (the Ineffable Words).

		A fallen megalith, half-sunk in peat

careful, their spirit will be drawn from

305

Primordial powers

Portals
There are places throughout the world that
touch each other on a fundamental level,
despite being hundreds of miles apart.
The Makers knew how to create portals
in such places, allowing for instantaneous
travel between them. One might find such
a portal…
… beneath the Ruined Tower (page 332)
… in the Labyrinth (page 242)
… hidden away near Three Coven Lake
(page 438)
… among the ruins of Vor Svetelik
(page 468)
… up on Tor’s Fist (page 481), perhaps
… maybe in certain ruins down in Lygos
and the South (page 256)
… anywhere else you think makes sense.
Portals can be activated via Opening the
Way (page 517), which requires inscribing the sigil sequence of one portal within
the sigils of another.

306

Should the PCs learn to use portals, they
might wish to create new ones. To do so,
they must:
		 Locate a suitable site of primordial power
		 Transport a slab of pure white stone
(Value 2 or 3, depending on size) to
the site
		 Discern the correct sigil sequence for
this site
		 Have an exceptional stonecutter spend
a season (Value 2) carving the sigils
into the stone
		 Risk damage to the stone or a
flawed portal
The Makers’ portals were safe and stable,
but flawed or damaged portals are terribly
dangerous. One might explode, tear travelers apart, and/or open a rift (page 311)
in the world. See the cracked portal (page
473) of Vor Svetelik for an example.

Primordial powers

		 Black iron
A few rare veins of this metal exist in the
world, but mostly it’s retrieved from meteorites. It is completely, utterly immune to
magic.
		Fae (page 94) treat it like iron, but
worse—its touch unravels their magic
and they take +1d6 damage (messy,
ignores armor) from it
		Spirits can’t touch it, perhaps can’t even
perceive it
		It can damage even insubstantial forms
of manifest spirits (page 358), but
does no true harm to the spirit itself
		It damages or destroys any open portal
it touches, going nowhere
		Spells, rituals, and other magic cannot
affect it, pierce it, detect it, or perceive
things enclosed by it
When the PCs encounter some, pick or
roll for its form:

etc.), likely immobile
Pick or roll for its size and its Value to
those who understand what it is. For

Black iron is strong and hard like steel and
worked much the same way. It’s a little on
the heavy side, and doesn’t hold quite as
keen an edge. Black iron weapons don’t
have “x piercing,” but a black iron warhammer would still have 2 piercing.

308

A bejeweled helm
, magical, loud, dangerous, 1d4+1 uses
Bronze, battered, and adorned with seven
fittings, 1d4+1 of which bear purple gems
that hum ominously. When you don the
helm, you sense the gems’ power and how
to use them but can hear nothing over
their incessant noise.
When you unleash a gem’s power, pick a
target you can see and roll +WIS: on a 10+,
your target freezes in time and space for as
long as you wear the helm and maintain
focus; on a 7-9, you must focus for a few
seconds, but then the effect kicks in as per
a 10+.
While your target remains frozen, the gem
hums louder and louder, painfully so. When
the effect ends, the gem crumbles to dust.
When you try to maintain focus, roll
+CON: on a 10+, you’re fine for now; on a
7-9, either mark a debility or let the effect
end; on a 6-, mark a debility and either let
the effect end or ask the GM how time
and space start to go haywire around you.
While frozen, the target (and anything on
their person) is inviolate—they cannot be
harmed, moved, or otherwise interacted
with. They rejoin the timeline as if no
time had passed for them, with whatever
momentum they previously had.

Primordial powers

A sealed amphora
, fragile, magical, 3 uses
					
A ceramic, two-armed jug with a wax seal.
Inside is water of life, the quintessence
of water, so pure that it washes away all
impurity, corruption, poison, or disease
from any living thing. It becomes sullied
and loses its potency when used, or if left
unsealed for more than a minute or so.
The amphora itself is a perfect vessel: as
long as it is sealed, no substance within will
rot, putrefy, or otherwise spoil.

A fossilized horn
					 , magical
A spiral thing, maybe 2’ long, the remnant
of some long-gone incarnation (page
300). Those sensitive to the spirit world
can sense its lingering, radiant power.
When you bear the fossilized horn, you have
advantage on any move to impress, intimidate, or threaten a spirit (though it grants
no special ability to communicate with
spirits in the first place). Should you roll a
6- on any such roll, the spirit can choose to
destroy the horn and end its effect, in
addition to whatever else the GM says.

Incomprehensible knowledge
					Threat (affliction)
Instinct to unravel any sense of safety/self
When one delves into primordial mysteries, one must grapple with alien and unsettling ideas. Dwell on them too long, and
you find your mind turning against itself.
l		 You fidget, fixate, have trouble sleeping

or relaxing

l		 Change your instinct to… (pick 1)

		 Paranoia: To invent dangers
		 Compulsion: To obsessively repeat
certain actions
		 Apathy: To let things escalate
l		 Henceforth, when the GM compels
you to act on your instinct, mark XP if
you do and Defy Danger if you don’t
l		 Impending doom:
Retire your PC; they
become a threat
(a wildcard)

Primordial flame
					Primordial, terrifying, magical
Damage 1d12 w/advantage (hand, messy,
ignores armor)
ä		 Radiate blistering heat, 1d10
damage (reach, area)
ä		 Start normal fires nearby
ä		 Reduce what it touches to ash
A tongue of perfect blue fire, somehow
brought into the world. It is likely found
held in stasis by some potent magic or
artifice. For while it consumes and destroys
all it touches, it cannot last
long in contact with
base reality.

Rift
When primordial power runs amok, it
can damage reality itself. Such rifts might
manifest as places where:
		Time runs slowly/quickly/not at all
		Things emerge from other times/
places/realities
		Natural laws like light, heat, gravity,
and death behave strangely
		The world breaks down into chemics/
crystals/raw elements
		New lifeforms/consciousnesses arise
Fundamentals (page 312), voidblights
(page 313), or other dangerous spirits
enter the world
		The Things Below spread their influence (see corrupted sites, page 422)
Most rifts are temporary, soon sealed by
archons (page 302) or other primordial
entities. But if one persists for any length
of time, write it up as a threat (a MacGuffin, Instinct to unravel reality).

Primordial powers

Fundamental
					Solitary, spirit, primordial
HP 19; Armor 1 (lacks vitals), harmed
only by black iron
Damage destructive touch 1d10 w/advantage (hand, grabby, ignores armor)
Instinct see below
ä		 Manifest in the proper conditions
ä		 Communicate poorly
ä		 Burden with incomprehensible
knowledge (page 310)
ä		 Refuse to cooperate
ä		 Manipulate/manifest its element,
locally and dramatically
These are spirits (page 358) of fundamental forces, far removed from mortal
concerns. They are active only near sites
of primordial power and uncontrolled
rifts (page 311), or when called forth by
powerful, dangerous magic.

Example fundamentals include:
Time

Instinct to overwhelm

Manifests as a hole in reality
Instinct to get confused/annoyed

Manifests as an unmoving crystalline form

Manifests as a bewildering series of
impressions
Instinct to undermine, to surprise

ä		 Pounce on unsuspecting prey
ä		 After a good meal: spawn another
of its kind
To those who can perceive them, they seem
like many-limbed salamanders made of starfilled night. Their broad, flat, eyeless heads
split open into rows of glittering teeth.
They are alien things, spiritual scavengers
and predators. They haunt forgotten corners of the spirit world, the dead worldmotes that float through the cosmic void.
Mostly, they wait and hunger. But if they
catch the scent of a traveler, or worse, a
rift (page 311) into our world, they will
follow it.

Treat an incursion of voidblights as a
threat (type: beasts) with the following
grim portents:
l		 A few local spirits are killed; others are

spooked; sensitive mortals are unnerved

l		 “Softer” local spirits die or flee; plants

fail, animals grow sickly, misfortune
abounds; voidblights attack sensitive
mortals
l		 Almost all local spirits flee or are eaten;
local systems collapse and die
l		 Impending doom: voidblights spread
into new colonies, all over the region
and soon the world
Stakes: Will any of the PCs’ enemies join
them to face this threat? How will the environment be permanently changed? What
will happen if the voidblights are corrupted
by the Things Below?
Other primordial entities
		The Things Below (page 412)
		Great spirits (page 300), possibly including the ﻿Lady of Crows (page 71)
and some or all of the major gods
(page 178)
Incarnations (page 300), including the
Willow Witches (page 120) and
perhaps the Nine-fingered stranger
(page 457) and/or the Pale Hunter
(page 292)
		Archons (page 302), including
﻿Tcaventes, Shackle and Key (page 254)
		Titans (page 303), perhaps including
the Crombil (page 62) and/or the
Suileach (page 93)

313

Red Groves
Half a day east of Stonetop is a copse of
trees that turn blood red (leaves, bark, and
all) at the autumnal equinox. By spring, the
color has returned to normal. The Forest
Folk (page 150) warned the villagers to
avoid this place. They never said why.
		An iron tang in the air
		The scrutiny of the forest upon you,
heavy like a blanket
		Words of warning, faintly heard in the
breeze, in the rustling leaves, in the
insect hum and birdsong.
		Spring/Summer: bright green shoots/
leaves bursting from black bark and mud
		Autumn/Winter: overwhelming,
glossy red
Tip: faintly whisper things like “turn back”
or “not welcome” or “go away” and then
pretend you didn’t say anything

Lore
Something interesting: A dozen such
groves dot the Great Wood. Some redden
in the fall, others in spring. Some, like the
one near Stonetop, are large and old; others
are just a few young trees.
Something useful: When you enter a red
grove, you draw the forest’s wrath unless…
… you come naked and empty handed,
			 having been ritually purified;
… you bear a dying spirit, wishing to pass
			on; or
… you are cloaked in subtle magic.
Redwood serves as a conduit for spirits of
the wild (page 356). Some of the Forest
Folks’ most powerful totems are made of
redwood. Alas, harvesting redwood without the permission of the forest spirits is
sure to draw their primal, relentless ire.
314

Why are they so special?
That’s for you to decide. Maybe...
…		 they are where spirits of the wild
(page 356) are born and where they
come to die;
… they each imprison some terrible evil;
… they each mark the grave of a cham			pion of Danu (page 64); or
… it’s different for each grove.

Red Groves

Hooks
		A dying glasbren (page 366) seeks a
mortal to convey her to the Red Grove.
		The Red Grove is corrupted by the
Things Below (page 412). Its spirits
are confused and unwell, and the Grove
will soon start spawning horrors.
		A villager steals a branch of redwood,
and is cursed with the forest’s wrath.

Inhabitants
Any spirit of the wild (page 356) might
be found here, but each grove hosts at least
a few brennauwed (page 366) and is protected by coedwaig (page 369). Butcherbirds (page 204) frequent the Groves
while they are red.

The Forest's Wrath
					Threat (affliction)
Instinct to punish the perpetrator and
those who protect them
The greater the transgression, the more
quickly the curse escalates and the greater
the penance required.
l		 Nightmares, ire from beasts and birds,

plants tend to wither and die

l		 Friends and family are affected, too;

birds and small beasts harass them
in town
l		 The whole village is affected; sleep is
scarce, nerves frayed, the Wood is hostile
l		 Saplings sprout in town overnight, or
when folk aren’t looking
l		 Impending doom: Crops fail, livestock
revolt, homes collapse, the granary rots
315

Rime Lords
In the utmost north, the Rime Lords made their domain. They
spread through the Whitefang Mountains (page 480) and the
tundra beyond, embracing the ice and the cold and the terrible
howling emptiness.
The Rime Lords developed all manner of ascetic disciplines
and esoteric arts. They inured their bodies to the cold.
They sent their minds wandering through spirit realms.
They shaped matter and energy with their will, imposed stillness on the world, froze pockets of reality
in timeless stasis.
Disciples came from the farm-valleys and the
mammoth herders, to study at the feet of these
living gods. Their teachings were harsh, rigorous,
and demanding. But for a time, monasteries
dotted the north and the ways of the Rime
Lords flourished.
But then the servants of the Stone
Lords (page 382) rebelled and set
loose the Things Below (page 412).
Something cracked open at the very
roof of the world, and the voice of
Y’aaw’kara (the Howling Wind,
the Flayer of Flesh) was unleashed.
Bloodthirst and madness spread.

316

Rime Lords
Some Rime Lords fell to the Howling
Curse (page 492). Others were brought
low by mortal mobs run rampant. The thirteen survivors agreed that something must
be done. They each retreated to a place of
power, sent forth their minds, and together
sealed the rift beneath a glacier of dark ice
(page 322), quieting Y’aaw’kara’s voice.
The Howling Curse dulled to a constant
whisper. But the last thirteen Rime Lords
had sacrificed themselves. Their bodies
remain encased in ice, their minds working
the magic intended to keep Y’aaw’kara’s
voice at bay for eternity.

Names
Sanskrit-inspired: Akativet, Artarapishi,
Dahishika, Goranat, Kanishanu, Mamaranu, Marash, Nilani, Prayanavi, Ralananan,
Ranaji, Shayand, Supnam, Vitatana
Often combined with a title of “Gar”
(“teacher”) or appellations of “Div” (“the
holy”) or Kav (“the wise”). For example
“Gar Akativet,” “Kanishanu Div,” or
“Ranaji Kav.”

Hooks
		A rogue has some dark ice (page 322)
to trade, and knows where to find more
		Timber-cutters in the Foothills (page
144) disturb a Rime Lord-era ruin
		Prospectors from Gordin’s Delve (page
190) find one of the last thirteen Rime
Lords, still in stasis, encased in ice
		A new glacier starts to form in the
Whitefang Mountains (page 480),
spreading into the Great Wood (page
200) and upsetting the spirits there

preservation

spirit-walking, thought-crafting

of the past

their sects, schisms, and rivalries

Thoughtform
Objects, sensations, or vignettes of manifest will, formed of base matter or energy,
like how a spirit manifests a body. Not real,
but real enough.
		A grand tree, growing in a lightless
cave. Its bark and wood are shaped of
stone, but its petals/leaves/fruits crumble to dust and water when shed.
		A bubble of stillness on a wind-swept
summit, boasting an elaborately decorated altar. The altar is flimsy as frost,
dissolving if touched.
		A chant that fills a valley, echoing
endlessly from the mountainsides.
		A spring in a low valley, the water
never freezing. Drinking vessels are
found at the water’s edge, impossibly
beautiful, but melting into water
if removed.

bearing a vivid painting of a Rime

		A column of dark ice, tall as a tower,
embossed with symbols and glyphs
		A 50-foot statue of a Rime Lord atop a
mountain, made of pebbles held impossibly in place

The PCs have sent a logging crew up to

terrain table and get “ravine/gorge/gully/

and is “buried, submerged and/or overgrown, but mostly intact.” I ponder and
come up with this:

Rime Lords

A series of tombs, carved into the side of a
ravine, containing the remains of a Rime
Lord’s mortal disciples. The lower tombs are
obscured by brush and debris, but the upper
tombs are still visible, their entrances carved
with guardians and ice-crystal patterns. I
suspect someone from the logging crew stumbled in here and upset a guardian spirit.

Rime Lords

A column of ice
magical, immobile
Amidst a high, lonely meadow stands a
pillar of dark purple ice, 12 feet tall and 2
feet thick. It rests on a circular slab of
rune-etched stone, a dozen paces across,
untouched by time or nature. There’s a
stillness to the place, a tension in the air.

A small black sphere
magical, indestructible, immobile
Found in or near some Rime Lord ruin,
this… thing… appears to be a perfect
sphere of lead, maybe 2 inches across. It
hovers motionless in the air. No amount of
physical force can mar it, nor move it from
its relative position.

Something interesting: The runes appear
to be some sort of spell, waiting to be triggered; it’d take days of study to puzzle out
the command word (and you risk triggering it accidentally).

When you focus all your will upon the black
sphere, roll +WIS: on a 10+, you can slowly
move the sphere with your mind, so long
as you keep your attention fixed upon the
task; on a 7-9, pick 1:

Something useful: You can determine the
command word with a few hours of study
(less if you’re versed in Rime Lord script).

		 Mark a debility and move the sphere as
per a 10+
		 Move the sphere, but only a few inches
		 Fling the sphere in a random vector (if
it matters: d8 damage, forceful, messy)

When you speak the command word near
the column, the dark ice expands in a flash,
encasing the stone slab and anything on it.
The ice traps even spirits (manifest or not).
Mortal prisoners soon perish, their souls
ensnared. The dark ice never melts and resists all but the mightiest magic and blows.
A dark ice knife
hand, magical, crude
A hilt of bone, with a spike of dark ice
tapering to a sharp point. The ice is hard as
stone, and not especially sharp.
When you stab an undead entity with the
knife, ask the GM whether it is driven by
obsession, hunger, and/or vengeance. If
the answer is “yes,” then deal +1d4 damage
(ignores armor), and leave the entity briefly
disoriented. Should this reduce the entity
to 0 HP, the dark ice knife melts away
along with whatever bound the entity to
this world.

Tulpa
A tulpa is a constructed spirit (page 356),
created by the powerful will of another. The
Rime Lords crafted many such entities,
which linger on in their creators’ wake.

324

A tulpa has the spirit and construct tags,
with a move that reflects them manifesting or possessing a physical form. They
are animated by more than biology (+4
HP) and usually lack vital organs (+3 HP,
+1 Armor). Pick or roll a purpose, size,
ability, tether, and disposition. Use
these elements to inform how it manifests
and behaves.

Some of the simplest tulpas to create,
each of these spirits is bound to a skeleton
that’s been stripped of flesh and intricately
carved. The Rime Lords and their disciples
used them as domestic servants, tending

when needed. Gingkara still attempt to
preserve their master’s homes, for what else
should they do with their existence?

These tulpas inhabit dark-ice sculptures
of three-eyed, stylized lions—muscular,
proud, fierce—standing atop decorated
pedestals. Always found in pairs, guarding
doorways or thresholds. They dutifully
challenge any who approach without an
invitation from their master. Never mind
that their masters are all long gone.
Some of the grander Rime Lord ruins
boast large khyagseng made of dark ice that
is strong as stone (+8 HP, +1 Armor, +1
damage). These greater guardians can also
unleash a thunderous roar, deafening and
terrifying, likely to trigger avalanches.

Rime Lords

Mkhalang
					Solitary, huge, spirit, construct,
			cautious, devious
HP 27; Armor 1 (lacks organs)
Damage trample d8+3 with disadvantage
(hand, close) or ice-tusks d8+7 with
disadvantage (reach, forceful, messy,
crude, 1 piercing)
Special qualities tethered to its dome but

to lie

			ä Fly quickly across the skies

The Rime Lords created these mighty spirits to act as emissaries and spies. A few still
remain, tethered to the meditation-halls
in which they were made but roaming
the Whitefangs in search of novelty and
diversion. They have grown bored in their
masters’ absence, and from boredom has
grown a subtle cruelty.
The people of the Whitefangs treat them
with respect, for they are still the emissaries of the Rime Lords. But they also know
better than to trust their counsel.

Shaksa
					Solitary, spirit, construct, hoarder,
magical, terrifying
HP 19; Armor 1 (lacks organs)
Damage antler of jagged bone d10+2
(close, messy, 1 piercing) or stone hooves
d8 (hand)
Special qualities tethered to its cemetery,
can only leave to track looters/vandals
Instinct to preserve the sanctity of the dead

			ä Manifest a form of bone and gravedirt (unharmed by stabbing; vulnerable to rain/running water)
			ä Track a looter or vandal, unerringly
The Rime Lords and their disciples created
shaksa to guard over cemeteries and charnel grounds. They usually watch, unseen
and unmanifest.
A shaksa manifests only within its cemetery, appearing as a powerful goat made of
rock and soil, with a human (?) skull for
a head, three eyes of floating light, and a
single great antler of jagged bone. Once
manifest, it can leave the cemetery in
pursuit of looters or vandals. Should this
form be destroyed, the spirit returns to the
cemetery and cannot remanifest until the
next sunset.

who enter; or fragile and holding
something dangerous in place
		Thoughtforms (page 319) composed
of dense particulate matter on the verge
of collapse or hiding some danger
Deadly cold
					Rime Lord ruins sometimes include
areas that are much, much colder than even
the surrounding mountains. Dangerously,
lethally cold.
ä		 Show a warning: a subtle change in
light/sound, breath or smoke condensing/freezing, hoarfrost
ä		 Weigh them down with frost
ä		 Have gear stick/freeze/break
ä		 Inflict hypothermia & frostbite (page
489), quickly and with little warning
328

Adept
					Solitary, cautious, cunning, magical
HP 16; Armor 2 (toughness, skill)
Damage freezing touch or aura d8 (hand
or reach, area, ignores armor)
Special qualities cold-proof; spirit sight;
no longer ages; needs little food/drink/
sleep; powerful will
Instinct see below
			ä Send their mind forth into the spirit
world, their bodies in stasis
			ä Reveal a teaching/technique of the
Rime Lords
			ä 1-4 additional moves (next page)
The Rime Lords taught that the self could
be perfected. That the needs and frailties of
the body could be purged. That the mind
could free itself of the flesh and know the
unseen world of spirits and fundamental
forces. That the will could conquer all. The
goal was nothing short of immortality, the
preservation of the perfect self.
For most of the Rime Lords’ mortal
disciples, these teachings did little more
than strengthen the body and clear the
mind. But for some—the adepts—these
teachings unlocked a fragment of their
masters’ power.
Rivalries were fierce among the adepts even
when the Rime Lords walked the land.
When the last Rime Lords disappeared,
these rivalries flourished into schisms, sects,
and violent purges. Some of the adepts fell.
Others went into hiding. Still others passed
their knowledge on, building schools and
monasteries around them.
To create an adept, start with the stats
above. Pick or roll for an instinct and 1-4
moves to represent their capabilities.

Some who practice the Rime Lord’s teachings push themselves beyond their limits.
Their bodies fail but they become ragefilled specters (page 79) or ever-hungry
wraiths (page 79). The Rime Lords had
many techniques for dealing with such
abominations.
Imagine, though, an adept who saw their
body and their life as a resource to expend
rather than a liability to overcome. One
who burned health and vitality to empower
their practice. One who saw vast, unused
potential in those around them. Such an
adept might deliberately sever their mind
and spirit from their body and gather a
herd of followers (victims) around them, to
serve as fuel for their mighty arts.

ä Calm/suppress emotions

ä Absorb intense heat/energy/
power/force unharmed

Rime Lords

Rime Lord
					Solitary, large, cautious, cunning, magical, organized,
terrifying
HP 26; Armor 5 (potent wards, skill)
Damage conjured ice d12+3 (any range, area, grabby,
			forceful) or heat-drain d12+1 (reach, ignores armor)
Special Qualities spirit sight; telepathic; cold-proof;
			 ageless; no need to eat/drink/sleep/breathe; aura of
			bitter cold
Instinct to seek perfection (in themselves, in their
			surroundings, in others)
			ä Overawe those in its presence
			ä Act simultaneously in both the
physical and spirit worlds
			ä Impose its mighty will on the world:
stilling emotions, freezing time, dismissing magic,
reshaping reality, etc.
			ä Create a tulpa or thoughtform
Only thirteen Rime Lords survive; each remains in stasis in
some key place of power. Their spirits are merged in an eternal dance and song, continuously weaving the dark ice glacier
that keeps the voice of Y’aaw’kara bound and prevents
madness from spreading unchecked across the world.
Pray, o mortal. Pray that they continue their vigil. Pray that
their wills do not falter. Pray that no interloper is so foolish
as to disturb them. Pray for your family,
your friends, and all that you know.
Pray that they do not wake.

330

331

The Ruined Tower
A long day’s march from Stonetop, a wide,
low hill rises from the Flats (page 126).
A pile of earth and stone squats atop that
hill. Metal spires reach out of the pile,
piercing the sky. If you squint, you can see
the rubble for what it is: a broken tower,
once grand in scale, long ago brought low.
Everyone from Stonetop knows about the
Ruined Tower and the giant-sized tunnels
beneath it. Adventuresome folks sometimes think to explore them. Most of the
time, they get spooked and don’t go far.
But those who’ve delved deeper have come
back with wondrous things. Or never come
back at all.

Stormcatcher’s tower
Before the tower fell, this was home to
Stormcatcher, last (?) of the Tempest Lords
(page 398). The tower was like the mast of
some mighty ship, catching wind and lightning, holding their power in the Storm Orb
(page 339), fueling her many magics.
For centuries, Stormcatcher’s tower stood
betwixt the realms of the other Makers
(page 264). They would trade with her,
and seek her council or her aid. But though
the Makers’ Roads (page 270) ran to her
very gate, she stood apart from them all.
Yet she did not live alone. A bustling
community grew up around her tower. Laborers, artisans, and scholars. Farmers and
domestic help. Soldiers, even. Generations
of families. Refugees seeking new homes.
Ambassadors and outcasts from the other
Makers. Seekers, misfits, and castaways
from distant lands.

332

When the Time of Cataclysm came—
when the Stone Lords’ (page 382) slaves
rebelled and unleashed the Things Below
(page 412), when the Rime Lords’ (page
316) disciples turned savage, when the
Forge Lords’ (page 156) cities were
buried by ash and war—Stormcatcher’s
tower endured. A bastion of civilization.
An island of safety. A refuge.
It could not last. Cultists of Og’molok
the Faceless Horde (page 349) swarmed
the ramparts, driving the defenders back.
Og’molok itself burst forth from below as a
wave of skittering horrors.
Stormcatcher unleashed a mighty spell that
turned the attackers into glass. But that
same spell shattered the Storm Orb. It laid
waste to the tower and broke a stretch of
the Makers’ Roads. It wrought Stormcatcher’s doom.
Many of the tower’s residents fled through a
﻿portal (page 306) beneath the tower. But
most were slaughtered by the attackers, or
﻿vitrified along with them, or caught in the
Storm Orb’s explosion. A slim few survivors
remained and tried to rebuild, but they soon
fell prey to the horrors now loose upon the
world.
In the centuries since, many have tried
to claim the ruin, or plunder its treasures
and its secrets. But all have failed, for one
reason or another.
And the Ruined Tower remains.

The Ruined Tower

Lore
Everyone from Stonetop knows:
		Where the tower is
		About the giant-sized tunnels below
		That some brave (foolish) folk have
explored them, and found
terrible and wondrous things

One might know:
		A tale or two about folks who’ve
explored here and what they found
		Details about the ruins above ground,
or some of the upper tunnels
		That the tower is a ruin of the
Tempest Lords
		That it was the last of the Makers’
strongholds to fall
		That pieces of the tower can be found
strewn all around the Flats
		That the outer ruins, the tower itself,
and the upper tunnels have all been
heavily looted, but still yield the
occasional trinket
Very few know:
		Stormcatcher’s story
		Her relationship with the
			other Makers
		How exactly the tower fell
		Reliable details about
			the depths
		Anything at all
			about Og’molok,
			the Faceless Horde

The Ruined Tower

Questions

Hooks

		 Which one of you has braved these
ruins before? What kept you from
going very far?
		 What wondrous trinket back in Stonetop supposedly came from here? Who
has it now, and what do they use it for?
		 What marvel is said to lie below the
Ruined Tower? From whom did you
hear such tales?
		 When the nightmares come and you
dream of the tower’s fall, are you one of
the doomed defenders? Or part of the
slavering horde?

		Bandits squat in the ruins, and find an
artifact that makes them extra dangerous.
		A sorcerer is raising her demonic child
in the tower’s depths.
		A drunk wanderer claims to have
explored the depths, but his friends
didn’t make it out.
		A southern scholar plans to delve the
tower and seeks local help.
		Frequent earthquakes coincide with
terrible nightmares, especially for those
touched by violence. The nightmares
feature the tower, its fall, or the
tunnels below.

The Ruined Tower

Getting there

Areas

To get there from Stonetop, take the West
Road 3-4 hours to the Crossroads (page
270). From there, it’s 5-6 hours southwest
across the Flats (page 126).

		Overgrown berm, about 15-20’ tall,
encircling the tower ~3/4 mile out.
Huge dressed stones poke out, similar
to the Old Wall (page 12).
		 Outer ruins between the berm and
the tower. Once a thriving town, now
just a jumble of ruins mostly swallowed
by the prairie.
		 A shallow crater, stretching ~400’ from
the tower’s northeast edge. Grass/soil/
mud/standing water cover glassy, slaglike bedrock.
		 The tower itself: ~300’ across. Northeast third is mostly rubble, but to the
south and west a few floors remain,
with walls ~200’ tall.
		 The upper tunnels start ~100’ below
the surface. Heavily looted, very
haunted, mostly maintained by a
ghostly Forge Lord and his patchwork
constructs (page 348).
		 The depths are even further down, a
warren of chambers, halls, stairs, chutes,
and burrows, all in various states of repair.
Full of infrastructure, artifacts, experiments, prisons, and vitrified horrors.

		 It’ll take at least a day each way, and a
corresponding amount of supplies
		 You’ll need to bring light sources (if
you intend to explore the tunnels)
		 You need to watch out for nosgalau
(page 272) and other dangers
(page 130) of the Flats
		 If there have been heavy snows, you
must wait until the thaw, or…
		 You’ll need snowshoes, and…
		 The way will still be grueling; you
risk exhausting yourselves and/or
your gear

The Ruined Tower

Aboveground
The Ruined Tower sits atop a wide, low,
lightly wooded hill. From a distance, it’s
just a landmark. As one gets closer, it
becomes clear just how massive the tower
once was.
Impressions
Anywhere
		Southerly wind, ever-blowing
		Squeak and shriek of metal spires when
the wind picks up
		Metallic barks and chirps from high
up, the snap of a metal cable
		The looming scale of the thing, big as
the landscape, hard to take in
		Smell of ozone, even on a clear day
		A fluting warble; a mocking laugh, a
flash of bright blue feathers
Outer ruins
		Piles of earth dotting the land, tufts of
grass and brush growing on them
		The uneven, rugged terrain, making it
so you can never catch your stride
		Occasional bits of broken pottery
or metal, centuries-old, just lying there,
turned up from the dirt
Inside a building or the tower
		Cave-like darkness, coolness, and
stillness as they step inside
		Faded mosaics/carvings, obscured by
time, vandalism, erosion, lichen
		Dim sunbeams, full of floating dust
		Wind whistling in through cracks in
walls/ceilings; water dripping through
those cracks in the rain

The Ruined Tower

The Tower itself
The base of the tower is ~300’ across;
rubble and piled-up soil spread out even
farther. The south and west sections still
stand tall, rising ~200’ from the hilltop.
Spires: Four metal spires support the tower’s walls, one at each cardinal direction:
massive, rusted, straining towards the sky.
The south and west spires each stretch
another 200’ or so above the walls (~400’
tall total). A length of cable dangles from
the tip of the south spire.
Rubble: The northeast third of the tower
is just rubble, thick walls, and soil piled
~30’ high, topped with grass and shrubs.
The rubble is riddled with trenches, pits,
old excavations, buried rooms, burrows.
Ground floor: The south and west thirds
of the tower are intact at ground level,
containing huge vaulted chambers (~40’
ceilings), including:
		A parlor with tall narrow windows.
Stylized friezes (faded, vandalized, full
of bird nests) show scenes of the
Tempest Lord (page 398) empire.
		The baths, a deep pool full of vile,
stagnant water. The walls are busted
open, the pipes looted.
		The great hall, mostly intact, with a
giant stone table. The ceiling is enchanted to show the night sky.
Human-scale servant passages/rooms
parallel the larger chambers and halls.

Inlays: Rooms inside the tower once had
aetherium inlays (page 341) all along
their walls and ceilings, but those were
gouged away long ago by looters and thunder drakes (page 135).
Main stairwell: A circular shaft at the
tower’s center, 30’ wide. Goes up to the second floor, down ~100’ to the upper tunnels.
Human-scale stairs spiral along the outer
wall, far too small for Makers. Did they fly?
Upper floors: The second floor is ~50’ up
and partly open to the sky, covering the
southwest half of the tower. Accessible via
the stairwell, holes in ceilings, or climbing
the outer walls. A few intact rooms remain,
where blue magpies (page 340) stash
shinies and build elaborate mosaics.
The third and fourth floors are another 40’
up each, accessible only via tricky climbs.
Unstable, with only a few “rooms,” all of
which are at least partly open to the air.
Magpie nests abound.
Creating rooms
For any specific room that you establish,
pick or roll for a purpose (previous page)
and combine it with a Tempest Lord
theme (page 399).
Imagine the room before the fall. Now,
how was it affected by the attack? By
the Storm Orb exploding? By centuries
of looting, vandalism, weathering, and
neglect? Who or what has lived here since?
What details remains to tell this story?
What discoveries (page 338) are waiting
to be found?

337

The Ruined Tower

The Ruined Tower

Shards of the Storm Orb
It was a filigree sphere of aetherium (page
404), maybe 50’ across. It floated near the
tower, a battery and a focus for tremendous
elemental power.
Stormcatcher’s final spell caused the Orb to
explode. Shards were flung across the Flats
(page 126), into the tower itself, and deep
into the soil—to someday be unearthed by
erosion, earthquakes, or burrowing beasts.
If the PCs encounter a shard, pick or roll
for its size and signs.

Vitrified remains
Stormcatcher’s last spell turned anyone it
touched into lumps of jagged glass. It was
directed at Og’molok (page 349) and the
skittering horrors (page 348), but many
of the attacking cultists were caught by the
spell as well. So were some of the tower’s
residents.
Above ground, vitrified remains are mostly
people (attackers or residents), with a few
domesticated animals. Most are jagged
lumps of black-grey glass, only vaguely
recognizable as what they once were, and/
or broken by time or vandalism. These are
covered in sharp edges (1d6 damage, messy,
1 piercing).
A few of these vitrified remains, though,
are perfect glass statues, the fear or frenzy
of their final moments frozen on their faces
(immobile, fragile, Value 3 to the right buyer).
Vitrification (page 408) can be reversed,
but it’s up to you to decide how.

Shards of the Storm Orb are unstable
aetherium (magical, dangerous). If damaged or
overloaded, they might explode or violently
discharge energy. But they are also etched
with arcane runes. Find a big enough piece, or
assemble enough smaller ones, and you might
unlock some of the Tempest Lords’ secrets.

The Ruined Tower

340

Blue magpie
Horde, tiny, devious, organized,
hoarder, magical
HP 1; Armor 1 (size)
Damage peck d4-2 w/disadvantage (hand)
Special qualities smart as a child (at least);
can speak Tempest Lord (understood
by all)
Instinct to amuse/impress its friends
			ä Pretend to be just a dumb bird
			ä Mimic sounds or voices
			ä Mock or trick someone
			ä Steal something
			ä Vanish with a hrummm and
reappear nearby (reload)
Like big crows with white-and-azure
plumage. Their songs are piping and musical, playful and complex, sprinkled with
actual voices and other unlikely sounds. A
few dozen of them nest in the tower and
the surrounding ruins. Smaller mischiefs
nest in the Flats.
Stormcatcher kept blue magpies as pets, and
their descendants have passed down much
oral lore. Sneak up on them, and you might
overhear magpies telling each other tales
that southern scholars would kill to know.
Magpies find humans boorish, but fun to
mess with, and they have such neat stuff.
They love shiny objects, especially glass
and fragments of larger things, and spend
years making elaborate mosaics or piecing
puzzles together.

The Ruined Tower

Belowground
Most of the rooms and halls below the
tower are spacious, vaulted to ~30’. Doorways are ~25’ tall and mostly ~10’ wide.
There are a few human-scale rooms, but
not many.
Impressions
Throughout
		A sense of smallness, of being
swallowed by the dark
		Walls and ceilings beyond the limit of
your light
		Distant sounds: dripping, rattling
clanging, moaning (?), footfalls, skittering
		The thought of all that stone and earth
above you
The upper tunnels
		Surprisingly little dust, mud, debris
		Paths worn smooth, as from the tread
of countless feet
		Faded gouges in flagstones, like heavy,
sharp things were dragged across them,
long long ago
		Carefully stacked rubble, plugging
holes in walls, blocking off passages
		The sense that you’re not alone, that
you’re being watched

Aetherium inlays
The four metal spires (page 337) extend
down through the bedrock. They run
through a number of underground rooms
and passages, as freestanding pillars or
embedded in walls.
Most rooms in and below the tower bear
aetherium (page 404) inlays, spreading
out from the metal spires. The inlays distribute power and anchor certain spells. Or
at least, they used to.
The aetherium has been stripped from
most of the upper tunnels, either carefully
salvaged by patchwork constructs (page
348) or gouged out by looters. More
inlays survive in the depths, but even there,
many have been marred by star-moles
(page 347) or the claws of skittering horrors (page 348), or burnt out and reduced
to crumbly chalk.
Where intact, inlays glow and pulse in response to winds above ground. When lightning strikes a spire, the aetherium flashes a
bright blue-white. Some inlays are stable,
but others are erratic and prone to discharge
(1d8 damage, forceful, maybe loud).

The depths
		Kicked-up dust, catching in your
throat, making eyes water
		The looming menace of a vitrified
horror, the skip in your heart before
you realize it’s just dead glass
		A thrum or whine, felt in your gut,
your teeth, the back of your eyes
		Wait, haven’t we been here before?

341

The Ruined Tower

Sites
		Central chamber at the base of the
main stairwell (page 337). Big, wellkept, drains in floor, multiple exits.
		Forge and nearby rooms, haunted by
Ferocedes (page 347), packed with
tools/parts and constructs in various
states of repair.
		Aetherium crucible, near the forge.
Dusty, unused, broken beyond
Ferocedes’s ability to fix with what’s
currently available to him.
		Barracks for soldiers who guarded
the Portal (next column). Haunted by
evacuees who hid and died there.
		Latrine near the barracks, with rattling,
moaning, clanging pipes and
still-working (!) showers.
		 Large chamber, out of the way, the
entrance blocked by piled stones.
Packed full of vitrified horrors by
Ferocedes’s constructs.
		 Frozen room, a chokepoint filled with
dark ice (page 322), encasing dozens
of skittering horrors (page 348) and
an Ustrina (page 464) who served as
Stormcatcher’s steward.
		Oubliette, a dead-end hall with
multiple cells/vaults. Each holds or held
a dangerous artifact or entity in stasis.
Some are torn open. The rest are guarded by wards, devices, constructs, magic.
		 Bottommost chamber, 400’ across,
partly flooded. Holds the feet of all
four spires, a massive toroidal coil,
hundreds of vitrified horrors (page
346), and the trapped manifestation
of Og’molok, the Faceless Horde
(page 349).

342

The Portal
In the upper tunnels, one can find a pair of
double-doors: locked and warded, barricaded from within, with countless gouges
clawed across their surface.
Beyond those doors: an austere room with
a dozen vitrified people, blown-out lightning sconces (page 346), a makerglass
window to the next room, and yet another
locked door.
Beyond that: a once-stately sitting room
(the furniture used for the barricade), a few
more vitrified evacuees. Another door, this
one ajar.
One last room. The far wall is a solid white
slab, etched with a magic circle. It’s a portal
(page 306), connected to those in the
Labyrinth (page 242), Three Coven Lake
(page 438), Vor Svetelik (page 468), and
so forth. It is fully functional, and can be
used via Opening the Way (page 517).
Layers of wards are etched in the floor, directed towards the portal, guarding against
things coming out.

Vitrified horrors
Skittering horrors (page 348),
fused into black, jagged glass by Stormcatcher’s last spell. There are more and
more as you go deeper. In some places, they
clog stairs or passages, making it difficult
to squeeze by without your gear or your
flesh getting shredded (1d4 to 1d8 damage,
messy, 1 piercing).
Lightning sconce
					Magical, construct, immobile
HP 1; Armor 4 (tiny, metal fittings, made
of crystal)
Damage lightning 1d6+2 (near, forceful,
reload, ignores armor)
A glowing gem, set in a fixture of aetherium. Found in pairs, or sets of up to six.
Each set is keyed to something (a pass
phrase, a hand signal, a color of robes, a
style of locket, etc.). Get too close without
the key, and zzzolt, they start blasting.

Big, naked mole-rats, like 5’ long, with
saggy skin, glowing freckles, and clusters of
wriggling feelers on their noses. They eat
vermin and fungus that grow near leaking
pipes. They also tunnel through bedrock,
collapse passages, and expose sealed-off
rooms.
Every so often, they wake a skittering
horror (page 348) from its torpor. This
spells doom for the colony, unless they can
trap it or cut off access to the nest. They’ll
treat the PCs as a similar threat.

Ferocedes Ogran, ghostly
Forge Lord
Solitary, large, spirit, undead, magical,
organized, obsessive, absentminded
HP 23; Armor 4 (metal, amorphous)
Damage pummel d12+3 (close, reach,
forceful) or barrage of metal d10+1
(near, area, messy, forceful, reload,
1 piercing)
Special qualities tethered to his forge, and
can’t venture far from it; speaks Tempest Lord (understood by all)
Instinct to preserve/restore the tower, to
ignore harsh truths
			ä Manifest a cloud of ember and ash, a
voice of crackling flame
			ä Ask what they’ve brought him, if
that one task is done yet, etc.
			ä Fail to understand their speech
			ä Grow annoyed, angry, violent
			ä Manipulate metal, telekinetically
			ä Repair metal, or shape it anew
			ä Animate a body formed of metal
tools, parts, and scrap
In life, he was a brilliant but awkward
Maker. Rivals sabotaged him, and he left
his people in shame. Stormcatcher took
him in, trusted him, gave him purpose—
and for that, Ferocedes was rabidly loyal.
Loyal unto death, and beyond.
He died without noticing it. He had no
time to treat the wounds he had suffered.
Stormcatcher needed his skills. Needed his
creations. Needed him.
So Ferocedes was already a ghost when
Stormcatcher cast her final spell. And in
the centuries since, he has fixated on his
task: to reclaim, rebuild, and repair, little by
little, with what tools he has, and preserve
the memory of his friend.

Patchwork construct

tremorsense

Ferocedes once had dozens of constructs
to defend and maintain the tower. But the
years have not been kind. Only ten or so
remain, and one or two are usually offline
for modifications or repairs.
Each one has an aetherium core (magical, fragile, Value 2 to the right buyer).
Ferocedes can repair minor damage to a
core, and/or install it in a new body, but he
can’t make new ones without the aetherium crucible (page 405).
Each construct is generally humanoid,
though most lack a “head” and all are
made of mismatched parts. Pick or roll a
current task for each. Consider picking or
rolling for an attribute (page 167) and
adjusting its stat block accordingly.

The Ruined Tower

Og’molok, the Faceless Horde
					Threat (magical entity)
Instinct to revel in slaughter, pain,
and terror
Themes cruelty/torture/violence and
destruction/chaos/ruin
			ä Send terrible dreams/visions to those
touched by violence
			ä Urge bloodshed, cruelty, rage
			ä Shake the earth in a fit
In the bottommost chamber (page 342)
of the Ruined Tower is a cone of jagged
black glass, a pile of vitrified horrors that
reaches almost to the ceiling. This is where
Og’molok burst into the world as a fountain of blood, coalescing into a skittering
swarm. This is where it met its doom, fused
into a pillar of red crystal (page 424),
encased in a pile of slag.

6 feet long, with segmented bodies and too
many legs. Scythe-like forelimbs. Feathery
antennae that caress the air and quiver when
they sense prey. No eyes, just red crystal
pulsing within its skull. No mouth, for it
never eats and lives on hate and malice.
Each is a manifestation of Og’molok’s
cruel will. Born of blood, given shape, sent
skittering up to slaughter. And slaughter
they did, until Stormcatcher’s final spell
turned them all into jagged lumps of glass.

Og’molok, of course, is not truly dead. Just
bound, neutered, diminished. It still corrupts the lowest levels beneath the tower,
still calls to those who might hear. It is
trapped, and it cannot escape. Not yet. Not
without help.
l		 Someone sets out to free it
l		 They learn how to release it from its

vitrified state (see page 408)

l		 They acquire what they need
l		 They begin the process
l		 Impending doom: Og’molok and its

swarm are loosed upon the world

Or, almost all of them. A few dozen
avoided the vitrifying spell. They linger on,
in a state of torpor until they sense a living,
feeling being. Then they wake. Then they
hunt. Then they slaughter once more.
349

South Manmarch
If you descend from the Steplands (page
372) to the southeast, you’ll find yourself
on a rolling prairie. The ground slopes
gently down toward the east, to the banks
of the Dread River (page 86).
Travelers and merchants aplenty cross
these plains. They come north, from Lygos
and the civilized south (page 256), heading north to the Highway (page 270) and
from there to Marshedge (page 274).
Or they head the other way, bearing herbs
and textiles, metals and gems, back to the
southern cities. Or they come from beyond
the Highway, from the North Manmarch
(page 282), to hunt or trade or raid.
But for all those who pass through the
South Manmarch, few call it home. Sure,
the Hillfolk (page 228) graze their herds
here in the warmer months. And some
new settlement is always popping up. But
the land is dotted with burnt-out homesteads and ruined forts: a mute testament
to those who have tried to settle here
before. Tried, and failed.

Impressions
Always
		The eternal, unresponsive sky
		 Shallow, rocky soil broken by outcrops
			 of white limestone and gray shale
Smoke from a lone homestead, overlooking a watercourse
		 A stream or dry gully, winding lazily
down toward the Dread River
		 An abandoned, collapsed longhouse
		 A Hillfolk horseband, watching from
higher ground
Spring
		A mottled carpet of new grass and
wildflowers—yellow, pale green, lilac
		 Pocket wetlands, home to nesting
ducks recently returned from the south
		 A great cloud of birds, bursting forth
from the brush
Summer
		Squinting into a blinding sky
		 The fervid and unrelenting chorus of
summer insects, shifting in tone and
rhythm with time of day
		 Tallgrass in full flush, waist-high and
rippling in a southerly wind.
		 A mob of white-tailed deer or mighty
elk, grazing on a long slope
Autumn
		Grassheads gone rust-colored, stalks
still green
		 A streamside stand of cottonwoods,
			 glowing gold in the dusk light
Winter
		Days of scouring, southwesterly wind
		 Powdery snowdrifts, stretching leeward
from ripples in the landscape
		 Patches of dead grass, showing orange
through the snow

350

South Manmarch

A long-disputed land

The Flintwash

Before the Cataclysm (page 412) the Dread
River formed the eastern border of Stone
Lord (page 382) territory. After the Stone
Lords fell, the Barrow Builders (page 34)
fought each other back and forth across this
expanse, grinding themselves down, like
shards of shale abraded to dust over time.

Draining out of the Steplands and into the
Dread River, this watercourse draws
thickets, copses of cottonwood, wildlife,
and wanderers to its gentle banks. The
Hillfolk call it Maengwalc in their own
tongue—“Flintwash,” after the stony
plateau from which its headwaters spring.

Now, the South Manmarch is the border
between the civilized south and the hinterlands of the World’s End. It’s a lawless
place, full of dangerous beasts and even
more dangerous people. Nomadic Hillfolk
claim much of the region, and bandits
(page 355) are a constant threat.

Hundreds of rills and brooks feed the
Flintwash, and in its lower reaches, it’s
quite broad and difficult to cross. Yet ways
do exist. How might a traveler get from
one side to the other? Ask a PC who
would know, but perhaps there is…

And yet, the lure of the prairie is great.
Every year or so, someone tries to carve out
a piece of for themselves. A new settlement
might be:
		 A family from Marshedge or the North
Manmarch, starting their own farm
		 An ambitious Lygosi merchant, investing
in a new trading post or caravansary
		 Some would-be chief or warlord from
the Manmarchers (page 282), building a new hamlet or hillfort
		The conquering armies of the South
(page 262), establishing a foothold

… an old bridge from the time of
the Makers (page 264);
… a newer bridge, built at great expense
by some southern merchant or noble;
… a shallow ford; wide, rocky and
usually safe;
… a ferry, run by enterprising souls;
… some combination of the above; or
… something else entirely.

Such settlements rarely last. They fall prey
to natural disaster or roving beasts, to rivals
or bandits, or to the certain wrath of the
Hillfolk who claim these lands as theirs.
Or perhaps, if you believe the stories,
there’s something darker afoot. Perhaps
these lands really are haunted. Haunted by
the numerous souls who’ve died here. Or
by foul things, slithering out of the Dread
River. Perhaps the land is cursed, and no
settlement can ever survive. Perhaps.
351

South Manmarch

Questions
		 Who did you know who tried to settle
in the South Manmarch? What fate
befell them?
		 What bandit crew is currently the most
feared? Who leads them, and what
makes them so dangerous?
		 At what landmark do merchant caravans typically encamp? Why do they
stop there?
		 What landmark does everyone who
travels these lands know to avoid?
Why?

the Highway

gets waylaid by bandits
		 A ruin in the region becomes a place of
pilgrimage, drawing the faithful of
Helior/Aratis from all over

Horned drake
Solitary, huge, hardy
HP 24; Armor 3 (stony hide)
Damage trampling charge d10+5 (hand,
area, forceful) or horn gore d10+5 (close,
forceful, messy, 1 piercing)
Instinct to protect their young
ä Issue a honking call
ä Stand its ground, still as a rock
ä Lurch suddenly into a charge
ä Absorb raw elemental power
		 (regaining 1d8 HP)
The behemoth of the southern grasslands
is striking for its great size, the quartz-like
plates of its segmented hide, and its fearsome
horns. The horns vary greatly from beast to
beast, ranging from a single spike on its
snout or forehead to a rack of five or six
curved points edging the flanges of its massive
skull. An intimidating beast, even at rest.
To the Hillfolk, the downing of a d’lkornek
(“thorn-dragon”) is a hero’s feat, to be followed by a week of harvest and celebration
at the kill site.
Something interesting: Some scholars
hold that the Stone Lords imbued horned
drakes with elemental qualities. Specimens
of their hide are commonly mistaken for
dolostone or some other mineral, and
Hillfolk prize their horns (Value 3).
Something useful: The horns of a
d’lkornek act like power-crystals (page
389), but larger and with great capacity.
They are extremely dangerous to harvest;
a wise butcher slowly bleeds away their
energy over the course of days.
Adult horned drakes might be accompanied
by their young (group, large, hardy; HP 14;
Damage d8+3; Instinct to get curious, to
play fight), whom they aggressively defend.

South Manmarch

				 Horde, stealthy, cautious, devious

			sling d6 (near, awkward, reload)

The Makers’ Roads (page 270) limit banditry throughout most the World’s End.
Not so in the South Manmarch. Here,
merchants travel without the safety of
warded pavestones. And those merchants
draw bandits like dung draws flies. They’re
motley crews, these bandits. Rogues from
Marshedge. Deserters from the armies of
North and South. Exiled Hillfolk. Scoundrels. Miscreants. Anyone who’s willing to
get their hands red, really.

355

Spirits of the wild
The physical world—this mortal realm of
base matter and flesh—is but one reality.
There is another, hidden reality. Arguably a
truer reality. Here, spirits dwell.
The spirit realm is filled with entities,
especially those of the natural world.
Each beast, each tree, each gust of wind or
flickering flame—they all have spirits. So
do the soil, the rocks, the mountains and
the streams.
Most of these spirits are quiet,
slumbering or barely conscious or
operating on a scale so alien as to
be impenetrable to human minds.
But some mortals can speak to
them nonetheless, and goad
them into action. Sometimes,
these sleepers shift and mumble
and stretch of their own accord.
The most active spirits are
those tethered to physical
objects, or those associated
with beasts or other “animate”
things—things full of life and
energy. These are the spirits
that mortals encounter
most often.

356

Spirits of the Wild

Themes

Hooks

Choose or roll 1, or 2 and combine.

		A villager (or visitor) has been cursed
by a powerful elemental spirit, maybe
in a way that puts others in danger.
		 A child hears voices and sees spirits;
maybe they’re crippled with fear, or
maybe they recklessly ask spirits for
petty favors.
		 Something that the PCs want can
be found in in a sacred site, one
that’s known to house unfriendly,
powerful spirits.
		 Someone has unwittingly brought a
spirit’s tether into the village, and it
starts wreaking havoc.
		 A PC learns that a particular spirit
knows an important secret, something
they desperately want to learn.

theme

357

Spirits of the Wild

Regarding spirits
The following apply not just to spirits of
the wild, but also to ﻿ghosts (page 76),
dool spirits (page 83), some ﻿primordial
powers (page 298), the ﻿Things Below
(page 412), and little gods (page 182).

But a tether is not just a boon; it’s also a
vulnerability. Harming the tether harms
the spirit. Destroying the tether destroys
the spirit—or at least weakens, diminishes, and greatly changes it.

Manifestation
Spirits lack physical form, existing in the
realm of thought and symbol. They can
perceive and influence the mortal realm in
subtle ways, but to directly affect it, a spirit
must manifest or possess a physical form.

Some tethers don’t just anchor their
spirits: they bind and imprison them.
A spirit so bound can’t leave its tether’s
presence. The most powerful bindings
seal their spirits away, greatly restricting
their perceptions and ability to influence
the world. But no binding is perfect, and
a spirit so bound might tempt or trick
others into releasing it.

Each spirit manifests differently. Some
shape a body from crude matter. Others
appear as intangibles: cold, shadow,
moonlight, etc. Some can only “ride”
living beings. Others can animate beings
that were once alive, or vessels specifically prepared to hold them. A rare few can
manifest bodies of pure spirit-stuff.
Spirits manifest or possess forms that
make symbolic sense. A fire spirit won’t
possess water; it might manifest as a
flame, or animate a cloud of cinders, or
possess a hot-headed youth.
Tethers
Some spirits are bound to a thing in the
physical world. The tether might occur
naturally (like a brennauwed and its tree),
by choice or mutual benefit (like an altar
where mortals leave offerings for the
spirt), or via trickery, force, or coercion.
A tether anchors and empowers its
spirit, granting it a stronger connection
to the physical world. If harmed or
weakened, the spirit can retreat to its
tether to rejuvenate.
358

Fighting Spirits
One can fight a spirit only when it manifests, and even then they can be difficult
to harm. Manifest forms often have lots
of HP and might be vulnerable to only
certain materials or attacks.
Even if one can harm a manifestation,
they’re not really harming the spirit.
Reducing it to 0 HP ends the manifestation. This weakens the spirit, forcing it to
retreat to its tether (if it has one). But it
doesn’t truly harm it.
To truly harm a spirit, one must use potent magic, damage or destroy its tether,
or fight it directly in the spirit realm. A
spirit reduced to 0 HP in one of these
ways is actually destroyed—or at least so
thoroughly diminished that it won’t be a
problem for many human lifetimes.
You might want to track a spirit’s “manifest” HP vs. “true” HP. If the PCs hurt a
manifest spirit with something that could
truly harm it, reduce both sets of HP.

Spirits of the Wild

Communion
One might wish to commune with a
spirit to seek knowledge or request aid.
The Blessed in particular is able to sense
active spirits, speak with them, and force
even dormant spirits to manifest and hear
them out. Some NPCs and monsters have
similar powers.
Most people, though, are unaware of the
spirits around them. Spirits of the wild
tend to ignore individual humans. The best
way for most mortals to commune with
a spirit is to enter a sacred site (carefully,
respectfully) and make an offering that
gets the spirit’s attention. One could also
intentionally provoke the spirit—getting it
to manifest—but that rarely ends well.
One might also seek out a spirit in its own
realm. This requires shifting one’s consciousness to the spirit realm, which might
involve…
… meditation;
… imbibing drugs/alcohol;
… exhaustion or deprivation;
… percussion, music, dancing;
… a guide or teacher; and/or
… a magic item or spell.

Once a mortal’s consciousness is in the
spirit realm, they must find the spirit(s) in
question, get their attention, and convince
it to help. None of these are easy. None
of these are safe. If a PC attempts such
a journey, Chart a Course with them
through the spirit world. Good requirements and challenges include…
… they need a knowledgeable guide or
detailed instructions;
… they risk getting lost;
… the way will be grueling, they risk
exhaustion; and/or
… they must watch out for ﻿specters (page
79), wraiths (page 79), or other ﻿ghosts
(page 76); dool spirits (page 83);
fundamentals (page 312); ﻿voidblights
(page 313); spirits corrupted by the
﻿Things Below (page 432); or just
elemental spirits that are territorial, or
violent, or powerful and careless.
Depending on the spirit that they seek,
they might also need to surmount/brave/
cross a hazardous stretch of the spirit realm
and/or risk drawing the attention of certain spirits. For example, if they sought out
the spirit of the Golden Oak (page 186),
you might tell them...
… they risk drawing the attention of the
crossing’s guardian; and/or
… they need to brave the secret crossing
itself, which borders the Last Door and
the realm of the dead.

359

Spirits of the Wild

Beast warden
					Solitary, spirit, beast-like, protective
Instinct to see its wards thrive, prosper
Notes A different spirit watching over
			 each type of beast.

Elemental spirits
These spirits mostly just do their thing,
with little concern for humanity unless
some mortal offends them or seeks them
out for aid. An elemental spirit can:
ä		 Reveal what it knows, from its
			own perspective
ä		 Spread word to other spirits
ä		 Bestow a blessing or curse, tied to
			its element
ä		 Shift, move, or direct that which falls
			in its domain
Elemental spirits rarely manifest. When
they do, they often possess or animate the
element itself, or appear as ghostly figures
somehow reflecting their element. They’re
more likely to be NPCs than monsters.
Here are examples; feel free to modify
and expand on them. Maybe you picture
cave spirits differently than this. Maybe a
decorated limestone cave has different tags,
instincts, and notes than a cave-bear’s den.
Or maybe this particular cave’s spirit is
different than other cave spirits. Consider
asking a character (who would know) what
certain types of spirits are usually like, and
use what they give you.

relative power.

Spirits of the Wild

The PCs are in the Steplands, heading towards Three Coven Lake, and they’ve just run
afoul of some Hillfolk. They escaped, but only because the Hillfolk turned back from the cave
they hid in, like they were scared to enter. I think this is a sacred site. I roll a 3 for theme
(air/wind/weather/fire) and a 4 for the marker (place that resonates with the theme). I’m
picturing one of those eternal flames, where a methane pocket seeps out and burns forever. I
get 10 for activity (alert, aware, manifesting freely) and roll a 12 for disposition (content,
pleasant, helpful, beneficent) which is odd given the Hillfolk’s reaction.
So: a cave lit by a flame with no apparent source, issuing from a crack in the earth. The
flame houses an old, powerful, talkative spirit that’s been burning since the days of the
Makers. It blames the Hillfolk for their ancestors’ role in
calling up the Things Below and toppling
the Stone Lords, so it’s hostile
towards them. It’ll be right
helpful to the Blessed
and her friends!

Artifacts
Length of braided leather
					magical
3 feet long, frayed on one end, and crusty
with flaking... something. The braids
entwine 2d4 beads of redwood. Each
bead contains some harm: a wound (1d10
damage, messy, ignores armor), a sickness, a
poison, a delusion. The whole thing binds a
spirit of blight.
When you unweave the braid and let a
redwood bead drop upon the earth, the spirit
manifests as a warm, damp wind. Name
someone in your presence; the spirit inflicts
upon them whatever harm was stored in
the bead. If you do not name someone in
your presence, the spirit will choose someone at random (maybe even you).
A tightly-woven net
					close, thrown, magical, crude
Woven from Flats-grass by a Hillfolk
sorcerer or witch. Hundreds of minor
root-spirits are tethered to the strands.
When you cast the net over a spirit, it is
ensnared—even if its form is intangible
or could flow through the net. The spirit
can’t escape on its own as long as the net is
intact. Don’t try to use this on fire spirits—
they’ll burn right through it.
A fur-lined bedroll
					 , magical
This bedroll is decorated with Forest Folk
beadwork and lined with the softest rabbit
fur. It tethers a nurturing, protective spirit,
one that is quiet and comforting.
When you Make Camp in the fur-lined
bedroll, you regain 1d6 (extra) HP and gain
advantage on your next roll.
364

A stone cup
					magical
A lump of limestone, big as a large man’s
fist, sheared off smooth at one end and
hollowed out. Water slowly seeps from the
interior wall, filling the cup in about half
an hour. The water is hard, mineral-rich
but potable, and just a little cursed.
When you drink water produced by the cup,
you’ll have troubling dreams the next time
you sleep—dreams of suffocation and endless wet caverns and huge uncaring things
stirring in the lightless depths. Roll 1d6:
on a 1, you wake up screaming and will get
no rest this evening; on a 2-6, the dreams
are merely unsettling.
A cracked femur
					 , magical
This leg bone is always cold and covered in
rime. It’s the bone of a witch who broke her
leg and froze to death. It houses not a ghost,
but a potent spirit of winter and cold.
When you first grip the bone, roll +CON: on
a 10+, the spirit acknowledges you as master
and you can wield the bone; on a 7-9, pick 1:
		 Drop the bone; you can never wield it
		 Endure as the spirit sucks away your
heat—take 2d4 damage (ignores armor)
and mark weakened—but you then
master the spirit and can wield the bone.
On a 6-, the spirit sucks away your heat—take
2d4 damage (ignores armor) and mark weakened—and you can never wield the bone.
When you wield the bone as a weapon
(hand, crude, magical, ignores armor) it deals
2d4 damage instead of your normal damage,
sucking the warmth from your foe.

A crown of flowers
					fragile, magical
A wreath of living vines, sprouting flowers
of great variety and beauty. A woman who
dons the crown becomes greatly attuned to
the fertility of everything around her, and the
whispering spirits of growing things. She also
becomes aware of the rites of spring.

A hunk of amber
					 , magical, Value 2
It’s about the size of two fists, with a mass
of blood-sucking insects floating in its
center. It tethers an ambitious spirit of insects, blight, and disease. Treat it as a threat
(type: magical entity, Instinct to foster
worship) with the following moves:

When a woman wears the crown and
performs the rites of spring—dancing
around an oak tree, making offerings to the
spirits, and joining with her lover in the
light of the full moon—then her steading
rolls with advantage to generate Surplus
in both summer and autumn. Also, if the
woman wearing the crown so chooses,
she will conceive and bear a healthy child,
regardless of any barriers.

ä		 Demand a blood sacrifice, poured over
			the amber
ä		 Shield someone from sickness, vermin,
			and poison
ä		 Blight an enemy with sickness, vermin,
			or both

A forked stick of redwood
					 , magical
A stout stick about 2 feet long, the bark removed, with fine woodworm trails making
entirely-too-regular patterns. If you hold
it gently, with a hand on each tine of the
fork, you can feel it tugging, pulling you
gently in certain directions.
When you dowse with the redwood stick,
it will guide you to the nearest site sacred
to the spirits of the wild. With practice,
you can estimate the distance and even the
disposition of the spirits that dwell there.

Spirits of the Wild

The Brennauwed
The Tall Ladies, Daughters-of-Seed, the
Slow Sisters: these are all titles given to the
Brennauwed, powerful tree spirits and, if
you believe them, kin to Danu (page 64)
herself. Each is tethered to a single tree.
The younger ones can roam, but the older
a Brennauwed is, the more bound she is to
her tree.

Glasbren
					Solitary, spirit, clever, magical, stealthy,
			beautiful
HP 19; Armor 2 (bark, no vital organs)
Damage raking fingers d8 (hand,
			disadvantage)
Special qualities tethered to its tree
Instinct to stretch and grow and
			experience life
			ä Travel freely between young trees
			ä Spy on someone or something
					interesting
			ä Manifest a beautiful form of moving
					wood (unharmed by stabbing,
					vulnerable to fire)
			ä Call on the forest and its creatures
					for aid
			ä Spread word through the Great Wood
Youngest of the Brennauwed, a glasbren’s
tree is barely more than a sapling. The
world is full of wonder and life, and they
would see it all.
From time to time, a comely mortal will
catch a glasbren’s eye. If the mortal proves
kind and curious and full of life, it might
reveal itself and shyly pursue a friendship—or more.
When you give your heart or loyalty to a
glasbren, your instinct becomes “Devotion:
act in the glasbren’s interests.” The glasbren
becomes a follower (Cost: wonder and
excitement; holds 2 Loyalty to start).
When a season passes without you spending
time with the glasbren, alone at her tree,
she loses all Loyalty and you mark miserable. You cannot clear this debility without
spending time with her.

Spirits of the Wild

		 Days or weeks pass without either of

Loyalty she held and she isn’t speaking

Birdwaig
					Solitary, large, spirit, cautious, clever,
			magical
HP 23; Armor 3 (woody, no vital organs)
Damage crushing branches d10+3 (close,
reach, forceful), choking vines/roots
d10+1 (reach, grabby)
Special qualities tethered to its tree
Instinct to care for the forest, especially
			the trees

Hennain
					Solitary, huge, spirit, cautious, clever,
			magical, ancient, terrifying
HP 31; Armor 4 (woody, no vital organs)
Damage walloping branches d12+5 (close,
			reach, area, forceful)
Special qualities tethered to its tree
Instinct to take the long, long view
			ä Remain unmoved
			ä See through any tree in the forest
			ä Animate her own tree (almost
impossible to harm with weapons, or
even fire)
			ä Call upon the power of Danu herself
			ä Lay a lasting curse on a defiler
Old grandmothers of the Wood, the hennain are few and ancient and mighty. They
watch, and they wait, and they endure. It
takes a dire threat indeed to rouse one, and
even then it acts in slow and inscrutable
ways. For a hennain cares not one bit for
any given creature or tree; she cares only
for the slow turn of the forest itself.

As they mature, Brennauwed grow more
sedentary, manifesting only via their own
tree or possessing other trees within a few
miles. A birdwaig has a motherly regard
for all natural things in her territory, and
will turn the very forest against any that
seek to do it harm.

Dangers
Andalau
					Horde, tiny, spirit, stealthy, mischievous
HP 8; Armor 2 (tiny, agile)
Damage none
Instinct to play and frolic
			ä Manifest as a fluttering gust of wind
					 (harmed only by lead or salt)
			ä Flit things about (dust, leaves, etc.)
			ä Annoy or spook someone
			ä Deliver a whispery message
There are places where the wind dances
constantly, fluttering and flitting, and here
the andalau dwell. They are more prone
to mischief than violence. Win their
favor, and you might convince them to
help you—especially if it involves going
somewhere new or playing pranks. Just
don’t be surprised when their attention flits
elsewhere.

Ceffylwraig
					Solitary, large, spirit, swift, tireless,
			cautious, magical, beautiful
HP 24; Armor 0
Damage kicking hoofs d8+3 (hand, close,
			forceful, disadvantage)
Instinct to be fiercely independent
			ä Appear as a majestic horse among
					the herd
			ä Slip free of any restraint, disappearing
					 into the wind if need be
			ä Guide the herd like it was a single,
					living entity
			ä Curse someone with wild, unbridled
					emotions
A herd of wild horses has a life, nay, a spirit
all its own, a spirit as free and fickle as the
wind. Take care, you who would poach
wild horses for your own purpose, lest you
find your heart turned against you!
Something interesting: “Ceffylwraig”
means “horse wife,” but she sees herself
more as queen than wife: proud and
haughty, disdainful of the weak and the
slow, the herd her subjects more than her
children.
Something useful: A ceffylwraig is
scornful and suspicious of praise or fancy
words, but can be appeased by thoughtful
gifts, offered with appropriate respect from
proud women of noble bearing.
		 What sort of gift do the ceffylwraig
			most value?
		 What sort of gift will do
			in a pinch?
