			ä Cause vicious thorns and spines to
					sprout from nearby plants
			ä Dig in deep, make them bleed (messy)
Each thorn on a plant is a sword, brandished boldly against a world that would
defile its flowers, steal its fruit, and devour
its delicate buds. What, then, are the coedwaig? Only Danu’s swords, brandished by
the Mother herself in defense of her most
beautiful and vulnerable children.

Fadyn
					Solitary, spirit, magical, stealthy,
			devious, audacious, cautious, beautiful
HP 16; Armor 1 (reflexes)
Damage bite, scratches, or knife d6 (hand,
			disadvantage)
Special qualities tethered to a fox skull
Instinct to make mischief
			ä Manifest as a fox (small) or a
					charming stranger
			ä Be all manner of charming and
					ingratiating
			ä Steal something when no one’s looking
			ä Make something seem like
					something else
Every child has heard stories of the
fox-spirit, the trickster, the fadyn. In some
tales, a fadyn makes fools of the cruel or the
proud. In others, they steal chickens from
the slow-witted. And in still others, they seduce a beautiful human, disappearing when
bored or discovered. Truth be told, there’s
little pattern to who a fadyn torments or
fancies. They are fickle, fickle things.
Something interesting: When a fadyn
takes the form of a person, it always has
some fox-like feature (a tail, feet, hands,
ears) that it strives to hide. It is easily
embarrassed by this feature, and flees if it’s
discovered.
Something useful: Fadyn are in fact
the spirits of old, cagey foxes who
passed away, and they’re tethered
to their old skulls. One can’t travel
far from its skull, and often carries
it hidden on its manifest person
or buries it near the community
that it torments. If you want to
destroy a fadyn, or bind it to your
service, getting your hands on its
skull is the best way to do so.
369

Spirits of the Wild

Grwgnach
					Solitary, huge, spirit, magical,
			mesmerizing
HP 27; Armor 2 (reflexes, swarm)
Damage whirlwind of pecks, claws, buffets
			d8+3 (hand, area, disadvantage)
Instinct to absorb individual identity
ä Manifest in a murmuration of starlings
					(nigh impossible to harm with
					personal weapons)
ä Strip a swath of land clean of seed,
					saplings, insects
ä Overwhelm with sound and the
					weight of numbers
When the sun sets, starlings flock in
absurd numbers: thousands upon thousands dancing and wheeling in the sky, like
a chittering thunderhead, like a cloud of
living smoke.
Farmers do what they can to keep starlings
far from town, and not just because they eat
precious seeds and bury the land in droppings. They do so for fear of the grwgnach.
370

The grwgnach is a spirit of the mob, of
the many coming together to become
something more. It’s not a king or a queen
of the starlings. Rather, it’s what a million
starlings become when each bird loses
itself to the flock.
When you gaze rapt upon a murmuration
of starlings, tell us of a time when you felt
small and insignificant, or when you lost
yourself in a crowd.
Then, roll +WIS: on a 10+, you feel the
pull of the grwgnach but shake it off; on
a 7-9, your mind is briefly adrift among a
thousand-thousand others—mark dazed
but also ask the GM one question about
the region, something that at least one
starling might know; on a 6-, your mind
is sucked into the grwgnach—mark dazed
and you stand rapt until the flock disperses
or someone removes you, physically. Also,
until you Level Up, your instinct becomes
“Birdlike: To act strangely, like a bird.”

Spirits of the Wild

The Halfeyd
					Spirit, magical, fickle, sadistic, terrifying
Instinct to terrorize its prey
ä Manifest as eyes in the dark, watching
ä Mark someone as prey
ä Set all manner of hunting beasts on
					their trail
ä Put ambush predators in their path
The hunters and trappers of Stonetop know.
So do the Hillfolk. The Manmarchers know,
and Fen-walkers, too. When you make a
kill, you leave an offering. An offering for
the Halfeyd. Fail to do so, and you might
find yourself as prey—for everything.
Something interesting: If the Halfeyd
marks you, you'll be hounded for 3 days
and 3 nights—even by small predators who
would never normally hunt you. Something
useful: A spirit of running water (like that of
a stream) can wash away the Halfeyd’s mark.
Nerth serpent
					Solitary, large, spirit, fearless
HP 20; Armor 4 (resilience), or 0 against
			bare hands
Damage bite and constrict d10+3 (hand,
			close, reach, forceful, grabby)
Special qualities tethered to the Nerth Tree
Instinct to test the might of supplicants
ä Politely offer a chance to back down
ä Restrain an opponent’s limb
ä Whisssper a foe’s own doubts in
					their ear
The Nerth Tree grows in the Great Wood.
It can only be found by one mortal each
midsummer, by following a particular path
in the woods (page 516). The roots are
littered with bones of former supplicants.
Hanging from the tree: a great serpent.
Crush it and eats its heart, and you will
gain its vitality. But good luck with that.

Prenysbyrd
					Solitary, large, spirit, magical,
			stubborn, terrifying
HP 23; Armor 2 (woody, lacks organs)
Damage club-like fists d10+3 (close, forceful)
Instinct to punish trespassers
ä Blame one for the sins of another
ä Fill minds with leering images and
					unreasoning dread
ä Form a body of fallen trees, leaves,
					rotting wood (vulnerable to fire)
ä Smother a prone foe with soil, leaves,
					wood (d8+1 damage, near, grabby)
What becomes of a dead tree’s spirit? Most
linger as simple log-spirits, weakening as
the log rots to mulch. But when generations
of trees grow and die in the same place, they
might become something more. Something
that defends the grove, and its current trees,
and all the trees that are yet to come.
Troelloff
					Spirit, magical, playful, cruel
Instinct to confuse, frustrate, delay
ä Show warnings left by others: spiral
					carvings, many-arrowed icons, etc.
ä Inflict a brief, intense vertigo
ä Make them lose their way
ä Show a landmark they’ve passed before
ä Obscure trails, way-signs, and markings
Some places never want you to leave: deep
woods, twisting caverns, rugged hills that
all look same. Such places give rise to troelloff, maze-spirits, who delight in mischief
and feed on the desperation of the lost.
Something interesting: Where a troelloff
dwells, there's always a true path. It can't
harass anyone who stays on this path, but
good luck finding it. Something useful: The
path is never obvious, but it’s always marked,
by a particular flower, a pattern in the moss, a
vein in the stone, etc.
371

The Steplands
The Steplands are a rugged hill-country
south of the Flats (page 126). To the
north, they touch Ferrier’s Fen (page
116) and the Great Wood (page 200).
To the east, they give way to the South
Manmarch (page 350). To the west, they
climb higher and higher, like a stairway to
the Huffel Peaks (page 236).
The region is a karst—a plateau of limestone and quartzite and granite, carved
away by ages of runoff and dissolved by
groundwater, leaving a jumble of hills and
valleys and rocky cliffs, all shot through
with caves, sinkholes, and streams.
The land seems wild and untamed to outsiders, but in truth it is home to dozens of
small nomadic bands, the Hillfolk
(page 228).

Impressions
Always
		Bare rock, host to lichen, moss, and
stubborn grass/shrubs
		 Cloud-shadows drifting over hillsides
		 The feeling of being surrounded, closed
in by the valleys
		 The burning in your legs as you climb
another damn hill
		 The grandeur when you reach a
summit: vista on vista marching, hills
on hills, slope beyond slope

372

Spring
		Snow still clinging to south-facing
slopes, never touched by sun
		 Sudden warmth as you step out of a
shadow; chill as you step into one
		 Gurgle of water downhill, through
ancient culverts under the Highway
Summer
		Emerald meadows, grass waving in
wind, splashes of wildflower color
		 Flutter of birds taking flight, surprised
when you round a bend
		 Blessed shade as you pass beneath
some trees or into the shadow of a hill
Autumn
		Fog in the valleys, the hills like islands
in clouds
		 Tickle on your face, from mist or an
early snow
		 Mournful songs of cranes or owls or
other large birds, echoing from far away
Winter
		White drifts half-covering the hills,
rocks still poking through
		 Bitter winds on your face as you crest a
hill or trudge through a pass
		 The breath of your companions, white
clouds hanging in the air
		 The sense of stillness and quiet, like the
world sleeping

The Steplands

A rugged land
By all rights, the Steplands should be lush
and fertile, teeming with farms and pastures. The same storms that soak the Flats
roll through these hills, and snowmelt from
the Huffel Peaks feeds many streams
and aquifers.
And yet: the region is rocky, almost barren
in places. The slopes boast meadows of
sedge and shortgrass and thickets of skinny
pine, but the soil is thin and prone to
erosion and mudslides. Cliffs and outcrops
abound, dotted with shrubs and gnarled
trees and even succulents normally found
only in the south. There’s life a-plenty,
sure—enough to support the roaming bands
of Hillfolk and their herds—but one would
be hard-pressed to make a go of farming.
Why is the land like this? That’s for you
and your table to decide, but perhaps…
… it’s a natural result of the thirsty
bedrock, the ceaseless southerly wind,
the grazing of beasts;
… the Stone Lords’ (page 382) magic still
infuses the land, repelling soil and seed,
fossilizing roots, devouring minerals;
… battles between Stone Lords and the
Barrow Builders (page 34) blasted
the land, and only the hardiest spirits
of the wild (page 356) still dwell here;
… the Things Below (page 412) sleep
fitfully here, breathing in through lakes
and sinkholes, sucking at the lifeforce
of the soil;
… some combination of the above; or
… something completely different.

Questions
		 What do the Hillfolk call the Steplands?
		 Which of you once explored the caves
that dot these lands? What did you
find there that haunts you still?
		 What treasure or wonder is said to be
hidden in the Steplands?
		 What landmark, visible from the Highway, have you always wanted to explore?

Places
		Blackwater Lake (page 48)
		The Makers’ Roads (page 270),
specifically the Highway
		Three Coven Lake (page 438)

Hooks
		Harsh weather threatens PCs on the
Highway; a nearby cave might provide
shelter
		A Hillfolk band is plagued by a bloodthirsty gouzadn (page 378), but a
concerned parent begs for their lost
child’s life to be spared
		A brawzbedr (page 377) naps on the
Highway, blocking a merchant’s wagon
		Someone the PCs care about went to
explore a cave and never came out

Mudslide
					 Start with a slope built up with
soil. Peel away the vegetation through
over-grazing, or fire, or drought, or too
much rain entirely. Add a sudden heavy
rain or some fools walking across it. Hey,
look, a mudslide!
ä		 Point out the lack of ground cover, the
sodden soil, the weight of waiting earth
ä		 It’ll take time/effort/risk to avoid the
unstable slope
ä		 The hillside shifts and buckles,
underfoot or overhead
ä		 People, stones, and trees are swept
away or buried, d6+2 damage (area,
forceful, grabby, ignores armor)
ä		 Trap them, crush them, deprive them
of air
ä		 Leave behind a slick, treacherous,
muddy swath

					 Some slopes are often covered with
scree or strewn with boulders, fist-sized,
head-sized, some as big as horses. Mostly
they stay put. Mostly.
ä		 A loose stone clatters downhill, skitterscatter, billowing dust, but over soon
enough
ä		 A smaller rock smacks them, d6 damage
ä		 A bigger stone teeters, starts to slip
ä		 A whole field of boulders gives way

(area, forceful)
ä		 Bury them under tons of rock and dirt
(Death’s Door)
Bat swarm
					Stepland caves are often home to
thousands upon thousands of bats. They’re
nothing to fear individually. But a swarm of
them, when you stand in their way? Oh dear.

Brawzbedr
					Solitary, huge, stubborn, grumpy
HP 24; Armor 3 (thick hide, boney nodules)
Damage tree-rending claws d10+7 w/
disadvantage (reach, close, forceful, messy,
1 piercing)
Special qualities poor vision and hearing;
excellent sense of smell
Instinct to overreact
			ä Not really notice something
			ä Tear up trees like a child tears
up grass
			ä Swat at something that annoys it
			ä Flail about in a rage (+area), then
get tired
Think of something like a badger or a
sloth, but big as a house: 20 feet long, 9
feet at the shoulder, forearms nearly 12 feet
long when you include those shovel-like
claws. Mostly they just lumber about the
Steplands, looking for their favorite trees
to tear up and chew through, sometimes
clearing whole slopes. Just about everything gives them a wide berth, when they
can. Even apex predators like a kleztigr
don’t think they’re worth the trouble.
You’d think that they’d be easy to spot and
avoid, and mostly you’d be right. But sometimes you’ll round a bend or clear a slope
and there one’ll be, just plodding along or
munching on a log or taking a nap, and
you’d best try to circle around, quiet-like.
377

The Steplands

Gouzadn
					Solitary, magical, cautious, stealthy,
shapeshifter
HP 16; Armor 4 (resilience), 0 vs. certain
sacred wood
Damage tooth and claw d10 (hand, grabby),
hoof and antler d10+2 (close, forceful),
rending strength d10+2 (hand, forceful,
messy)
Special qualities confused and frustrated
by human speech, host to dozens of
beast-spirits
Instinct to see people as either threat or prey
			ä Assume the aspect(s) of one or
more beasts
			ä Do something impossible for a
human, easy for a beast
			ä Suffer through a terrible wound
unfazed, healing with a few days’ rest
			ä Ask beasts or spirits (even quiet
ones) for information or favors
When a young Hillfolk shows signs of
being touched by spirits, their band had
best seek out a spirit-talker (page 235) to
mentor the child. Without such guidance,
these youths might forsake their humanity,
forget their language, and eventually bind
spirits of the wild to their very flesh.

A gouzadn shuns people, living with only
spirits for company. They’re more likely
to flee and hide than fight when people
encroach on their territory. An aggressive
or unwary party, though, might find their
numbers picked off one-by-one in the dark
or when separated. And when that happens, a gouzadn might get a taste of killing
people, maybe a taste for their flesh. Such
monsters haunt the tales and nightmares of
the Hillfolk.
Gouzadn gain their spirits by bonding
with a beast, then consuming its flesh in a
special, intuitive ritual. They might be reasoned with by the Blessed or someone else
who speaks the Spirit Tongue. They might
even be re-taught language, and convinced
to divest their flesh of its surplus spirits.
But most will be loath to do so; at best,
they’ll insist on keeping these spirits with
them in a sacred pouch or similar vessel.
Something interesting: Tales tell of using
“sacred wood” to put down a gouzadn.
Maybe a Hillfolk elder could tell you the
specific type of wood to use?
Something useful: To kill this gouzadn,
you’ll need a weapon made of... (roll the
Die of Fate)

Grochslon
					Solitary, patient, stealthy
HP 12; Armor 0
Damage outer jaws d10 (close, grabby),
inner jaws d10+2 (hand, messy, 1 piercing)
Special qualities blind; senses prey by
scent, hearing, vibrations
Instinct to feed on the unwary, then flee
			ä Lurk unseen in a dark, sheltered spot
			ä Spring forward without warning,
chomping down
			ä Hold tight with outer jaws while
tearing away with the inner
			ä Squirm through a narrow opening
Pale, eyeless things that spawn in the
lightless lakes below the Steplands. Like a
great big pale salamander or snake with two
stubby, muscular legs. As they grow, they
find food down there sparse, and make their
way to the surface. Mostly they stick to cave
mouths, but sometimes—sometimes—they
get bold enough to leave their sheltered
holes of rock, and they find other places to
lurk, places where you’d never expect them.

Something interesting: Some Hillfolk
bands “adopt” a kleztigr, making offerings
of meat or young goats, sheep, or even
horses. The kletztigr follows the band
about, protecting them from outsiders.
Something useful: Such kleztigr won’t
harm anyone wearing some item or clothing distinctive to “their” band (but they
might expect a present).
Spitting drake
					Group, stealthy, organized
HP 6; Armor 2 (pebbly scales)
Damage vice-like jaws d8 (hand, grabby,
messy, 1 piercing)
Special qualities chameleon skin,
excellent climbers
Instinct to pin down a good meal, then
swarm it
			ä Lay an ambush, where they can
surround prey
			ä Spit a head-sized gob of sticky
mucus (near, grabby, reload)
			ä Sprint much more quickly than
you’d expect

Kleztigr
					Solitary, large, cagey, stealthy, agile,
powerful
HP 16; Armor 1 (reflexes)
Damage huge saber-like fangs d10+3 w/
advantage (hand, grabby, messy, 1
piercing), claws or pounce d10+1 w/
advantage (close)
Instinct to bring down a good, solid meal
			ä Follow prey for hours, even days
			ä Pounce on prey from hiding (reach,
forceful, grabby)
			ä Inflict a deadly, bleeding wound

They’re about the size of a big ﻿wolf, but
lower and squatter, with arms out to the
sides like lizards and a big old frill that pops
up when they’re startled or bearing down on
prey. They favor canyons, defiles, or
overlooks where they can bask in the sun
but have a good angle to ambush passersby.

Like a cougar (page 206) but twice as
big, with 8-inch fangs hanging out of their
mouths. Hillfolk tales claim they once were
much more common, pets of the Stone
Lords even. Now they are (thankfully) rare.

Something useful: Strong spirits (yes, like
whisky) quickly dissolve the mucus. Also,
you can identify their territory by the pale
chalky stains their mucus leaves on stone.

Something interesting: Their mucus
adheres quickly to rock, pinning prey in
place. Even if it doesn’t pin prey down, it
gloms on and slows them up. It’ll dry out
and start to flake away after a few hours.

carry heavy loads

		Cave-ins, rock-falls
		Getting stuck in a squeeze
		Running out of/losing your light
		Fear, panic, or confusion in the face of
any of the above

381

Stone Lords
In the time of the Makers (page 264),
the Stone Lords ruled the Steplands (page
372), the southern stretch of the Huffel
Peaks (page 236), and the South Manmarch (page 350). They carved cities out
of the hills. They raised towering monuments. They sang stone, crystal, and glass
into impossible shapes.
The Stone Lords’ empire was grand, but it
was not all glory. It was built on the backs
of countless human servants. And while the
Stone Lords were not especially cruel, neither were they kind. They imposed order on
their world, by whatever means necessary.
For, better than the other Makers, the Stone
Lords knew the Things Below (page 412).
Knew the insidious songs that echoed in the
deep. Knew the chaos promised. Knew that
they must never, ever be freed.
And so, when the Green Lords (page
210) called on the Things Below to put
down their own servants’ rebellion, the
Stone Lords turned on their brethren.
They shattered the Green Lords’ lands and
hunted down the survivors.
Yet: the seals had cracked. The Things
Below sang all the louder. The Stone Lords
themselves stood resolute, but their servants did not. Corruption seeped through
their society like water finding cracks in a
poorly laid foundation.
The end came swiftly. Sorcerers turned the
city of Dulenn into a massive sinkhole, creating what is now Blackwater Lake (page
48). Seals were broken across the land
and horrors slithered up through the tunnels. Mobs turned on their former masters.
One by one, the domains and cities of
the Stone Lords fell into ruin.
The Time of Cataclysm
(page 413) had arrived.
382

Names
Breton- and Cornish-inspired, but with
extra vowels for a drawn-out sound:
Aenedellion, Caedour, Daenizel, Eelowain,
Gaeille, Iaves, Kaeteal, Loiawen, Mearauwd, Neassa, Oaneaz, Paerrain, Rozainne,
Saennaen, Taenguey, Wainoac, Yainniac
Combine with prefixes like “Aalz” (“the
great”), “Skol” (“master”), or “Loaji” (“apprentice”), etc.

The PCs cut north from Three Coven Lake and seek out a place to
camp. I decide that they find a Stone Lord ruin. I roll 6 for theme
(authority/order/hierarchy/tradition), a 5 for structure (chambers
dug into the sides of hills), a 6 for purpose (civic life), a 3 for
architectural elements (crystals/makerglass), and 4 for condition
(buried or hidden, mostly intact). I imagine some sort of belltower, once used to signal the hour and give order to the day.
What remains is the hint of stone stairs leading up a rocky
outcrop. Near the top, a partially collapsed tunnel opens
into a smooth-walled, domed chamber. A makerglass chime
hangs from the ceiling, just a few inches in diameter but a
hundred feet tall or more, dropping down a shaft barely
wider than it is. If tapped, the chime rings painfully
loudly. If struck firmly, it resonates with a thunderous,
deafening noise. I think there’s also a great big bronze
maul still in the chamber, and that a kleztigr (page
379) has been using this place as a den.

A chalky potion
					magical, fragile, slow
1d4 glass vials containing thick, gritty,
whitish fluid. The stopper bears a mark of
transformation, flesh, and stone.
When you drink the whole potion while
touching stone, you transform (over a
matter of minutes) into a mass of stone,
similar to that which you touch. You retain
awareness of your surroundings, blind but
with excellent hearing.

Stone Lords
When you attempt to end your lithic vigil,
roll +CON: on a 10+, you return to your
original form, rested and invigorated—regain all your HP and clear all your debilities; on a 7-9, the transformation back is
slow and arduous, mark a debility; on a 6-,
you are stuck until someone pours another
dose of the potion over you or communes
with your spirit and helps you try again.
Power-crystal
					 , magical, dangerous
A chunk of crystal the size of a clenched
fist. If charged, it thrums or crackles with
power. If depleted, it fills you with a sense
of emptiness and longing.
When you brandish the depleted powercrystal at a source of elemental energy, roll
+CON: on a 10+, the crystal absorbs the
energy fully and as safely as possible (it is
now charged); on a 7-9, the crystal absorbs
the energy (it’s now charged) but pick 1:
		 You are harmed by the coursing energy,
taking damage as the GM sees fit
		 The power stored in the crystal is
extremely unstable, likely to discharge
without warning
		 The crystal cracks; henceforth, treat a
10+ on this move as a 7-9, and you
can’t pick this option again.
In the hands of a skilled arcanist, a charged
power-crystal can fuel or empower a magical effect (though this is dangerous). In the
hands of the incautious, the reckless, or the
desperate, it can split or crack, leading to a
violent explosion.

A scarred slab of marble
					immobile, magical
A slab of once-beautiful stone, some 30
feet tall and about half again as wide,
standing in a vaulted chamber, surrounded
by deeply etched runes that speak of union
and protection, consumption and sacrifice.
The stone bears a multitude of chips, gouges, and cracks. Dust and debris surround it.
When you pry a chip of stone from the marble and swallow it whole, while standing
within the ring of runes, your flesh becomes
like stone and your DEX becomes -2 until
the effect ends. The next three times you
would suffer physical harm, the marble slab
suffers the harm instead. The effect ends
after the third blow.

Unstable construction
					 The Stone Lords’ work has stood
for centuries, and they were the cleverest
architects to ever walk this world. But the
problem with grand, clever architecture is
that when it fails, it fails spectacularly.
ä		 A wall/floor/building is precariously
perched, or a pillar/support/buttress
supports an impossible load
ä		 Something creaks/teeters/sways/groans
ä		 Cracks form/debris tumbles/something
starts to tip
ä		 Age-old construction starts to fall like
dominoes (run!)
ä		 All is reduced to rubble, and dust,
and death

Ask yourself: where does this construct’s
sapience come from? Clever scripting?
Spirits bound to stone? The will drained
from living servants? Something else?
1d12

Imagine one of those artists’ mannequins,
but full-sized and made of polished rock.
Two fingers and a thumb on each hand.
Faceless, expressionless, making a stoneon-stone grinding noise as they move.
They’re most often found inert and covered
in dust, waiting for some energy source—
like a Stone Lord’s song or a thrumming
power-crystal—to give them “life.” They’ll
then follow any orders given to them in the
Stone Lord’s tongue.
A few of these servants are still active,
powered by inexhaustible sources in
ancient halls. Their joints are worn smooth,
their feet and hands ground down to nubs.
Some crawl on broken limbs, attempting
to complete tasks that will never end.

394

Articulated statues, squat and wide, with
huge stone hands, set to watch forever over
some ruin, passage, or treasure. Their stone
“minds” are full of vigilance, and violence.
Each sentinel bears a power-crystal in its
chest, slowly absorbing vibrations through
earth and stone. A few days of charging is
enough to animate the construct for only a
few minutes—which is usually more than
enough time for it to repel (or crush)
an invader.

Mosaic swarm
					Solitary, large, construct, amorphous,
			stealthy
HP 24; Armor 3 (made of thousands of
			ceramic tiles)
Damage jagged fists of ceramic d10+3
			(hand, close, forceful)
Special qualities externally powered; buglike mind; blind; tremorsense; unharmed

Stone Lords adorned their halls with elaborate tilework and mosaics. Most of them
were decorative. Most of them.
Something interesting: Either some spirit
is possessing these tiles, or this is some sort
of construct. If it’s a spirit, there must be a
tether. If it’s a Stone Lord construct, well,
the individual tiles are too small to power
or direct themselves.
Something useful: The mosaic’s frame
remained on the wall, and it’s glowing
blue—aetherium runes! Destroy the runes,
and the construct should fall apart.

Pacifier
					Group, small, construct, amorphous
HP 10; Armor 4 (just a stone sphere)
Damage stupefying pulse of sound d6
			(reach, area, reload, ignores armor)
Special qualities must recharge after a few
hours; limited autonomy; blind;
tremorsense; has trouble with stairs;
vulnerable to blunt, forceful attacks
Instinct to keep mortals docile
ä		 Sense and follow strong emotions
ä		 Hum a soothing, pacifying sound
(near, area)
ä		 Sound the alarm, call for
			reinforcements
They’re basically hollow stone spheres,
maybe scratched and dinged but otherwise
featureless. Often found in their little
alcoves, charging. The Stone Lords would
deploy them among the rowdier servants
to keep things running smoothly.
When you are in range of a pacifier’s hum,
your emotions lose their edge. When you
rouse yourself to act violently, urgently, or
passionately, roll +WIS. If you are in range
of multiple pacifiers humming in unison,
you have disadvantage. On a 10+, you
shake off the effect; on a 7-9, you shake off
the effect but pick 1.
		Your mind is addled; mark dazed
		Your freedom is short-lived; the effect
returns if you pause for any reason
		You get carried away or take things too
far, resulting in a consequence of the
GM’s choice

395

The Stream

A few minutes from Stonetop, near the
base of the bluff, in the Great Wood (page
200). Wide, shallow, swift, lined with
small stones. Folks come here to wash and
fetch water, but rarely alone. It's not deep,
but it's still a body of water.
		The current's constant rush and murmur
		Flat plain on either side, gravelly, dotted
with saplings, weeds, wildflowers
		Vague sense of dread, of being watched
		Spring: cold snowmelt swelling
the Stream
		Summer: dragonflies, lightning bugs
		Spring/Autumn: leaves in current
		Winter: eerie stillness, icy surface
Tip: raise your voice a little, like you’re
talking over background sound
396

Questions
		 What little ritual do villagers perform
every time they cross the Stream? Why?
		 How far have you followed the Stream?
Which way? What strange sight did
you see on the way?
		 Which of you have encountered the
Quiet Twins, the ghostly children who
haunt the Stream?
		 What's the scariest, darkest story
you've ever heard about the Stream?
Who told it to you?
		 Who do you think the Quiet Twins
were? Why?

The Stream

Hooks

Inhabitants

		A dangerous beast takes to watering
near town.
		The Stream runs dry, despite ample rains
		Something mysterious (dangerous?) is
deposited by the current.
		Haunting songs are heard on misty
days; hot-blooded youths are intent on
finding the source (an entrancing Fae,
like Blush-of-Dawn, page 112).
		The Quiet Twins appear more
frequently, urgently trying to
warn of… something.

The Quiet Twins
					Group, small, spirit, undead, cautious,
unnerving
HP 13; Armor 1 (lacks organs)
Damage icy touch d6 (hand, disadvantage,
ignores armor)
Special qualities mute, bound to the Stream

			ä Watch what happens at the Stream
(seen or unseen)
			ä Manifest a ghostly form of mist,
cold air (harmed only by silver or salt)
			ä Mouth and gesture in silent terror
Tip: shrink into your seat, to make yourself
smaller, bug eyed, while mouthing gibberish.
A pair of ghostly children who
haunt the Stream. Sightings
are uncommon, but

397

Tempest Lords
They were, perhaps, the first of the Makers
(page 264). They charted the seas and the
stars. They leashed the elements to their
will. They built mighty ships and flying
castles, sailed forth from their island
home and conquered. All who stood
against them were reduced
to ash and glass and slag.
The pride of the Tempest Lords was
warranted, but their hubris led to
their fall. They sought to tear their
entire homeland free from the earth,
to set it floating above the world
they ruled.
They failed. Calamity followed.
The seas boiled and belched forth horrors.
Fire rained from the sky. Their island capital sank beneath the waves, and the spine
of their empire was shattered. Civil war
erupted. Colonies rebelled.
The Tempest Lords who survived were
scattered to the winds. Some tried to
restore the empire, but met violent ends at
the hands of rivals or would-be subjects.
Soon, only a few Tempest Lords remained,
enigmas even among other Makers.
One of these survivors, Stormcatcher,
made her way to the the Flats (page
126), beyond even the most far-flung of
the empire’s old outposts. There, between
four other nations of Makers, she built her
tower. And when the horrors of the Things
Below (page 412) were set loose upon the
world, Stormcatcher’s tower—what folks
now call the Ruined Tower (page 332)—
was the last of the Makers’ enclaves to fall.

398

Language and names
Tempest Lord writing was ideogrammatic
(characters represent ideas, not sounds),
and they spoke a primordial language—all
who heard it understood it as their native
tongue. As such, the names of Tempest
Lords are rendered as descriptions, titles,
or honorifics.
Example names: Brinedrinker, EyesLike-Lightning, Fardweller, Foehammer,
Lightning-Is-Her-Spear, His-Smile-Isa-Harbor, Mind-Like-Fire, Moonspeaker,
Oarbreaker, Rainchaser, Soother-OfWaves, Sparkspitter, Squalltalker, Starfinder, Taller-Than-Masts, Voice-of-Thunder,
Wavecutter, Waymaker, Windwalker
When other Makers spoke or wrote of
them, they used their own tongue. Windwalker was called Vetrodokha by the Green
Lords (page 210) and Avaelkerzhaer by
the Stone Lords (page 382).

Tempest Lords

One of the PCs is heading to Titan Bones (page 448), and I’d like some sense of what’s
out there. I think it holds a Tempest Lord ruin, probably once associated with Stormcatcher
and the now-Ruined Tower (page 332).
I roll a 3 for theme (“the heavens: weather and clouds...”), a 4 for structure (“bunker/
vault”), a 3 for purpose (“infrastructure”), and an 11 for architectural elementals (“verticality”). I then roll a 5 for condition (“largely buried... hidden”). I ponder a bit and come up
with this:
Stormcatcher erected a spire on Titan Bones, like a lighthouse, topped with an aetherium
weathervane that modulated the weather. When the Stone Lords’ rebellious servants
attacked, the tower’s custodian took the weathervane and hid beneath the spire. The spire
fell and the custodian was trapped. All that remains are scattered stones and a circular
foundation. And beneath centuries of dirt and rubble, there’s a locked trapdoor. It reveals a
spiral staircase, a deep shaft, an intact cellar, a skeleton, and an aetherium weathervane.

		 Aetherium
An alloy of copper and lightning. Pure
aetherium is light, soft, and ductile (similar
to tin), but the Tempest Lords and Forge
Lords (page 156) could further alloy it or
manipulate it with magic to have a wide
range of properties and uses.

Tempest Lords
Making aetherium
A clever arcanist might rediscover the
process of making aetherium. To do so, you
must…
		 Study a largely-intact aetherium
crucible for a few weeks
		 Study both active and depleted
aetherium for a few weeks
		 Consult a knowledgeable metalsmith
(or be one yourself )
		 Acquire the appropriate chemics
(Value 0) and a sizeable mass of copper
(Value 1, immobile)
		 Devise some way to introduce
lightning to molten copper
		 Risk injury, fire, destruction, and failure
When you meet all the requirements, you
can transform a purse of coppers (Value
0) and the appropriate chemics (Value 0)
into an ingot or casting of pure aetherium (magical, crude, possibly dangerous,
Value 1 to those-who-know). The process
is risky; you are Defying Danger each time
you undertake it.
To imbue aetherium with one of the characteristics above, you’ll need access to a
trove of Tempest Lord or Forge Lord lore,
at least. Beyond that, Make a Plan with
the GM.

. steading improvement .

AETHERIUM CRUCIBLE
Requires all of the following:
		 Learning the process of making
aetherium (at left)
		 Learning to imbue aetherium with
at least one special characteristic
		 A reliable means of capturing,
storing, and directing lightning
that strikes the Stone
		 Securing a steady supply of the
chemics needed to make aetherium
		 Securing a steady supply of copper
		 An exceptional smith who knows
the secrets of aetherium
When you mark all the requirements,
add “Aetherium Crucible” to the
Resources list and the map. As long
as you trade aetherium to the outside
world, increase Prosperity by 1.
Henceforth, small amounts of raw
aetherium (magical, Value 1) are
commonly available in Stonetop,
and you have advantage to Trade &
Barter for the aetherium items with
special characteristics (+2 Value).

Aetherium urn
, magical, dangerous
The lid is held fast by no apparent means.
It takes some effort to pry open and eagerly snaps back into place.
When the urn is open and empty, it draws
raw elemental force into it like a lodestone draws iron. With the lid in place,
the force is safely stored. If the urn is
damaged or dented (not hard), any stored
force escapes, violently.

406

Bronze ring
, magical, fireproof, l l l hours
About 8 inches in diameter, an inch thick
throughout, scorched and smeared with
ash. A button protrudes, with a Tempest
Lord glyph that might mean “stubborn.”
When you find the ring, it has enough
power to run for 1d4-1 hours.
When you press the button, the ring fixes
in place, defying gravity. It simply will not
budge. The effect ends if the ring is bent
or broken, or when it runs out of power.
There’s no obvious way to end the effect
early. When the ring runs out of power, the
button pops out and the effect ends.
When you heat the ring in a forge or kiln
for about eight hours, it gains an hour of
use (up to 3 hours).
Floating sash
magical
A length of bluish-white lamé, maybe 6
inches wide by 6 feet long, snaking about
in the air. Like a belt, but for someone far
larger than you.
When you wrap the sash securely around
your waist, it buoys you against falls—you
never fall faster than if you had fallen your
own height.
Silvery tuning fork
magical, loud
When you strike this tuning fork against a
hard surface, it emits a loud tone for about
a minute. Nearby (reach, area) magical
effects and creatures become visible: shaking, vibrating, distorted. The noise draws
the attention of any spirits or magically
attuned beings for miles around.

Tempest Lords

Storm-cloud potion
magical, fragile
A glass vial of dark-grey liquid, roiling and
shot through with sparks.
When you drink a storm-cloud potion,
lightning courses through you. Take 1d10
damage (ignores armor) and mark a debility. Should you survive, hold 3 Storm.
While you hold any Storm, you cannot feel
pain or fear, tell friend from foe, speak coherently, or exercise patience or care. Add
your Storm to your Armor, and your melee
and thrown attacks do +Storm damage
(+loud, +forceful).
When you reduce someone or something to 0
HP, lose 1 Storm.

Radiation
					Sometimes, the Tempest Lords
unleashed such terrible power that it
poisoned all it touched: soil, bone, stone,
metal. Poisoned them all so deeply that
now, still, centuries and centuries later,
those things poison all that draws near.
Such poisonous places are often corrupted
by the Things Below (page 412). But even
when they are not, they are dangerous.
ä		 Point out flora or fauna, stunted
and strange
ä		 Make their skin itch, make them
feel unwell
ä		 Inflict a debility that refuses to go away
ä		 Have symptoms appear (or return)
hours or days after exposure
ä		 Have symptoms linger or worsen,
perhaps for months
PCs or NPCs exposed to such radiation
are prone to developing terrible, wasting
illness, months or years later. Write this up
as a threat (an affliction).
407

Tempest Lords

Vitrification
					 A few Tempest Lords could call up
a bolt of power so raw and potent that it
would turn whatever it touched into glass.
It was dangerous for even them to use, but
no foe could stand against it.
It’s possible that some device still exists
that can unleash this sort of power. If so,
treat it as a threat (a MacGuffin; Instinct
to tempt people into using it). It would
require vast amounts of energy and involve
extraordinary risks.
When you are struck by a vitrifying bolt, reduce your HP to 0 and roll +CON: on a 10+,
you merely suffer terrible burns and will soon
be at Death’s Door; on a 7-9, you and your
possessions are turned to jagged glass, but
you might be restored; on a 6-, you die but
your corpse, your soul, and your possessions
are also turned into jagged glass—should the
vitrification be reversed, it’s as though you
rolled a 6- on Death’s Door.
Vitrification can be reserved, but you must
decide how it’s done. Perhaps it involves:
… vast amounts of power and great risk;
… a sympathetic link to the victim, or
whatever vitrified them in the first place;
… the intervention of a powerful god
(page 178) or primordial entity
(page 298);
… spilling the life-blood of your kin upon
the jagged glass;
… a spell of transference, where someone
else becomes vitrified instead;
… a spiritwalker releasing the vitrified
soul, and guiding it to a suitable vessel;
… something different for each victim, or
for each source of vitrification;
… some combination of the above; or
… something else entirely.
408

Elemental vortex
					Picture a filigree sphere of aetherium,
maybe 6 inches across, a glowing mote
swirling at its center. This device—called a
matrix—binds an elemental spirit (page
360) and absorbs a particular force, with
which the spirit is able to manifest a swirling vortex.
A spirit bound to a matrix must follow
any orders given in the Tempest Lord’s
primordial language. As few alive can give
them such orders, any matrices encountered will likely be acting on orders from
centuries ago.
Power and size: Each matrix absorbs a
particular type of power, and current HP
of its vortex reflects its current power.
As a vortex gains or loses HP, its size,
armor, and damage change as well. At 0
HP, it dissipates and its matrix falls to the
ground, inert until it absorbs more HP.
Pick or roll to determine a vortex’s starting
size, HP, Armor, and Damage.
1d12
1-4

hp

stats

1 to 5 tiny; Armor 1;
Damage d8-2 (hand)
5-7
6 to 11 small; Armor 2;
Damage d8 (hand)
8-9
12 to 18 Armor 2;
Damage d10 (hand, close)
10-11 19 to 23 large; Armor 3;
Damage d10+3 (hand,
close, reach, forceful)
12
24 to 28 huge; Armor 3;
Damage d10+5 (hand,
close, reach, area, forceful)

Tempest Lords
Attacking the matrix: A matrix itself has
only 3 HP. If reduced to 0 HP, the spirit
within escapes, dealing the vortex’s current
damage (+area, one range tag greater than
normal).
When a PC tries to strike the matrix inside
an active vortex, the vortex’s Armor applies. Also, tell them the requirements and/
or consequences. such as:
		 You’ll have disadvantage to Clash/
Let Fly/deal damage
		 You’ll need to create or wait for an
opening, where the matrix is exposed
		 You’ll need to get in close/let it
engulf you
		 You’ll need a longer/heavier/magical
weapon
		 You’ll risk losing/ruining your weapon
		 You’ll suffer its attack, no matter what
Pick options based on the nature of the
vortex, the PC’s weapon, and the rest of
their fictional positioning. The larger the
vortex, the harder it is to strike the matrix.

Earth vortex
					Solitary, size by HP, spirit, construct,
amorphous, grumpy, clumsy
HP varies; Armor by HP, +1 (made of rock)
Damage smash (damage and tags by HP)
Special qualities half damage from stabbing
or slashing weapons (unless magical)
Instinct to topple and undermine
			ä Manifest a churning mass out of
nearby earth, sand, and rocks
			ä Pin a smaller foe (+grabby)
			ä Grind a pinned foe, dealing damage
w/advantage (+messy)
			ä Ruin a sturdy object or structure
(costs 1d4 HP)
			ä Shake the earth (+area, +forceful,
one range greater than normal; costs
1d4 HP)
An earth matrix absorbs power from
pressure, blunt force, and seismic activity.
It gains 1d4 HP from a week buried under
earth or stone, from being at the bottom
of a rockslide or cave-in, or from touching
the ground during a major earthquake (not
one it created).
When you strike an earth matrix with a
bludgeoning attack, it takes no damage. If
the blow was forceful and its vortex has 10
HP or less, it gains 1 HP. (The vortex itself
has no such immunities, smash away!)

Fire vortex
					Solitary, size by HP, spirit, construct,
amorphous, intense, aggressive
HP varies; Armor by HP
Damage flames & fire (damage and tags
by HP, +dangerous)
Special qualities fireproof; harmed only by
magical attacks, water, or smothering
Instinct to consume
			ä Set nearby combustibles alight
			ä Manifest a vortex out of nearby fire,
cinder, smoke, and ash
			ä Extinguish fires, absorbing their
power (near, area)
			ä Engulf a smaller foe (+messy,
+ignores armor)
			ä Fling flames (+near; costs 1d4 HP)
			ä Leap across a barrier (but lose
1d4 HP)
A fire vortex absorbs power from heat and
flames. It gains 1d4 HP by spending a day
in an oven or an hour in a forge or kiln, or
by absorbing a raging fire.
When you target a fire vortex or its matrix
with magical fire or heat, it takes no damage. If the vortex has 18 HP or less, it gains
1d4 HP instead.

A wind matrix absorbs power from moving
air. It gains 1d4 HP from weeks exposed to
normal weather, an hour in high winds, or
a minute or so in a tornado.
When you strike a wind vortex with a salt
or lead, it takes 1d4 damage from a handful, 2d4 damage from a item, and 3d4
damage from a
item or amount.

A water matrix absorbs power from
currents, tides, waves, and falling water. It
gains 1d4 HP from a few days in a flowing
stream, a few hours in a rushing river, or a
few minutes in rapids or a major waterfall.
When you are engulfed by a water vortex,
hold 1d4 Breath. When you make a move
while engulfed, and that move involves a
roll, spend 1 Breath or mark a debility after
rolling. If you can’t do either, you drown
and are at Death’s Door.
411

The Things Below
They are older than the world. They are
primordial entities (page 298) of darkness, chaos, and corruption.
When light first filled the void, they
were huddled in the shadows. When
matter coalesced, they were there,
chewing at its edges. When the First
Creators thought “I am me” and
named themselves, Those Who Dwelt
in Darkness sought their ruin. They
whispered lies and stoked egos. They
urged the Creators to war amongst
themselves. To lay waste to their
creations. To let chaos reign.
But the First Creators saw the lies for
what they were. They saw the danger
posed by the Eldest of Evils. But they
saw, too, the utility of such deception.
And so, the Creators laid a trap. They came
together to forge the world itself: matter and
energy, land and sea and sky, life and death.
They set in motion a system so vast and intricate and wondrous that They Who Whispered could not help but be jealous, could not
help but try to destroy it.
When the Terrible Ones moved to destroy the
world, the First Creators sprung their trap.
They folded the world around the Things That
Knew Only Hate, sealed them away in rock
and stone, bound them in the earth itself. The
world— this world, what would become our
world—became their tether and their prison.
And they, so shackled, became the Things Below.

412

The Things Below

The Time of Cataclysm
No prison is perfect. With the slow turn
of ages, the bindings weakened. The
Things Below whispered once again, and
those whispers found ears.
The Stone Lords (page 382) learned
firsthand the chaos that those whispers
could unleash. They found the cracks in
the world-prison and laid great seals
upon them. For a time, the whispers
were quiet.
But the Green Lords (page 210)
remembered those whispers and what
they promised, and called upon the
Things Below to crush the traitorous
Fae (page 94). The Green Lords were
ultimately defeated, but the real damage had
been done: the bindings were weakened. The
whispers of the Things Below had returned,
had become a song.
The Stone Lords’ own servants heard that
song. Sorcerers arose among them, gathered
followings, then cults. In secret, they laid
their plans and spread their lies. When the
time was right, the sorcerers broke the seals
and ushered in the Time of Cataclysm.
The Stone Lords’ cities sank. Mobs of their
servants turned against them. In the north, a
dark and terrible howling arose and the Rime
Lords (page 316) sacrificed themselves to stop
it. A wave of horrors burrowed up from the Flats
(page 126), bringing the last of the Tempest
Lords low (page 398). The Things Below stoked
the jealousies and fears of the Forge Lords (page
156), turning them against each other.
The time of the Makers was over.

413

The Things Below

The current age

Hooks

In the aftermath of the Makers’ fall, the
would-be sorcerer-kings of the Barrow
Builders (page 34) came to dominate
the World’s End. For generations, the
region—and perhaps the wider world—
knew only horror, and suffering, and war.

		A local unwittingly offers sacrifice to a
corrupted spirit, gaining power; the
spirit asks for (and offers) more
		A conundrum vexes the PCs; consulting
a black menhir (page 425) might help
		Crinwin (page 58) have started to
worship at a corrupted site (page
422) becoming corrupted in turn
		An antiquarian (page 260) is secretly
a thrall to a Thing Below, and seeks to
unleash a more powerful servant
		A spot in the fields around Stonetop
goes bad, festering like an infected boil,
corrupted by a discarded artifact

And yet: the power of the Things Below
waned. For centuries now, they have lain
mostly quiet in the earth. What brought
those dark times to an end? That’s for you
and your table to decide, but perhaps…
… the gods themselves (page 178)
intervened;
… mortal heroes worked a great binding,
empowered by great sacrifice;
… the brave and the desperate rose up
across the land, toppling sorcerers and
banishing horrors one-by-one;
… the world began to heal itself and
renew its bindings;
… the Things Below—sated on destruction
and woe—simply tired and withdrew;
… some combination of the above; or
… something else entirely.
Whatever caused the Things Below to fall
back, their retreat was incomplete. Some
places remain touched by their influence,
some creatures stained to the heart.
Objects of power linger, corrupting the
unwitting, and some of these—like the
sorcerers of old—dance in the dark to a
nightmarish tune.
Of late, within the past generation or so,
tremors have coursed through different
quarters of the World’s End. Do prisoners
in the depths strain once more against
their shackles?

414

The Things Below

Lore
Everyone knows:
		Evil things are bound deep within
the earth
		Sometimes, they send forth demons to
wreak havoc upon the world
		Deep water is the most likely place for
such horrors to emerge
		Marshedge (page 274) keeps such
horrors at bay by constantly burning
bendis root
One might know:
		About the Time of Cataclysm and
what came after
		The name, titles, aspects, powers, or
minions of a given Thing Below
		How their power corrupts places,
artifacts, people, beasts, and spirits
		How to bind, cleanse, or ward off their
taint/power/minions
		That unclean things recoil from bendis
root smoke, but truly fierce or willful
ones can overcome it
		That bronze is bane to the Things Below, and that orichalcum (page 162)
is even more effective
		The difference between corrupted
beings and emanations
Very few know:
		Of their true nature
			 		How they came to be imprisoned
		 		 Any way to truly destroy one

Questions
		 What frightening tale from childhood
featured the Things Below?
		 What little thing do villagers do to
ward off their attention or influence?
		 When did you first feel their presence?
What happened? What still haunts you
about that experience to this day?
		 What place nearby is said to be tainted
by their influence? What sign marks it
as such?
		 How do the locals speak of them? In
hushed tones? In dire sermons? Somewhere in between?
		 When was the last time the village
dealt with a demon, cult, or sorcerer?

Demons
In truth, there’s no such thing as
demons. Or rather, “demon” is not a
meaningful term. It’s often used to
refer to emanations (page 436) of
or beings corrupted (page 432) by
the Things Below. But folks might
use the term to apply to any number
of supernatural terrors. If it’s hostile,
magical, and has (or can take) a
physical form, someone is likely to
call it a demon.

415

The Things Below

Themes
When you introduce a Thing—or more
likely, when you introduce a site, artifact, or
danger associated with one—choose or roll
2 or more prompts from the theme table
here. Combine the results and see where
they take you. Alternatively, pick one of the
established Things Below (page 419),
																		and use its themes.

1d12

theme

1

Darkness/cold/despair: the
swallowing of light, warmth, and
hope (obsidian, hoarfrost)
Denial/shame/secrets/deceit: the
rejection of truth, forgiveness, trust
(pearls, eyes, masks)
Delusion/delirium/nightmare: the
loss of self, the breaking of minds
and wills (quicksilver, mirrors,
reflections)
Hunger/need/addiction: lack and
desperation, the howling want
(wind, fangs, meat)
Cruelty/torture/violence/rage: the
urge to hurt, kill, and dominate (red
crystal, spilled blood)
Destruction/chaos/ruin/ignorance:
the tower crumbling; anxiety and
loss (fire, ash, dust)
Wounds/injury/pain: the rending of
flesh, the breaking of bone (blood,
bones, claws, blades)
Corruption/disease/decay: the loss
of strength, wholeness, wellness
(vermin, poison, acid, filth)
Gluttony/greed/jealousy: the need
for more, ever more (mouths, serpents, gems, swarms)
Confinement/suffocation/pressure/
abuse: the crushing weight of
helplessness (chains, water, earth)
Mutation/transformation/roiling
flesh: the malleability of the body
(wax, clay, slime, tentacles)
Death/undeath/loss/grief: the end
of all things, the refusal to let go
(skulls, tombs, pyres)

The Things Below

Creating a Thing
As it becomes necessary to flesh out a
Thing Below, combine its themes (previous
page) with 2 or more aspects to inspire
your descriptions of it. Choose or roll an
instinct, or invent one of your own.
If a Thing Below becomes an ongoing
source of problems for the PCs (and it
probably will), then give it a name and one
or more grandiose titles. Write it up as a
threat (a magical entity).

Remember that the Things Below are not
bound by constraints of biology, physics, or
reality. Make their aspects impossible and
weird, things of fever-dream and drugfueled vision.

417

The Things Below

Names & titles
The true names of the Things Below are
nigh impossible for humans to comprehend, much less pronounce. What names
we have for them are crude approximations. Still, they work well enough to call
upon their power.
To name a Thing Below, try one of these:
		Pick an unpleasant word or concept,
translate it into a language you and
your players don’t know (“hunger” >>
“Hlad” in Czech)
		Take the name of a supernatural entity
from pop culture, literature, or another
game; change it a little or a lot
(“Dagon” >> “Daagon”).
		Combine short, unlikely sounds, often
with apostrophes to denote short
pauses or inflection (“Hec’tumel”)
		Some combination of the above
Then, give each Thing one or more evocative titles, keeping in mind the themes
and aspects already established. Look to
the lists below for inspiration, altering
word forms to suit (i.e., “suppurate” might
becomes Suppurating, Suppuration, or
Suppurator).
Article/preoposition: the, a, an, who,
which, that, of, in, on, through, with,
without, o

418

Verb: bargain, bleed, break, bring, burn,
burrow, bury, butcher, chew, consume,
crawl, crush, dance, deceive, deny, desire,
despoil, destroy, eat, end, engulf, feed,
fester, flense, freeze, haunt, hide, hoard,
howl, hunger, keep, know, lack, laugh, lie,
melt, offer, promise, rage, rend, roil, rot,
scatter, scurry, seep, shape, shatter, sing,
slaughter, sleep, slough, smother, snuff,
spoil, starve, steal, strain, suppurate,
swallow, swarm, topple, unravel, wallow,
want, weave, whisper, wither, etc.
Adjective: all-consuming, awful, black,
blackest, broken, crimson, cruel, dark,
dead, desperate, eldest, false, forty, endless, first, forgotten, forlorn, gray, great,
hidden, hundred, hungry, implacable,
inexorable, lost, mad, many, pale, pallid,
red, rusty, secret, terrible, thousand,
unkind, unliving, vast, wrathful, yellow
Role: child, daughter, father, heir, herald,
king, lady, last, lord, master, mistress,
mother, prince, princess, thief, queen,
son, tyrant, etc.

The Things Below

For We Are Many
The Things Below are legion, varying
wildly in power and predilection, activity
and ambition. Some are whispered of by
peoples far and wide. Others are local
terrors, gnawing at a single thread of the
world that binds them.
Following are half a dozen Things Below
that may or may not exist in your game.
Daagon, Who Waits
					in Deep Waters
Threat (magical entity)
Themes gluttony/greed/jealousy,
roiling flesh/mutation/transformation
Instinct to take from the surface,
to never let go
ä		 Send forth minions to do its bidding
ä		 Offer power, wealth, immortality
ä		 Demand a sacrifice
ä		 Twist its servants into wet and
wriggling things
Those foolish enough to brave deep water
will tell you: something lurks below. Something patient, something vast, an abyssal
mouth opening into a belly that can never
be filled. Its countless squirming servants
brave the dry and choking land to gather
flesh, to gather bone. To steal all wealth
and beauty. To take lives full of hope and
promise, and feed them to the yawning
dark wet jaws.

El’rash-Orra, Lord of Orbs,
					the Many-Eyes
Threat (magical entity)
Themes denial/shame/secrets/deceit,
delusion/delirium/nightmare
Instinct to reveal all manner of ugliness
ä		 Spy on someone’s actions,
			desires, shames
ä		 Give knowledge (wanted or not)
			via dreams/visions
ä		 Twist someone’s senses (of reality,
			of themselves)
Should you find yourself alone, and yet
convinced that you’re being watched, it’s
best not to turn and look. For if you do,
you might see a great and disembodied
eye. Or worse: a faceless sovereign upon a
silver throne, brandishing a silver orb. And
should it stand, and let its robes fall open,
it will be as the night sky with countless
twinkling eyes that see all shames and
doubts, all regrets, all guilts and secret fears
that have ever been or those that might yet
be. Lo! It will show them all to you! Gladly, it will show you! Whether you wish to
see or not.
See also: the Staff of the Lidless Orb
(page 540).

See also: Blackwater Lake (page 48), the
secrets of Gordin’s Delve (page 190), and
the Ring of Daagon (page 560).

419

The Things Below

Hec’tumel, Pale Serpent!
					Slitherer In Darkness!
Death Is Its Eyes!
Threat (magical entity)
Themes darkness/cold/despair, undeath/
death/loss/grief
Instinct to smother life, hope, and light
ä		 Sense powerful longings/emotions
ä		 Offer services, secrets, or power
ä		 Twist a bargain to its favor
‘Tis a pale thing, a dead thing, a long reptilian creature with skulls where it should have
eyes. It slithers through darksome, bonestrewn caves. Caves that drip with tears and
lost hopes. It raised up the first sorcerers,
gave them the power they craved. It asked
only for little things, harmless things, things
they’d never miss. And it delighted, yesss it
delighted so, when the gifts it gave brought
their recipients low. Brought them low, and
everything around them, yesss.
See also: the Hec’tumel Codex
(page 556).

420

Hlad, the Devourer,
					the Eternal Maw
Threat (magical entity)
Themes hunger/need/addiction,
			destruction/chaos/ruin/ignorance
Instinct to consume reality
ä		 Erode strength, wholeness, life
ä		 Draw in the weak-willed
ä		 Grow in strength and power
The Eternal Maw is a hole in reality, a
howling vortex of destruction. To hear its
song is to know that all things will end, to
wish for the respite of oblivion. To look
upon it is to see the void, to know the
futility of struggle. To touch it is to feel all
strength and courage fail, to face a terrible
choice. Will you submit to its implacable
hunger? Or will you become one of its
infinite mouths?
See also: the Hungering Maw of Hlad
(page 572).

The Things Below

L’bin’bozia, Flesh-Candle,
					the Wax Skull, Roiling Tomb
Threat (magical entity)
Themes roiling flesh/mutation/transformation, confinement/suffocation/
pressure/abuse
Instinct to horrify and overwhelm
ä		 Torment with nightmares
ä		 Turn someone’s flesh against them
ä		 Offer them respite, at a terrible price
In your dreams, it finds you. It grabs you
with a host of hands, thick as tastebuds on
a terrible tongue. The hands drag you down
through water, through soil and stone, to a
tomb of black basalt and splintered bone.
They twist your head, unwilling, to look
upon your host: a corpse, bubbling and blistering and wreathed in white flame, leering
from a chair of squirming limbs, welcoming
you to your eternal wretched home.

Y’aaw’kara, the Howling Wind,
the Flayer of Flesh
Threat (magical entity)
Themes hunger/need/addiction, cruelty/
torture/violence/rage, wounds/
injury/pain
Instinct to spread pain and misery
ä		 Sense or amplify hunger/fear/
			anger/pain
ä		 Offer the strength of cruelty
ä		 Inflict the Howling Curse (page
492) on someone
To the people of the far north, Y’aaw’kara’s voice is constant as the wind. For he is
the angry word. He is the gnawing winter
hunger. He is the dream of vengeance upon
those who have wronged you, or those
who simply have more. Greet him, and
you shall see a starving wolf with the body
of a starving man, bleeding from a dozen
wounds. Listen to him, and you shall hear
his wheezing breath, howling like a blizzard
gale. Invite him in, and never be prey again.
See also: Barrier Pass (page 26), the
Whitefang Mountains (page 480).

The Things Below

Corrupted sites
These are places where the influence of the
Things Below has taken hold, where their
power seeps into the world. If the site itself
hasn’t been established, combine the terrain in which it is located with a feature.
1d12

feature

1

Nothing to mark it but the
terrain itself
A distinctive (un)natural landmark
(big tree, natural arch, spring, etc.)
Signs of great destruction (ash fields,
crevasse, lava flow, mudslide, etc.)
A beast’s lair/burrow/den
A tomb/barrow/memorial, left by
the locals or their ancestors
An old dwelling/gathering place of
the locals or their ancestors
A ruin of the Makers (page 264),
or a lingering sign of their presence
Deep water, the depths obscure,
conceals the site (roll 1d8)

2-3
4
5
6
7
8
9-12

Once the site is established, pick or roll its
themes (page 416) to identify the nature
of the Thing corrupting the place (or, if you
know the Thing is one already established,
apply its themes to the site).
Pick or roll the cause and severity of the
corruption. Populate the place with artifacts (page 424) and dangers (page 428)
associated with the Things Below, plus
discoveries and dangers appropriate to the surrounding area.

422

1d12

cause

1

Erosion/shifting earth/
the implacable passage of time
2
A failed attempt to wield
primordial power
3-5
An artifact/a sorcerer’s bones/
remains of a corrupted beast/etc.,
left to fester*
6-8
An offering of flesh/blood/trauma,
intentional or not*
9-10 A summoning/an invocation/
a misguided invitation*
11-12 A seal or binding that kept prior
corruption in check, now
weakened* (roll d10 again for the
original corruption)
* Decide whether this was intentional, or
have someone roll the Die of Fate (1-2:
intentional; 3-4 misguided; 5-6 accidental/
incidental). If it was intentional or misguided, who did it and why? If accidental,
what happened?
1d12
1-3

severity

A shunned place: full of disquiet
and unease (page 428), not actively dangerous but where it’s easy to
draw upon their power
4-6
A hungry place: full of whispers
and visions (page 428), calling
the unwholesome, trapping and
consuming the unwary
7-9
A poisonous place: full of unnatural
phenomena (page 429), corrupting
those who visit
10-11 A spawning place: where emanations (page 436) congeal and
manifest
12
A wound in the world: spreading
rot, bleeding horror, getting worse

The Things Below
The dangers from a lower level of severity
can be found in higher levels, too. A hungry place is full of whispers and visions but
perhaps disquiet and unease as well.
If a corrupted site will be a source of
ongoing trouble, write it up as a threat
(a MacGuffin), with an impending doom
and grim portents that reflect it getting
worse. Look to the list of causes (previous
page) for ideas about what could make a
corrupted site worse, and what sort of new
dangers it might start to manifest.
Cleansing corrupted sites
The Things Below are loathe to relinquish
what power they have in the world, but they
can be bested with bravery, cunning, and
knowledge. Some PCs (like the Blessed)
will have powers or arcana that let them
bind or contain the evil in a place. Absent
such power, the PCs will need to Make a
Plan. Consider such requirements as:
		 You must learn the name of the Thing
Below that taints this place
		 You must find the seed of corruption
within the site
		 You must create/obtain __, with which
to bind the evil (next column)
		 You must repair the bindings that
previously held back the corruption
		 You’ll need the help of a powerful
spirit/Fae
		 It’ll take days/weeks of prayer and
ritual cleansing
		 You must wait until high noon/the
solstice/the equinox/the full moon
		 The best you can do is stop it from
getting worse
		 It will require burning sacks of bendis
root (Value 2)
		 You’ll risk being corrupted yourselves
		 You’ll risk making things much worse

To bind corruption, one might use…
… a tree, especially an elder tree;
… a large piece of stone/crystal/amber/
dark ice (page 322);
… a ring of runes, deeply etched;
… a spike/vessel of bronze, orichalcum
(page 162), or black iron (page 307)
… a Fae domain (page 102);
… a barrow/cairn/vault/urn/vessel, sealed
and sanctified;
… a perpetual holy flame;
… the bones of a pure soul, properly
carved and prepared;
… some combination of the above; or
… something else entirely.
The PCs are following the river Sruth
to its source. I’ve decided that they’ll pass
a corrupted site along the river. I’ve no
particulars in mind, so I roll a 6 for feature (tomb/barrow/memorial of the locals
or their ancestors). That makes me think
of the Barrow Builders (page 34).
For themes, I roll 6 (destruction/chaos/
ruin/ignorance with “fire, ash”) and 8
(corruption/disease/decay with “vermin,
poison, acid, filth”). I roll a 2 for the cause
(attempt to wield primordial power), and
a 3 for severity (a shunned place).
I discard my first instinct to make it a
sorcerer’s tomb and decide instead that
it’s a cairn on a low hill, overlooking
the river. Inside lie the remains of a
sorcerer-king’s child. The sorcerer tried
to call them back from beyond the Last
Door, but succeeded only in blighting
the hillside with fell power. To this day,
the stones are hot to the touch, steaming in rain and snow. Nothing grows
there, and a swath of sickly, barren soil
runs down the hill to the river, where it
becomes bubbling, poisonous muck.
423

The Things Below

Artifacts
Various treasures

		 Red crystal
Pulsing, warm, and thirsting for blood, like
cruelty solidified. Red crystal is associated
with the most violent and hateful of the
Things Below. It grows in places they have
corrupted, sometimes jutting from the
earth in prodigious quantities. Creatures
corrupted by the Things Below might have
claws or fangs or antlers made of the stuff.
Red crystal has subtle effects, but can be
put to many uses. For example:
		Those with power over others find it
fascinating; the victimized find it terrifying (plenty of people find it both)
		It causes tempers to flare, stokes resentment, pushes folks to act on impulse
		It absorbs any blood it touches,
growing warmer and glowing brighter
		Sorcerers can fashion it into potent
talismans, or use it to fuel their magic
		Demon-hunters sometimes use it to
lure their prey into traps

424

		A crude stone figurine of some beast,
the claws and fangs exaggerated and
seemingly stained with blood (Value 0)
		A pot full of dust (3 uses, fragile,
Value 1 to the right person) that causes
a racking cough, weakness, maybe even
paralysis
		A lantern (5 hours, reach, area,
magical, Value 1) made of twisted black
metal; its flame casts pale light that
leaches color and chills the air
		A chunk of red crystal (previous
column), shaped to look like a leering
skull (Value 1 to the right person)
		A
large tome of supple vellum
(best not to ask the source) full of ramblings in an archaic dialect, unsettling
images, and arcane diagrams (Value 2)
		A human(ish) skull but with six eye
sockets and jutting lower tusks, carved
with disturbing patterns and inlaid
with silver (Value 3 to the right buyer)
		A twisted elder tree, found with deer
licking at its sap and ignoring the
approach of threats; the sap can be
refined into a highly addictive drug

The Things Below

A black menhir
					magical, immobile
A number of these megaliths have appeared in the Great Wood (page 200) of
late. They are jagged chunks of basalt, tall
as a tall lad, etched with intricate patterns
that could be writing, almost. The earth
around them is broken and disturbed. They
are unmarred by moss or lichen, untouched
by shoot or vine.
When you study the intricate markings and
let your mind wander, you receive a vision
or intuition about the current situation.
The GM will describe it. But first, roll
+WIS: on a 10+, pick only 1; on a 7-9, pick
2; on a 6-, alas, all 3 are true.
		Part of what you learn is false or
misleading (but what?)
		You start to suffer nightmares, of
horrors shifting in the earth
		The menhir asks a question about your
shame, guilt, or fears—answer truly

A deep, dark shaft
					 A hole in the ground, maybe 5 feet
wide, at least 100 feet deep, ending in a
watery chamber or underground lake that
contains a Mouth of Daagon (page 55).
When you linger near the shaft, answer one
of the following:
		 Of whom are you most jealous, and why?
		 What do you wish to possess, more
than anything?
		 When did you last feel trapped
and helpless?
When you toss something down the shaft,
Daagon devours it utterly. When someone
falls down the shaft, they are gone, body
and soul.
When you feed the Mouth of Daagon someone or something beloved (by anyone, not
just you), Daagon gives you a gift.
1d6

gift

1

A physical change (scales, rubbery
flesh, gills, a tentacle, etc.)
An item of luxury or status (Value 2)
A precious item (Value 3)
A “priceless” item (Value 4)
A way to best your rivals or defeat
your enemies
A way to acquire great power or
long life

2
3
4
5
6

Whatever the gift, it arrives soon: while
you sleep, in your dreams, or via vision or
coincidence or happy accident.

425

The Things Below

An iron spider
					magical, indestructible
About the size of one’s palm, half an inch
thick, rusted around the edges, with legs
folded up on the back. A hidden button
makes the legs extend and reveals their
bladed tips. Sometimes, when you go to
retrieve it, it’s not quite where you left it.
The spider is hungry when you find it.
When you plunge the hungry spider into a
person’s flesh, they lose 1d4 HP every few
seconds until it’s torn free (1d8 damage,
messy, ignores armor). Should the spider
drain them to 0 HP, it becomes sated.
When you plunge the sated spider into a
person’s flesh, they take 1 damage (ignores
armor) and will not age for 1d10 years. If
they are middle aged or older, they regain
some vigor of their youth. Then, the spider
becomes hungry.
Each time someone uses the spider to extend their life, their features change subtly,
becoming blander, waxier, more pallid, less
specific. Should they die after exceeding
their mortal lifespan, they become a revenant (page 80), hungering for blood.

An obsidian pendant
					magical
A lump of volcanic glass, impossibly black,
set in a pendant and hanging from a
leather cord. The glass sucks up light from
nearby sources, causing them to dim. (The
sun and moon are unaffected, as they are
far, far away.) More subtly, the pendant also
sucks up kindness, making individuals in
its presence more callous and self-centered.
When you wear or carry the pendant
openly for a few minutes or more, your eyes
adjust and can see clearly in all but pitch
darkness. But also, your instinct for the
session becomes “Callousness: to disregard
the feelings, needs, or wellbeing of others.”
A serpentine armband
					magical, beautiful, Value 3
Cast from silver that never seems to tarnish,
with what appear to be two small rubies in
its eye sockets. It seems like it might not
fit—but what do you know, it does.
When you wear the serpentine armband openly for all to see, NPCs find you
magnetic, becoming jealous and possessive
of your attention. Tempers flare quickly,
and you might need to Defy Danger or
Persuade others in order to take your leave.
Ask each PC you encounter how the armband affects them.
When you remove the armband after
wearing it and basking in the regard of
others, you find yourself pining for that
feeling of being adored. Roll +CON: on a
10+, you’ll get over it; on a 7-9, either put
the armband back on (and keep it there) or
mark a debility; on a 6-, you cannot sleep
until you put the armband on and wear it
openly around others.

426

The Things Below

A silken noose
					magical
A length of fine cord, tied into a noose
with a few feet hanging free. It’s remarkably tough, but a bronze blade will slice
right through it. If untied and left unattended, it re-ties itself into a noose.
When you slip the noose around someone’s
neck, it cinches tight—snug and uncomfortable, but the victim can breathe.
When your victim lies or refuses to answer
a question, the noose tightens and strangles
them until they die or gasp out the truth.
When you (or anyone else) tries to remove
or loosen the noose, it starts to strangle the
victim. If you (or they) stop, it relaxes. If
you (or they) persist, it quickly crushes the
victim’s windpipe.
When the victim dies, the noose slackens
and can be removed.

427

The Things Below

Dangers
Disquiet and unease
					Places touched by the Things Below
are wrong. Most people and natural beasts
shun such places, and spirits of the wild
either flee or become tainted themselves.
As you describe the environment or make
any of the GM moves below, show off the
themes (page 416) and instinct (page
417) of the Thing Below behind all this.
ä		 Introduce something ominous (a cold
spot, a dramatic gust of wind, mist/fog,
a foul stench, etc.)
ä		 Make something seem worse than it is
ä		 Imply that allies might betray them
ä		 Ask about their fears/doubts/shames/
animosities
ä		 Have an NPC get edgy/act weird/
freak out
ä		 Reveal a lack of natural spirits, or only
unsavory ones, plus a hateful power
that yearns to be called forth

Whispers and visions

					Where the influence of the Things

Below is strong, people hear voices, see
things that aren’t there, experience powerful emotions.
As you describe the environment or make
any of the GM moves below, show off the
themes (page 416) and instinct (page
417) of the Thing Below behind all this.
ä		 Twist their senses
ä		 Lure them deeper/closer
ä		 Keep them from sleeping
ä		 Fill them with growing dread
ä		 Overwhelm them with terror
(next page)
ä		 Stir an unsavory emotion (anger,
jealousy, despair, hate, resentment, etc.)
ä		 Whisper a suggestion or compulsion
ä		 Show an NPC struggling to keep it
together, to keep control
ä		 Have an NPC succumb to the whispers/
their fear/their emotions
When you do as the whispers suggest, mark
XP and they can now compel you.
When the whispers compel you, mark XP if
you do as they bid. If you resist, roll +WIS:
on a 10+, do as you wish; on a 7-9, choose 1:
		Act as compelled, but divert yourself at
the last moment
		Struggle for control until someone/
something snaps you out of it
		Hurt yourself (1d4 damage, ignores
armor) and immediately regain control
On a 6-, pick 1:
		Mark a debility, lose 1d4 HP, and do
something drastic to regain control
		Run with it, coming to later having
done gods-know-what

The Things Below

Terror
					When you are overwhelmed with
terror, and do anything other than flee,
hide, or cower, pick 1:
		Work yourself up to the task (ask the
GM what happens in the meantime)
		Act rashly and recklessly (incurring
additional cost or consequence)
		Mark a debility of the GM’s choice,
and act immediately

Unnatural phenomena
					As the corruption of a site grows
stronger, the world starts to twist and
unravel. As you describe the environment or
make any of the GM moves below, show off
the themes (page 416) and instinct (page
417) of the Thing Below behind all this.
ä		 Present something impossible and
disturbing (bleeding walls, water pooling on the ceiling, darkness that light
won’t penetrate, etc.)
ä		 Have light sources dim/sputter/snuff out
ä		 Reveal that food has spoiled/drink
has fouled
ä		 Inflict a debility (from fear/cold/ennui/
hunger/fatigue/nausea/despair/etc.)
ä		 Eat away at their gear, have it fail for
no good reason
ä		 Trap them in a maze of time/space/
perception
ä		 Threaten/hinder/grab/tear at them
with the environment
ä		 Corrupt them (page 430)

429

The Things Below

Corruption
					Threat (affliction)
Instinct to make monstrous
The power of the Things Below can infuse
a person, beast, or spirit, corrupting them.
This could happen when they…
…		 call on the Things Below for power,
intentionally or not
…		 spend time in a corrupted site (page
422), one that’s poisonous or worse
…		 attempt to cleanse someone or something of corruption, and things go wrong
…		 are harmed/infected by an artifact
(page 424) or emanation (page 436)
of the Things Below, by one of those
who bear their taint (next page), or by
a corrupted being (page 432).
Add these to the list of GM moves for
afflictions:
ä		 Give them a gift (page 432), or
increase one’s potency
ä		 Inflict a mark upon them (page 432),
or make one worse

PCs and corruption
If a PC possesses a major arcana
associated with the Things Below
(page 427), then the arcana’s mysteries and consequences reflect the
PC’s growing corruption. Likewise,
the Thrall playbook insert (Book I,
page 152) reflects a PC being corrupted by the Things Below. If a PC
has one of those arcana or inserts,
allow its mechanics to drive their
corruption. Don’t give them additional gifts or marks unless they are
corrupted in some additional way.
Should a PC become corrupted by
the Things Below without gaining a
major arcana or insert, then ask the
player how they want to handle it.
Do they see the corruption as a threat
to their character, something to rid
themselves of and struggle against? If
so, write it up as a threat (threat type:
affliction) and focus on increasingly
problematic marks. But if the player
wants to explore the power that’s
now available to them, and the cost
of that power, then create a playbook
insert for them, using the Thrall
insert as inspiration.
