Consider these gifts and marks in light
of the Thing and original being. How has
their corruption made them both more and
less than what they once were? Revise their
stats, tags, qualities, and moves to reflect all
of the above. Add the corrupted tag.
If the being is a spirit, alter its manifestation to reflect the themes and aspects of
the Thing that corrupts it. Bronze weapons
can always harm a manifest corrupted
spirit, and might ignore its armor.
How has corruption changed the being’s
behavior? Are they merely a conduit for their
master’s instinct? Do they struggle to maintain their own identity? Has their corruption
combined with their original personality in
an unusual way? Rewrite their instinct to suit.

The Things Below

The Seasons Change roll indicates that a threat makes itself known or gets worse. I decide that
Iwan, an aggrieved villager, has unwittingly started to dally with a corrupted spirit. I like
the idea of an evil glasbren (page 366) sensing Iwan’s resentments and “courting” him.
I start by working up the glasbren’s tree as a corrupted site. I get 4 for a feature (signs of
great destruction), an 11 for the cause (seal/binding weakened and roll 1d10 again), and
a 4 on the reroll (an artifact/remains festering). For severity, I roll a 9 (a poisonous place).
I think some evil artifact was bound by an old tree in the glasbren’s grove, but a fire swept
through and only the glasbren survived. It’s deeply scarred and corrupted by the artifact.
Looking at the names of Things Below, my eye catches on “Ta’l Themyin (Burning Child,
Cruel Infant, Prince of Rage).” I pick themes of “destruction/chaos/ruin/ignorance (fire,
ash, dust)” and “cruelty/torture/violence/rage (red crystal, spilled blood).” I pick aspects of
“fire/light/haze/cloud/vortex/void” and “a youth/a child/an infant.” For instinct, I pick
“to inflict harm, cruelly and unnecessarily.” I think the Red Scepter (page 558) is the
artifact buried in the glasbren’s grove!
I pick gifts of “immunity to certain harms” (fire), “serendipity/luck/hexes/misfortune, bestowed on others,” and “vicious/terrible physical attacks” (a fiery grasp). I think it is marked
by “strange appetites” (burnt and bloody offerings). Combining these elements with the
glasbren’s curiosity and penchant for bonding with mortals, I end up with this stat block:

CORRUPTED GLASBREN

Solitary, spirit, devious, magical,
fiery, beautiful, corrupted
HP 19; ARMOR 1 (lacks organs)
DAMAGE searing grasp d10 (hand,
			grabby)
SPECIAL QUALITIES tethered to its
tree, fireproof
INSTINCT to spread cruelty, pain, fire
> Travel freely between fires,
		burnt trees
>		 Sense resentment, anger, wrath,
		and cruelty
>		 Manifest a beautiful form of
cinders and flame (unharmed by
stabbing, vulnerable to water
and bronze)
> Stoke the flames of conflict
> Offer affection/confidence/power
in exchange for “gifts” of fire
		and blood

There is a burnt and blasted place, where
the Steplands (page 372) abut Ferrier’s
Fen (page 116). It once boasted a Stone
Lord (page 382) fortress: grand, a tower
eternal, lit by a fiery gem. But the servants
rebelled. The seals were sundered. Ta’l
Themyin, the Burning Child, shattered the
gem and toppled the tower and laughed
and laughed and laughed. His rage still
boils to the surface, poisoning any beast
bold enough to forage in these ruins. His
cruelty sends them forth, to uproot and
topple, to sunder and burn.

The spirits of even shallow ponds can be
callous and uncaring. How much worse,
then, are the spirits of those deeper pools
that have claimed the lives of mortals?
For—willing or not—they have made
offering to Daagon, and let it in to their
watery hearts.
When you listen to the morgais’ song or let
your gaze linger on its graceful form, your
heart fills with longing. If you go to it willingly, mark XP. If you wish to turn away,
roll +WIS: on a 10+, you see the ugliness
beneath and can think clearly; on a 7-9,
you shake yourself free but the longing
remains—mark miserable or dazed (your
choice); on a 6-, you approach before you
realize what you’re doing.

Folks are capable of all sorts of dark things,
if someone else suggests they do them. Especially if they’re desperate. Especially if the
person leading them is someone they trust,
or respect, or admire. And if that first little
dark deed comes without any consequences,

next dark deed might not be so little, hmm?

When the Last Door stands open before
you, what will you do? Some step through
bravely, with a nod to the ﻿Lady of Crows
(page 71). Others stagger through, weeping
and wailing. Others—consumed by
longing, vengeance, or duty—spit on fate,
turn their back on the Last Door, and cling
to this world for a time.
But what if… you simply want to live?
What if, when faced with oblivion, you
called upon one of the Things Below and
offered yourself up to them?
What if they said yes?

Some seek out corruption, embrace it.
They might not have chosen the path, but
at some point they chose to keep walking,
believing the power on offer was worth the
cost. Some think they are in control. And
perhaps they are—for now.
Sorcerers are often in possession of one or
more arcana (page 508), which they do
not hesitate to use. They may or may not be
followed by cultists (see above).

The victims of L’bin’bozia (Flesh-Candle, the Wax Skull, Roiling Tomb) find
themselves turned into… things. Furniture.
Decoration. Masses of flesh where it’s hard
to see where one poor soul ends and the
other begins. They do not die of old age.
They cannot starve. They must endure,
trapped in the cave or the mire or the
tomb, or wherever they met their fate. They
must persist, in pain, begging for some
outsider to come and end their suffering.

Emanation
					 The Things Below are well and truly
trapped—bound within the earth, to the
earth. Even during the Time of Cataclysm,
they did not walk free. They are tethered as
long as the world remains.
But there are cracks in the world. Places
where their influence bubbles up. Deep
water is the surest of these, which is why it
is often filled with horrors. But any place
can bear their taint.
The power of the Things Below usually
just corrupts what’s already there. But
sometimes, it coalesces. It creates something new, something terrible. A spirit or
being with a will and purpose of its own.
Scholars call these “emanations.” They are
not truly part of a Thing Below. They are...
discharge. Leavings. Spawn.
To create an emanation, identify the Thing
Below it spawned from, including its themes
(page 416), aspects, and instinct (page
417). Identify, too, the corrupted site (page
422) it inhabits. Pick or roll for its origin.
1d12
1-2

origin

Arose in the aftermath of great
devastation/atrocity
3-4
Burst forth from a corrupted being
(page 432) that absorbed too
much power
5-6
Called forth, via a terrible ritual
7-8
Given shape by worship/adoration/
feeding
9-10 Let into the world by accident/
hubris, a failed attempt to bind/
cleanse/wield the power of the
Things Below
11-12 Spawned spontaneously from a
truly corrupted site (page 422)
436

The Things Below
Imagine the emanation that forms as a
result. It will share at least some of the
themes and aspects of the Thing from
which it spawned, and might share its
instinct. An emanation has its own memory, arguably its own personality. It might
communicate with its progenitor, or do its
bidding, but an emanation is its own thing.
Hand of Daagon
					Solitary, large, emanation, terrifying,
cautious, curious
HP 23; Armor 4 (resilience), 0 vs. bronze
Damage many hands d12+1 (hand, close,
grabby, ignores armor)
Special qualities blind; tremorsense;
reaches through matter like it was water
Instinct to feed the Mouth
			ä Sink into the stone, or emerge from it
			ä Provoke abject revulsion
			ä Reach through armor, clothing, flesh,
bone to take what is most valued
			ä Retreat with its prize, back to
the water
In a deep deep cave, where light has never
shined, there is a lake. In that lake, there is
a Mouth of Daagon: ever-hungry, evergreedy, consuming all that it is offered.
And in the caverns around that lake? There
you will find the Hand: ever-groping,
ever-grasping, dragging all it finds of value
back to the Mouth.

Voice of the Eternal Maw
					Solitary, spirit, emanation, devious,
terrifying, hungry
HP 19; Armor 4 (resilience), 0 vs. bronze
Damage scream d10 (near, area, reload,
ignores armor) or unnaturally large and
toothy maw d10+2 (hand, messy,
3 piercing)
Special qualities tethered to the vortex,
unable to leave the mountain
Instinct to create more mouths,
more wraiths
			ä Compel someone with whispers (see
page 428), even from afar
			ä Possess a traveler, especially one who
is hungry, weary, or tired
			ä Drive its host to feast
			ä Leave its host corrupted, withering,
doomed to become a wraith (page 79)
Hidden in the southern arm of the Huffel
Peaks, there is a gorge that splits a mountain. The gorge is cloaked in constantly
churning mist, cold and clammy. At the
base of the gorge is a thin split in reality,
a vortex, a manifestation of Hlad the Devourer, sucking in everything it touches.
The mountain and the gorge are shunned
now. The Hillfolk (page 228) and the
Ustrina (page 464) who live nearby know
better than to approach. But the Barrow
Builders (page 34) fed the vortex. They
brought it gifts, and offerings, and living,
screaming sacrifice. In time, these offerings
gave the vortex a Voice. And the Voice
roams the mountain still.

437

Three Coven Lake

Deep in the Steplands (page 372) lies a
large lake, eight miles long by three. The
waters are murky, often choppy, shallow in
some places, in others 100 feet deep.
Rocky islands poke from the water. Bluffs
line much of the shore. But look closer.
Those islands are the feet of ancient towers,
and those bluffs are riddled with rockcut rooms. For long ago, in the days of
the Stone Lords (page 382), this was a
mighty city. Teirstered, they called it. The
City of Three Stars, rivalled only by great
Dulenn (page 48).
Picture it! See it in your mind! The stone
and crystal spires, rising from the lake.
Their glowing tips of makerglass (page
388), shining in the night. The walkways,
stretched between the towers, so that one
might walk the valley’s length without ever
setting foot on shore.
Those walkways were more than just
convenience. They formed a mighty rune,
a seal that helped to bind the power of the
Things Below (page 412). The ceaseless
march of countless feet powered the rune,
like how water turns a wheel.
438

When the Stone Lords’ servants rebelled,
they sank the city of Dulenn in one night.
But here, in Teirstered, things took longer.
Mobs rose up against their masters and
besieged them in their towers. They broke
the walkways, called up horrors from the
deep. Towers swayed, then crumbled, then
fell beneath the waves.
Soon, the city lay in ruins. But it was not
abandoned. The Barrow Builders (page
34) fought over its bones. They sought
the treasures of their fallen masters, and
to seat themselves upon their thrones.
Their armies spilled each others’ blood. The
waters drank it up.
In time, there was little left to fight over.
In time, the Things Below withdrew. The
tyrants of the Barrow Builders were cast
down, or imprisoned, or left alone to rot.
Teirstered, the City of Three Stars, was
no more.
But the blood-drunk waters of Three
Coven Lake remained.

Three Coven Lake

Impressions

The Three Covens

		So… much… water… (gulp)
		Calm water: reflections of the bluffs
		Choppy water: cold and dark, the
angry churn and spray
		Broken buildings, glimpsed beneath
the water
		Echoes off the bluffs: waves, voices,
birdcalls, mournful screeches
		At night: motes of light in the deep
When chilly: fog—thick, wet, gloomy
		In winter: a white expanse, frozen solid,
windy, slick, snow-covered

Three lights can be seen every night, like
ghostly flames: one among the northern
bluffs, one on the south shore, the third
upon (below?) the waters. Hillfolk (page
228) call them “the Covens” or the “the
Witch Lights.” They hesitate to say more.
Who or what are the Covens? That’s for
you to decide. But perhaps they…
…		 burn in memory of the three conspiracies, or covins, that rose up against
the Stone Lords, and the witches who
led each one;
…		 were once the “Three Stars” of
Tierstered, makerglass gems burning
with eternal flame;
…		 are banefires (page 42), each one
imprisoning an undead sorcerer;
…		 are spirit-catchers, enthralling ghosts
and keeping them bound;
…		 are ghostly hordes standing vigil over
mass graves; or
…		 are some combination of the above; or
…		 something else entirely.
439

Three Coven Lake

Hooks

Areas

		A foe of the PCs makes their lair near
the lake
		An ally went to explore the lake but
never returned
		Hillfolk are gathering near the lake, in
large numbers
		The PCs acquire the key to the Vault
of Skol Paasco (see next page)
		Someone aims to destroy the dam
(draining the lake, flooding the Sruth,
dooming Marshedge)

		The Gwazhi: A sizeable river that
feeds the lake, flowing from the Huffel
Peaks (page 236).
		 Surrounding hills and valleys: Once
full of farms, mines, villages, manors.
Site of many Barrow Builder battles.
		 Western shores: Where the Gwazhi
enters the lake. Marshy, silty, gross.
Frogs, birds, bugs.
		 Northern shore: Piles of boulders and
a great looming bluff. Treacherous
shorelines.
		 Southern shore: Gentler (but still
rugged) shorelines, with smaller bluffs
a ways back from the water.
		 Eastern shore: Deep water. Sheer
bluffs joined by a Stone Lord dam.
		 The Lake itself: Shallow near shores
but plenty of deep, open water. Hides
many dangers and many treasures.
Freezes over in winter.

Getting there
To get there directly from Stonetop…
		 First you must travel to the Steplands,
which will take at least 4 days.
		 From there, it will take at least 3 days
and a corresponding amount of supplies.
		 You need a knowledgeable guide; but
		 Even then, you risk getting lost.
To get there from Marshedge (page 274),
follow the River Sruth upstream to
the lake, and…
		 It will take at least 4 days, and a corresponding amount of supplies; and
		 You need a knowledgeable guide or you
risk getting lost.
Either way…
		 The terrain itself is treacherous, you
risk injury on the way;
		 The way is grueling, you risk exhausting
yourselves/your resources; or
		 The way is perilous, plagued with danger.

440

Three Coven Lake

Sites

Terrain

		The dam: A mighty wall between two
bluffs on the eastern shore. Granite
blocks, mortared in place with makerglass. A huge pipe drains water into
the river Sruth. Beware the whirlpool
(page 445).
		Glassworks: The westernmost bluff
on the south shore, hollowed out. Partly collapsed, wholly looted, still full of
cold, echoing furnaces and huge globs
of makerglass.
		 The Academy: Part of the north bluff.
Maker-sized dormitories, workshops,
and lecture halls. Gutted and vandalized. Used by Barrow Builders as a
fortress.
		Amphitheater: Carved into a bluff,
overlooking a large cove on the southern bank. Impossibly good acoustics.
The site of countless bloody sacrifices.
		 The Vault of Skol Paasco: A large,
rocky isle in the middle of the lake, a
tower’s foundation clearly visible. A
heavily-warded makerglass door guards
the untouched cellars.

Pick 1 or combine 2, or have someone roll
the Die of Fate (d12). If you like, roll 1d6
for size/scope/intensity.

Three Coven Lake

Dangers
Natural hazards
		A stretch of water and reason to brave it
		A bluff to climb and a reason to climb it
		Poor footing (mud, unstable rubble,
scree, slick wet stone, etc.)
		Rockslides or mudslides (page 376)
		A gap or barrier in a path/walkway
		Jagged rocks to fall on
		Swarms of midges or mosquitos—
annoying, distracting, painful, diseased
Unnatural hazards
		Unstable construction (page 390)
from the days of the Stone Lords
		Perilous stone (page 391)
		Traps (page 42) left by the Barrow
Builders
		 Corrupted sites (page 422) and all
that they entail
		 Cursed objects (page 90)
		Darkness and the risk of getting lost,
deep inside the bluffs
The lake itself
		Instinctive dread, from a generational
fear of water
		Steep, unexpected drop-offs
		Poor footing (slick rocks, mud, plants
and other snares)
		Strong, often unseen currents
		Choppy water, waves up to 4’ on
extremely windy days
		Shivering cold, the threat of debilities
or even hypothermia (page 489)
		Getting dragged down by your own gear
		The fact that they probably can’t swim
See also: The Blood-drunk Queen
(page 447).
444

Drowning
					When you plunge beneath the water,
hold Breath equal to 1 + your CON. If
you first took a deep breath, hold 2 extra
Breath.
When you make a move while under water,
and that move involves a roll, choose 1 of
the following after the move is resolved:
		Spend 1 Breath
		Mark a debility
		Drop to 0 HP; you’re drowning and
soon to be at Death’s Door
Thin ice
					 The lake starts to freeze in early
winter and thaws in early spring. It’s
usually frozen at least 4” thick (safe enough
to walk on) by midwinter. But it doesn’t
freeze uniformly; it can be 2’ thick in one
place and just a few inches thick only a few
yards away.
ä		 Footsteps fall with a hollow noise
ä		 Water/slush well up around your feet
ä		 Something underfoot cracks, lurches
ä		 SPLOOSH
Anyone falling through the ice risks panic
and drowning. If they manage to escape,
they’re probably already suffering hypothermia (page 489).

Three Coven Lake

The whirlpool
					 Near the dam at the east edge of the
lake, a great drain draws in water and empties it into the river Sruth. When one or
more PCs get too close, start the following:
l		 They’re caught in the current
l		 They’re dragged closer, and can no

longer swim/paddle free

l		 Down the funnel they go
l		 Impending doom: They are sucked

into the pipe

When you get sucked into the pipe, take
1d10+2 damage with advantage (ignores armor) and roll +CON: on a 10+, you get spat
out into the river, alive but soaked and beaten
(mark a debility); on a 7-9, you get spat out
but mark a debility and lose 1d4 4 from
your inventory and 1d4 small items; on a 6-,
as a 7-9 but also you are at Death’s Door.
People
		Hillfolk (page 228): exiled, on patrol,
on the hunt, on a rite of passage, etc.
		Antiquarians (page 260), adventurers
(page 260), or legionaries (page 263),
exploring the ruins
		Bandits (page 355), seeking shelter or
after some big score
Witches or their ilk
		A necromancer (page 84), treating
with the many many dead
		A hdour (page 234) or sorcerer (page
435), seeking/hoarding power
		Cultists (page 435) and/or a thrall
(page 435), living among the ruins
		A gouzadn (page 378), quite possibly
corrupted (page 430)
		A deathless one (page 46), who’s
been here all along

Stone Lord magic

Three Coven Lake

The Chained Beast
Solitary, large, corrupted, aquatic,
terrifying
HP 20; Armor 2 (fur and scales)
Damage toothy maw d10+3 w/advantage
(close, grabby, messy, 1 piercing) or
raking claws d10+1 (hand, messy, 1
piercing)
Special qualities chained to a heavy block
of makerglass, takes +1d6 damage from
bronze, soothed by a well-sung song
Instinct to gorge itself
ä		 Scent blood, anywhere in the lake
ä		 Shoot through the water
ä		 Snatch prey, pull it under
ä		 Regenerate any wound not dealt
by bronze
ä		 Drag its mooring across the
lakebed, slowly and painfully
It was a gift from the Things Below, a present to some Barrow Builder tyrant. They
bound it fast and kept in the waters nearby,
feeding it a steady of diet of screaming,
bleeding victims. It’s still out there, sleeping for years or decades at a time, roused
by copious amounts of fresh blood in the
lake—or by a few drops somewhere nearby.

446

Screech eel
					Horde, small, aquatic, stealthy, cautious
HP 3; Armor 1 (speed), 2 vs. attacks from
above the water
Damage tearing bite d6 (hand, messy,
1 piercing)
Instinct to feed on easy prey
ä		 Slink up on potential prey
ä		 Screech to attract other eels (barely
heard above the water)
ä		 Attack in a frenzied swarm, all bites
and screeches and churning bloody
water
About 5’ or 6’ long. They mostly prey on
fish or fowl, hunting silently and by themselves. Somehow, though, they learned that
people are delicious. Too big for one eel
to tackle, sure. But people in the water are
clumsy and oblivious. So when a screech
eel spots someone in the water, it calls out
to its friends. And when enough of them
have gathered, they feast.

Three Coven Lake

The Blood-drunk Queen
Threat (magical entity)
Instinct to slake her thirst for violence
and suffering
ä		 Appear in dreams, or to those who
are drowning: a gaunt and longlimbed queen, her mouth stained
crimson
ä		 Wash up a secret/treasure, to tempt
fools or stir up violence
ä		 Turn the lake against those who
have not appeased her
The lake has always had a spirit. A fickle
goddess, beautiful and bountiful, sometimes
harsh and greedy. But then the walkway-seal
was broken, and horror seeped into the
waters. Then the multitudes were drowned,
and the goddess drank their deaths. Then
came generations of violence, and she
lapped up all the blood that was spilled.

When you make a bloody offering to the
spirit of the lake, roll and add…
…		 +1 if you do it at a proper shrine;
…		 +1 if the victim dies in agony & fear;
and
…		 +1 if you spill the lifeblood of a
human-sized creature, or +2 if you spill
substantially more than that.
On a 7+, you have appeased the spirit of
the lake and She will not actively torment
you or your companions (for this visit, at
least). Also, on a 10+, hold 2 Preparation
(per the Bolster move), which you or a
companion can spend on any roll related to
exploring or navigating the lake, or facing
its dangers.

447

Titan Bones

448

Titan Bones

Impressions
		The hill on the horizon, never seeming
to get closer until it’s right up on you
		Those bones, bigger than they ought to
be, bigger than any beast you’ve seen
		A sense of smallness, insignificance
		Southerly wind: ceaseless, buffeting
		Riders approaching, the anticipation as
you wait

About two days’ hike from Stonetop stands
a lone hill, rising from the ﻿F lats (page
126). A huge, fossilized skeleton is visible
on one side of the hill.
The Highway (page 270) runs past the
hill to the east; a wayside sits at its foot.
The hill rises over 300’ above the prairie.
The northern, leeward face is steep, rocky,
cliff-like. The other sides are rugged but
manageable slopes, covered in sparse,
gnarled oaks and smaller brush.
The crown is flat, sparsely wooded, the
highest point for nigh on 100 miles. When
Stonetop or the Hillfolk (page 228) need
to signal each other, they light a great bonfire up there, on the top of Titan Bones.

Tip: Up top, portray NPCs with short,
loud sentences, like they’re shouting over
the wind. Ask, “What?” if players don’t do
the same. Down at the wayside, keep looking up and away, like NPCs are distracted
by the hill.

Questions
		 Which of you has been (t)here before?
Why?
		 Do the bones peek out of the sheer
rock face, or jut from the southern
slopes, or lie flat on the crown, or what?
		 According to the tales, what are they
bones of ? How did they come to
be here?
		 What superstitions surround the
bones? What little rites do folks observe in their presence?
		 What other oddities have you found on
or around the hill?
449

Titan Bones

Meeting place
A footpath leads from the wayside, climbing
the rugged slope in a series of switchbacks.
It takes half an hour to reach the crown
at a reasonable pace, but a fit person could
charge straight up in just a few minutes (at
considerable risk of injury or mishap).
When the PCs wish to summon the Hillfolk to a meeting, they must:
		 Travel to Titan Bones and climb the hill
		 Wait for a clear day, with no rain or haze
		 Spend the best part of a day gathering
fuel and stoking a bonfire
		 Tend and feed the bonfire all through
the night and into the early morn
		 Wait for the Hillfolk to arrive
Choose or roll for how long before the
Hillfolk arrive, what happens while they
wait, and the disposition of the Hillfolk
who show up. It’s usually a small group of
riders (page 234) who respond to the signal,
maybe with a spirit-talker (page 235).

4 to 5 days

Should the Hillfolk light a signal fire
themselves, folks in Stonetop will likely see
the pillar of smoke first, then the fire as the
sun sets. If it burns through the night and
into the morning, that means the Hillfolk
wish to meet.
Meetings take place on the wayside at the
foot of the hill, under the protection of the
pavestones’ wards against violence (page
270). Hillfolk will assume that anything
else is a trap.
		 How often do meetings like this occur?
		 When was the last meeting called? By
whom? What was it about?
		 With which group of Hillfolk do y’all
normally meet?
		 How do you and the Hillfolk traditionally greet each other? What other
customs do you observe?
		 Where exactly do folks typically light a
signal fire? What’s special about it?
		 What else can be found up on the
hill’s crown?

Titan Bones

The titan’s bones
The fossilized bones on the hill are clearly
ancient, and far bigger than anything has
a right to be. But what are they the bones
of ? That’s for you and your group to decide, but perhaps they are the bones of…
		
… one of the three great beasts vanquished
by Tor (page 452);
…		 one of the First Creators (page 299),
slain by the Things Below (page 412)
as they were imprisoned;
…		 some fell beast brewed up by the
Green Lords (page 210);
…		 a mighty Stone Lord construct (page
392), not a creature at all;
… a colossal ﻿emanation (page 436) of the
Things Below, a world-ending horror,
﻿vitrified by ﻿Stormcatcher’s (page 332)
magic;
…		 some combination of the above; or
…		 something else entirely.

Ruins of the past
Today, Titan Bones is little more than a
landmark and a meeting place, but what
was it in the past? That’s for you to decide,
but perhaps it was once…
… a site sacred to the ﻿Makers (page 264),
a place of pilgrimage;
…		 a fortress or trading post of the Stone
Lords (page 382);
…		 one of the Tempest Lords’ (page 398)
sky-islands, crashed or landed and
(temporarily?) unable to fly;
…		 part of Stormcatcher’s (page 332)
domain: a lookout, a laboratory, a
power station, etc.;
…		 a fortress from the time of the Barrow
Builders (page 34), likely haunted
(page 72) from the battles fought here;

…		 a corrupted site (page 422), a place of
great evil, now mostly cleansed or at
least contained;
…		 some combination of the above; or
…		 something else entirely.
Whatever you decide, consider how that
interacts with your decisions about the
titan’s bones (see previous column). For example, if they are the bones of a great beast
and the hill was once a sky-island, did the
beast and sky-island bring each other low?
Did the beast (or its bones) power the
sky-island? Or what?

Dangers
		A nasty turn of the weather
		The nosgalau (page 272), calling out
to those on the hill
		The ghosts (page 76) of those who
died here or at nearby battlefields
		A prairie fire (page 130) lit by an
errant cinder
		Bandits (page 355), encamped on the
hill or drawn by the signal fire
		Something big and dangerous, on the
hunt or lairing on the hill, like a
beznpol (page 132), a frythanc (page
133), or a kleztigr (page 379)
		A nest of hlomren (page 134), infesting the hillside
		Spirits of the wild: a brennauwed
(page 366), a flock of andalau (page
368), or a fadyn (page 369)
		Local blue magpies (page 340), some
real jerks
		A rockslide or mudslide (page 376)
		Something inspired or suggested by the
true nature of the titan’s bones, and/or
the ruins of the past

451

Tor, the Rain-maker
Tor, the Rain-maker, also called the Thunderhead and Slayer-of-Beasts, is one of the
gods (page 178) in Stonetop’s pavilion of
the gods. He is the god of wind and rain,
weather and storms, patron of warriors
and hunters too. Many tales are told of his
victories, and yes, of his foibles, too.

Themes
Pick, or roll 1d12.
1d12

theme

1-2
3-4
5-6
7

Rain/snow/good clean water
Wind/weather/the sky/clouds
Storms/thunder/lightning
Courage/strength/prowess/endurance
Generosity/gift-giving/celebration
Exuberance/confidence/folly
Surprises/moodiness/whims
Wrath/rage/destruction
Prophecy/doom foretold

10

452

Questions
According to the tales:
		 How did Tor come to be (a god)?
What’s his origin story?
		 Which god is his rival/lover/ally/kin?
		 What three great beasts is Tor said to
have slain?
		 What natural feature did he shape in a
fit of rage?
		 From what predicament did a mortal
once rescue him?
		 What gift did he give to Stonetop/
humanity?
		 How did he lose one of his fingers?
		 What is one of the names he goes by
when he walks the world as a man?
Use the answers to help decide what Tor’s
true nature is (page 180).

Hooks
		The PCs encounter a nine-fingered
stranger (page 457).
		During a violent storm, multiple
villagers dream of a terrible beast,
unleashed.
		One of Tor’s rites is disrupted; drought
afflicts the village.
		A wandering priest of Tor arrives,
offering to cast runes and tell fortunes.
		Travelers praise some new/foreign god
of strength/storms/war, and heap insult
and derision upon Tor.

Tor

In Stonetop
Tor is the most popular god in Stonetop.
The Stone (page 12) is sometimes called
his anvil, or the quiver for his javelins. The
Cistern (page 12) is sometimes called
his drinking horn. The locals use many
colorful phrases that invoke his name.
What is Tor’s shrine like? Perhaps it is…
…		 laden with whisky and gifts, trophies
and charms;
…		 just a simple idol, for his true altar is
the Stone;
…		 home to a smoldering fire, constantly
tended;
…		 shrouded, held in awe, approached by
only the worthy;
…		 some variation on the above; or
…		 something else entirely.
Ask the PCs:
		 Who tends Tor’s shrine? Is it a formal
position (like a priest), or what?
		 How is Tor depicted?
		 What symbols adorn his shrine?
		 What do locals do to ask his blessings?
		 What do they do to avert his anger?
		 What are Tor’s high holidays? How is
each one observed?

...and elsewhere
How widespread is the worship of Tor?
That’s for you and your group to decide,
but perhaps Tor…
…		 is a local deity, Stonetop’s patron but
largely unknown elsewhere;
…		 is worshipped throughout the World’s
End, but dismissed as a barbaric god by
those from Lygos and the South
(page 256);
…		 is widely known and worshipped, and
served by a wandering order of priests;
…		 is known the world over, but often with
very different aspects;
…		 some variation on one of the above; or
…		 something else entirely.
In any other community that knows and
worships Tor, consider/ask:
		 Which of his themes (previous page)
do they emphasize most?
		 Is he gladly worshipped? Given due
respect? Feared and appeased? Or what?
		 Do they maintain a public shrine/
temple? If so, where is it and what’s
it like?
		 Who conducts rites in his honor?
		 What unfamiliar tales do they tell/rites
do they perform/symbols do they use?
If the worship of Tor is unknown to a
people: what other god or gods of the sky,
storms, weather, and/or violence do they
worship? (See page 184 for guidance.)

reach, area, forceful, loud, ignores armor)

Tor

Drought
					Threat (affliction)
Instinct to deplete resources
Rain and snow are Tor’s life-giving gifts.
But what if he withholds them? Or worse,
is unable to give them freely?
l		 The Stream (page 396) dries up, the

Cistern runs low

l		 The crops are stunted (generate Surplus

with disadvantage)

l		 Everything/one goes unwashed;

disease spreads (page 122)

l		 Impending doom: Crops and gardens

fail, livestock die/are slaughtered
(steading marks lacking and generates
no Surplus)

Something interesting: Past droughts
have been broken by making the proper
offerings/ completing some quest, but the
details are unclear—perhaps you could
commune with a spirit of the wild (page
356) to learn more?
Something useful: You know what offering needs to be made, or what quest needs
to be completed—Make a Plan with the
GM to determine the details.

Tor

Nine-fingered stranger
					Solitary, spirit, magical, enigmatic,
			moody, curious
HP 22; Armor 4 (resilience), 0 vs. black iron
Damage by weapon d10+2 (tags by weapon,
+forceful)
Special qualities unable/unwilling to solve
problems (only causes/presents them);
unaware of its true nature
Instinct to test people, set events in motion
ä Manifest as a nine-fingered man
ä Present a challenge (next column)
ä Cloud their judgement, make this all
seem reasonable
ä Bestow a (dubious?) gift or curse
ä Vanish mysteriously
Many tales tell of a stranger met in an unlikely place. The stranger is in some sort of
pickle, or challenges our hero, or presents
a tempting opportunity. Whatever else
happens, the stranger ends up vanishing,
but not before our hero notices his missing
finger—and realizes that they just met Tor.
When the tale’s hero rises to the occasion,
they win a gift from the stranger: a treasure
or a magic item, a secret or a prophecy,
or maybe just some useful bit of advice.
But when the “hero” comes up short, they
wind up cursed or “win” some treasure that
brings them ruin and woe.

When you introduce the nine-fingered
stranger, pick or roll a manifestation
and the challenge he presents. Consider,
too, what gift or curse he might dispense:
an arcanum, an answer, a bit of advice, a
blessing (e.g. hold 3 Preparation, per the
Bolster move), a curse (a threat, a magical
affliction), etc.
1d6

manifestation

1
2
3
4
5
6

A brash youth
A formidable warrior
A put-upon father
A wealthy merchant
A mysterious/cagey traveler
A hapless old man

1d6

challenge

1

A test of might (a duel, a wrestling
match, a beast to slay, etc.)
A test of prowess (a race, a climb,
an archery contest, etc.)
A test of endurance (a drinking
contest, a long trudge, an all-night
watch, etc.)
A test of courage (a terror to face,
a fate to accept, an injustice to
prevent, etc.)
A test of honor (a chance to steal,
or cheat, or rig a bet, etc.)
A test of kindness (someone
wretched to help, some wrong to
put right, etc.)

457

Useful or valuable flora

Pick one of the following, or create a new
kind of flora (page 461) for the PCs to
discover.
Either way, keep in mind when and where
the PCs will encounter this. If you’re
presenting something useful, it should be
useful now (perhaps with a bit of care or
effort). Don’t present them with an apple
tree in winter and claim it’s useful. If you’re
presenting them something valuable,
it should either be something they can
harvest now, or something that they can
feasibly come back and get later.

Wild food
Mushrooms, morels, asparagus, leafy
greens, dandelions, all sorts of berries,
cherries, plums, apples, beans, nuts, bulbs,
tubers, etc.
Can gather 1d6× provisions (1d6 uses
each). If you like, roll 1d6 to determine
effort required and/or palatability.
458

Poison
Death caps, hemlock, belladonna, arum,
foxglove, fire lily, oleander, yew, the wrong
parts of many edible plants/mushrooms or
medicinal herbs, etc.
Can gather 1d6 doses (dangerous). If you
like, also roll 1d6 each for potency, rapidity
of onset, and ease of delivery. A skilled
herbalist could refine it, make it more
potent, rapid, or easier to use.

Medicinal herbs
As a poultice, chew, incense, tea, extract,
essential oil, etc.
		Treats injuries/aches; use in lieu of
supplies to Recover (1d6 uses)
		A remedy for a debility, illness, infection, or poison (1d6 uses)
		Valuable in
bulk to a skilled
herbalist (Value 1), the source of a
medicine (or a poison)

Useful or valuable flora

Useful materials
		Vines that could work as rope
		Saplings, good for setting snares
		Saplings, straight and sturdy, good for
arrows/bows/spear shafts
		Large tree, toppled in the Great Wood,
a good source of timber
		Good kindling (dry leaves/needles/
twigs, birch bark, etc.)
		Dry wood/peat for burning

Sample specimens
Brightberry
A thorny shrub with delicious
berries that glow with soft, golden light
(hand). In spring, it smells like baking honeycakes. Harvest berries as provisions
(3 uses, fragile), but they burn away within
small shrub, pot it,
hours. Dig up a
and nurse it to health (tricky); merchants
will pay handsomely for it (Value 2).
Sleeping elksheart
A giant mushroom, sometimes big
as a house, found in dark forests. Fills the
air with paralytic spores (inhaled); the mycelium digest the victims. Carefully collect
spores to use as a poison (dangerous, Value
1). Burn the cap to destroy the spores, cut
it up, and feed a town with it (1d4 Surplus,
immobile, Value 2).
Violet lotus
A lily, grows alone along rivers/
streams in protected eddies. Inhale its
burnt petals to ask the GM a Seek Insight
question about whatever situation you
wish. A tincture of the flower makes a
lovely perfume (Value 1).

Twisting pine
An evergreen that rotates as it grows,
resulting in twisting trunks. The sap flows
freely when tapped, but hardens quickly
unless bottled up and protected from air.
Use a vial in lieu of supplies to Recover
(seals wounds, flakes away days later). Harvest a jar (Value 1) to use as a sealant or
a pottery glaze.
Vanta’s Blade
A low, herbaceous plant with broad,
blade-shaped leaves that absorb light.
Patches of the plant look like holes in reality. A pouch can be made into a rich dye,
ink, or paint (Value 2), but Hillfolk (page
228) and Manmarchers (page 282)
consider it cursed and will burn patches
(and bearers) of it on sight.
Hartwood
A small tree with a distinctive twopronged shape, found growing atop rocky
outcrops. Climb up, chop off a branch, and
burn it; apply the ashes to skin as a ward
against possession.
Snowembers
Pretty flowers found only in certain
snow-covered meadows of the Huffel
Peaks (page 236). The petals are edible
and warm, even in the dead of winter, but
they turn to mush soon after picking. Can
harvest 1d4+4 provisions with a few hours’
effort; those who partake grow flush and
count as wearing warm clothes for the next
day or so.

459

Useful or valuable flora

Questions
		 What’s distinctive about it? How did
you spot or recognize it?
		 What is it (sometimes) called?
		 Who taught you to recognize/use it?
		 What superstition hangs over it?
		 According to the tales, how did this
plant come to be?
		 Why is it thought impossible
to cultivate?
		 What bird, critter, or bug eats it/lives in
it/pollinates it/spreads it?
		 Who back home would be most
excited by this find?

Cultivation
Should the PCs wish to cultivate a useful
or valuable flora, then Make a Plan. Some
example requirements include:
		 You must acquire a source of seeds/
spores/cuttings/sprouts/etc.
		 You must learn the secrets of
its cultivation
		 You must commune with the spirit of
the flora and earn its respect
		 The steading must Pull Together to
clear a plot for and plant the flora
		 It’ll take a season/a year/multiple years
for the flora to mature
		 You risk attracting a threat/it failing to
thrive/it spreading unchecked
Also establish the benefits of success, such as:
		Access to a new special possession
		Extra Surplus in a particular season
		A trade good (Value 1-2) produced
each spring/summer/autumn/winter
		Extra HP regained when Recovering
		Extra folks fed when you use a mess kit
and supplies

Useful or valuable flora
Now, considering all of the above, and
where and how it’s found, decide:
What is it? Pick 1: Lichen, moss, algae,
fungus, mushroom, fern, grass, sedge, plant,
weed, vine, climber, creeper, briar, shrub,
succulent, deciduous tree (willow, ash, oak,
alder, birch, elm, fruit, maple, chestnut,
rowan, walnut, etc.), conifer (cedar, pine, fir,
juniper, cypress, spruce, yew, etc.).
What part is useful/valuable? Pick 1-4:
Trunk, stem, shoot, limb, body, wood, flesh,
bark, skin, root, tuber, thorn, leaf, fruit,
gourd, nut, seed, spore, flower, pollen, pod,
cone, sap, oil, resin, ash, etc.
How is it prepared/used? Pick as many
as make sense: Eaten, chewed, rubbed in,
used as a poultice, burnt, smoked, ground,
pulverized, stripped, soaked, cooked,
infused, reduced, tinctured,
mixed with __, carved.

What is it called? Mix and match words
however makes sense (like “raven’s ash” or
“witchpetal”). Maybe translate it based on
who named it (e.g. to Welsh for a plant
local to Stonetop).

463

Ustrina
These mask-wearing people dwell to the
west, deep in the Huffel Peaks (page
236). Every now and then, they emerge
from the Labyrinth (page 242) and visit
Gordin’s Delve (page 190), trading finely
worked goods, wonders and the odd bit of
orichalcum (page 162).
		Masks of polished metal, only vaguely
humanoid features
		Voluminous black robes and strangely
ornate headdresses
		Faint chemical smells: a whiff of rust,
or ozone, or sulfur
		Tinny, echoing voices, like someone
down a well, speaking an obscure dialect
Tips: speak formally and proceed all proper
nouns with “the” (“the Rhianna,” “the Gordin’s
Delve”); talk into an empty soda can (or at
least with your hand in front of your mouth).

Names
Portuguese-sounding, but often ending in
“-ix”: the Avilio, the Brenix, the Caesana,
the Donatix, the Emitia, the Flagio, the
Galvaix, the Jacintix, the Lucisia, the Marcix, the Numia, the Otavix, the Paolix, the
Ruia, the Sibonio, the Tafia, the Valerix.

Questions
		 Which of you has encountered the
Ustrina before? In Gordin’s Delve, or
elsewhere in the Huffel Peaks?
		 What is it about the way they move
that unnerves you so?
		 Who did you know in Gordin’s Delve
who tried to rob them, and what doom
befell them?
		 What’s the oddest thing that you’ve
heard of the Ustrina asking for in trade?
		 Even though you’ve never seen it, what
do you think they really look like?
464

Lore
Something interesting: The Ustrina dwell
among the ruins of the Forge Lords (page
156), and are heirs to many of their arts.
And while few outsiders can tell, there are
sects among them—the Peridita, the Albedo, the Citrinita, and the Rubedix—with
rivalries and politics aplenty.
Something useful: Ustrina (or their ancestors) were servants of the Forge Lords,
and each group holds the relics of their old
masters in great esteem. They might sell
artifacts of other Forge Lords to outsiders,
but they will pay handsomely to have the
artifacts of their own masters returned.

Hooks
		Ustrina haven’t appeared in Gordin’s
Delve for six months; one of the
Bosses (page 192) offers a reward for
information.
		Mining crews go missing in the First
Delve (page 198); survivors are blaming Ustrina.
		Members of one sect hire someone to
rob a group of another sect (discretely,
of course).
		A trio of masked, black-robed figures
have been seen poking around the
Ruined Tower (page 332), but why?
		A merchant from Gordin’s Delve
brings an Ustrina to Stonetop. It wishes
to study the Stone or Maker-ruins about
town, but a trio of Ustrina (from the
same sect) soon follow, looking for it.

Ustrina

True nature

Trade missions

The Ustrina never remove their masks,
robes, or gloves. Rumors abound, of course,
but if anyone reliable knows what they
truly look like, they aren’t telling. The true
nature of the Ustrina is up to you and your
group to decide, but possibilities include:

Ustrina come to Gordin’s Delve a few
times every season, emerging from the
First Delve (page 198). Some have contacts in the town, and approach those contacts directly. Others approach individuals
seemingly at random, offering to trade.

		Flesh and blood, much like us, but
with elaborate traditions of privacy
and decorum
		Mutant scavengers of the Forge Lords’
ruins, horrible to behold, disguised
when they deal with humanity
		Naught but elemental spirits (page
360), animating a simple skeleton
of bronze
		Constructs of impossibly intricate clockwork, animated by glowing soul-gems
		Ancient servants of the Forge Lords
who have slowly replaced flesh with
clockwork
		A bunch of smaller creatures animating
an elaborate puppet

For any given trade mission, pick or roll
what they’re offering and what they want
in return, using the tables on the following
page. Their wants and needs are inscrutable, making them difficult to haggle with
and fairly easy to offend. They are often
willing to make arrangements now and
return in a few weeks or even months to
make the transaction.

465

466

Ustrina

Curiosities and wonders
Perhaps crafted by the Ustrina themselves,
or pilfered from troves of the Forge Lords. If
the Ustrina do know the secrets to creating
these things, they aren’t inclined to share.
Artificial limb
					crude, magical (?), Value 3
A clockwork hand, foot, arm, or leg, with
straps and buckles to attach it. Impossibly
intricate, and with practice it’s nearly as
functional as the real thing. It cannot feel,
though, and is prone to jamming up and
needing repairs.
Barrel of anthracite
					immobile, dangerous, Value 3
Lumps of lustrous, gray-black stone, like
coal but drier and much more useful. A
small lump catches fire as readily as dry
wood and burns all night with the heat of
a good cook-fire. It casts only dim light
(close, area) and there’s almost no smoke,
but the smell is something awful.
Brass sphere
					 , magical, Value 2
A heavy lump of smooth metal that fits in
one’s hand. When you touch the sphere
with bare flesh, it glows with an orange
light (reach, area, l l l hours). It recharges
an hour of light for every hour that it sits
in a hot fire or coals.
Bright-sticks
					fragile, dangerous, Value 1
A small box containing 3d4 stiff metal
rods, about the size of incense sticks. Snap
one, and each piece bursts into a blindingly
bright, blisteringly hot flame (d8 damage,
messy) for a few seconds while it burns
away to ash.

Clockwork bird
					 , fragile, magical, Value 3
Made of tin and set with agate eyes. When
you wind it up and let it run, it will dance
and chirp loudly for a few minutes. When
you wind it up and twist one of its feathers
just so, it will sit and wait, only starting to
dance and chirp when a creature enters its
line of sight.
Ever-burning ember
					 , fragile, magical, Value 3
This ceramic pot has a number of thin slits
along its shoulder and a ring of rune-etched
brass around its neck. The pot is always hot,
painful to touch, but it won’t cause blisters
outright. Inside is a glowing ember of redwood with a fire spirit bound inside. The
ember will smolder forever as long it stays
in the pot and isn’t completely submerged.
Pale metal gauntlets
					 , fireproof, magical, Value 3
Made of a pale metal, lighter than it should
be, and intricately carved with geometric
patterns. The insides are an impossibly fine
chainmail of the same metal. They seem to
absorb heat, and are often damp.
When you wear the pale metal gauntlets,
you can pick up and manipulate even
white-hot steel without harming your
hands (the rest of you, though, has no
special protection from heat).
Self-burning lamp
					 , reach, area, l l l l l hours, Value 2
A bronze oil-lamp with a small valve on
the top. When you screw the nozzle open,
a hot blue flame pops out of the nozzle.
When you screw the nozzle shut, the flame
snuffs out. The lamp itself gets blisteringly
hot while running, and once empty of fuel,
only the Ustrina know how to refill it.
467

Vor Svetelik

The Green Lords (page 210) called this
place “the Great White City.” It was a
place of beauty, learning, and study. But
when the Fae (page 94) rebelled, the
scholars of Vor Svetelik were tasked with
breeding beasts of war. When the Green
Lords turned in desperation to the Things
Below (page 412), the city’s waters fouled
and its white trees grew sickly. When other
Makers (page 264) shattered what is now
Ferrier’s Fen (page 116), this city became
the Green Lords’ last refuge and the site of
the war’s last battle.
The Green Lords made the invaders pay.
They laid traps. They sent their loyal servants to die in waves. They unleashed new
horrors, each worse than the last, retreating
all the while. And when the Fae and their
Stone Lord (page 382) allies reached the
center of the city, they found it… empty.
Naught remained of the Green Lords,
except tunnels leading deep into the earth,
collapsed behind them.
The Stone Lords retreated to their hills.
The Fae fell to bickering and their armies
disbanded. Those few mortal servants of
the Green Lords who survived were left to
fend for themselves, eventually becoming
the Forest Folk (page 150).
And Vor Svetelik, the Great White City,
was left a blighted, infested ruin.
468

Impressions
		A scraggly canopy in the valley below,
hints of streets and buildings, a broken
white tower
		 Pale and spindly trees, bearing
withered leaves and oozing black sap
		 A steady, damp warmth, like foul
breath, even in the dead of winter
		 That feeling you get when you move a
rock and see the grubs and worms
squirming beneath, but constantly,
everywhere you look

Hooks
		An artifact that the PCs desperately
want lies in the city’s ruins
		 A southern antiquarian (page 260)
knows where to find the ruins, and is
leading a substantial expedition there,
using Stonetop as a basecamp
A Fae chieftain (page 111) is besieging
the ruins, attempting to finally exterminate the Fomoraij
		An earthquake cracks open the ruins;
horrors vomit forth into the surrounding woods

Vor Svetelik

Getting there

Areas

The ruins of Vor Svetelik lie in the Great
Wood (page 200), wherever you (the
GM) need them to be. They should be at
least a few days’ travel from Stonetop.

		Outer slopes of the hills surrounding
the city, jagged, overgrown, littered with
Forest Folk glyphs and invaders’ graves.
		Inner slopes of those same hills, steep
and unstable, trees thinning out as one
descends. Signs of old battle abound.
Stone Lord constructs (page 392) still
stand watch.
		The city’s outskirts, hiding ruins of
manor-houses and plantations.
		The city’s center, tightly packed with
ruins, corpses, and danger.
		The undercity, old sewers, cisterns,
tunnels, basements, and vaults; linked
via erosion, collapse, and beast-burrows.

To find the ruins, PCs will need a guide or
detailed directions.
		A Fae, a spirit of the wild (page 356),
or the Forest Folk (page 150) could
guide them, but wouldn’t want to. All
sane beings shun the ruins.
		A Green Lord ruin might contain a
map or directions, but the PCs would
risk getting lost. Such instructions
would be centuries out of date!
		 Magic like the Sylvan Web (page 521)
could help the PCs find the ruins
themselves, though they’d still risk
getting lost on the way.
		The West Road (page 270) once led
from where Stonetop stands to Vor
Svetelik. It’s broken and long-buried,
but could show the way to one who
knew what to look for.

469

Vor Svetelik

Sites

Terrain

The following can all be found in the city’s
center, though still some distance apart.

Pick 1 or combine 2, or have someone roll
the Die of Fate (d12). If you like, roll 1d6
for size/scope/intensity.

		The Conservatory, once a latticed
dome of living wood and glass, now
burnt, broken, shattered. Bound fertility spirits mournfully tend to hazardous
growths (page 219).
		The Menagerie, mostly intact. Cubbies
line the walls, each with tissue (fur,
tooth, etc.) from a different beast. A
plasmic horror dwells in a central pit.
		The Great Library, broken white
tower, poking through the canopy.
Lower levels largely intact—grand,
overgrown. Heavily warded, a few
chambers still perfectly preserved. Littered with the remains of invaders.
		The Necropolis, cluster of ziggurats,
domes, and monuments near the city’s
center. Ransacked and vandalized, with
few tombs intact. Deeply haunted.
		The Hallow Tree, amphitheater
carved from a single, massive, fossilized
tree stump. Green Lords once met here
to share and debate. All that remains
is a basalt slab, piles and piles of bones,
and hints of tunnels long-ago collapsed.

6

The cracked portal
Somewhere deep in the ruins is a huge slab
of white rock, etched with a magic circle.
A large crack mars the stone and the circle,
yet they still thrum with power.
The circle is a portal (page 306), connected
to those beneath the Ruined Tower (page
332), the Labyrinth (page 242), near
Three Coven Lake (page 438), and so
forth. The portal can be opened (see Opening the Way, page 517), but it is dangerous
and unstable.
When you open the cracked portal (from
either direction), roll +INT: on a 10+, the
portal opens but pick 1 from the list below;
on a 7-9, the portal opens but pick 2:
		 Any who traverse it risk serious harm
—1d10 damage (messy, ignores armor)
		 You draw the attention of some
dangerous primordial entity, like an
archon (page 302) or a voidblight
(page 313)
		 The cracks grow bigger; subsequent
attempts to use this portal will have a
cumulative -1 penalty
On a 6-, the cracked portal shatters violently (1d10 damage, reach, area, messy, forceful,
ignores armor) and leaves a rift (page 311)
in its place. The other portal cracks (or
shatters, if it was already cracked).

boobytraps. Many remain. The most common are fast-growing vines that bear large,
ominous pods.

474

Vor Svetelik

Deathgrip vines
					 In some parts of the city, you’ll find
hunks of stone cracked apart by vines. The
vines bear clusters of burrs all up and down
their stalks, which easily fall off and cling
to clothing, gear, hair, even skin. The burrs
are annoying, maybe a little painful, but
not (immediately) harmful.

Blade wasps
					 Like wasps, but bigger and with
iron-infused stingers. Hey, at least they’re
not venomous.

Throughout the ruins, though, are old sigils
of power. When these burrs are brought
within a few paces of the sigils, they rapidly sprout and start to grow. And burrow.
And grip. And tear.

When you get swarmed by blade wasps,
take 1d6 damage (messy, ignores armor)
and you’re wracked with pain. Doing anything other than curling up and writhing
means that you’re Defying Danger (likely
with CON). Each subsequent time you
take damage from the swarm, increase the
die one step (max d12).

When you try to free yourself of sprouting
deathgrip vines, roll +STR or +DEX (your
call): on a 10+, you can get free if you pick
1 from the list below; on a 7-9, you can get
free if you pick 2:
		 Tear vines from your flesh (1d8 damage,
messy, ignores armor)
		 Lose/discard 1d4 × worth of items
		 Someone else (who isn’t in a similar
plight) needs to help you
On a 6-, take 1d6 damage (messy, grabby,
ignores armor) and the vines continue to
grow, burrow, and constrict. Each time they
deal damage, increase the damage die by
one step.

When you are stung by a single blade wasp,
it hurts, draws blood, and takes some effort
not to cry out.

When you flee a blade wasp swarm, roll
+CON: on a 10+, you escape the swarm
but pick 1 from the list below; on a 7-9,
you escape the swarm but pick 2:
		 Suffer damage from the swarm
		 Mark a debility from blood loss
		 An important piece of gear is lost
			or ruined
		 You run straight into another danger
The Undying
					
		Ghosts (page 76) of mortal soldiers,
reenacting ancient battles
Specters (page 79) of those subjected
to the Green Lords’ most terrible
experiments
		Hollow Fae (page 114), wounded by
iron, abandoned by their fellows
Wraiths (page 79) of ﻿Green Lords or
their ﻿mummified servants, their
canopic jars destroyed
		Mummies of Green Lords (page 221)
and their servants (page 221), safe in
their still-standing tombs

Nemurvojak
					Horde, construct, undead, corrupted,
			fearless
HP 11; Armor 4 (resilience), 0 vs. bronze
Damage iron-wood spears d6 w/advantage
(close, messy, 1 piercing) or bare-handed rending d4 with advantage (hand,
grabby, messy, 1 piercing)
Special qualities bound by its last orders;
shuns the sun or holy light
Instinct to revel in their prey’s fear
and suffering
			ä Rush a foe with an unearthly howl
			ä Lash out with a mass of black, viny
					tendrils (reach, grabby)
			ä Brutalize an already fallen foe
When the Fae rebelled, some of the Green
Lord’s mortal servants—the ancestors of
the Forest Folk (page 150)—sided with
the traitors. But not all. Most remained
loyal, or at least too afraid of their masters
to risk turning on them.
Their loyalty, alas, did not save them.
At first, the Green Lords would plant
demon-seeds in only the bodies of servants
who had already fallen. But when they saw
just how effective those animated corpses
were, well… the Green Lords were nothing if not practical.
Many of these horrors remain in Vor
Svetelik, bound by orders to protect the
city or certain sites within. Thank the
gods they were not allowed
to roam free.

476

Vor Svetelik

Plasmic horror
					Solitary, large, construct, amorphous,
			terrifying
HP 19; Armor 2 (membrane, lacks organs)
Damage pseudopod d10+3 (hand, close,
reach, grabby, forceful) or acidic engulf
d10+5 (hand, grabby, forceful, messy,
ignores armor)
Special qualities child-like intellect;
susceptible to strong wills
Instinct to recklessly explore the world
			ä Ooze through the smallest cracks
or openings
			ä Assume the form (wholly or partially)
of any being it touches (from small
to large size)
			ä Reveal its true form, shockingly
			ä Grow (and gain 1d8 HP) by
consuming a living thing
			ä Spawn a smaller version of itself
These… things… served as blank canvasses
for the Green Lord’s flesh-weaving. Once,
they were kept carefully locked away in
laboratories and breeding pits. Now, with
their creators gone all these years… well,
who knows where they might
be found?

Why haven’t these plasmic horrors spread
beyond Vor Svetelik? That’s for you to
decide, but perhaps…
…		 some Fae magic keeps them contained;
…		 they require some nutrient that is only
available here;
…		 something in their cellular programming
keeps them from wandering far; or
…		 a thrumming artifact emits some sort
of song that they are loathe to leave.
When you command a plasmic horror in the
tongue of the Green Lords, roll +CHA: on a
10+, it does as you say, and accepts you as its
master; on a 7-9, it does as you say for now.
When a plasmic horror thoroughly consumes a living being, it gains 1d8 HP, even
if that takes it above its maximum. When a
plasmic horror spawns a smaller version of
itself, it loses a number of HP and the new
version starts with that same number.
Plasmic horrors can be “locked” in their
current forms by a particular incantation
and a drop of Green Lord blood. Good
luck getting a hold of either.

477

Waystones

Something useful: To step onto the Fae
paths, you must leave behind all iron and
steel, plus one of the following:

The Great Wood (page 200) and Ferrier’s
Fen (page 116) are dotted with massive
markers of pale gray stone, moss-covered
and largely overgrown, deeply etched with
the curling, branching runes of the Green
Lords (page 210). Fae magic (page 94)
binds the waystones together.

Lore
Something interesting: The waystones
mark entrances to the Fae paths. If one can
access the paths, they can quickly travel
great distances. The paths, however, are
fraught with peril. Tales tell of travelers
being lost or entrapped for years or
even centuries.
478

		Follow the footsteps of a Fae guide
		Be able to see through Fae magic and
glamours, via Old Mother’s Wine
(page 509) or a similar effect
		Go at a certain time (under a __ moon,
at midnight, at sunrise, etc.) and either...
... have Fae blood in your veins; or
... open your mind with drugs/alcohol/
delirium/exhaustion/etc.
Each waystone connects to at least one
other, and most connect to a few. When
you step on the path, you see the routes
laid out before you, and can sense roughly
where in the mortal realm each of the
adjoining waystones lies.
Travelling the Fae paths is dangerous, but
safer when you go with trusted companions.

Waystones

Walking the paths
When you walk the Fae path from one
waystone to the next, roll +WIS: on a 6-, you
lose your way and find yourself in peril—tell
us what chases or lures you from the path;
on a 7+, you arrive safely, only a few minutes
having passed, but first tell us what tried to
chase or lure you from the path and why it
failed; on a 10+, you can also intervene and
save someone who has lost their way, but
only if you can tell us how.
If you roll a 6- and someone saves you, don’t
mark XP.
When you lose your way and none of your
allies save you, decide if you wish to keep
playing this character. If you do, you will
escape from the Fae domains, a relatively
short time having passed in the real world,
but for you it might have taken days,
months, or even years. Pick 2:
		You’re different now. Change your
instinct, change your look, and lose one
of your moves, replacing it with another
move available to you.
		You lost something important: a move,
a precious item, the memory of a loved
one, an earnest hope, etc. Tell us what
you lost.
		You owe some favor to a Fae—one who
ensnared you or one who rescued you.
Work with the GM to determine who.
		Days, weeks, or months have passed
in the mortal realm, instead of minutes
or hours.
		You emerge at a different waystone
than the one you intended to reach.

If a player needs inspiration, have them roll
the Die of Fate.
1d6

lure

1

The need to escape from danger,
heartbreak, or shame
Someone lost to you, calling out
A child, beast, or loved one in distress
A revel, a meal, a drink, or some
other pleasure of the flesh
Something of great beauty or wonder
Something of great value, or a chance
to learn something of great import

2
3
4
5
6

Stepping off the paths
When one steps off the Fae paths, they
might find themselves in any number of
Fae domains. It’s nearly impossible for a
mortal to navigate between them, as the
ways are ruled by dreamlike logic and
secrets and ancient Fae pacts. A guide is all
but essential, but even the Fae might
struggle to access or escape certain realms.

Hooks
		Something dangerous has learned to
use the waystones, emerging near the
one closest to Stonetop, causing trouble, and retreating.
		Someone was lost to the Fae paths
years or decades or even centuries ago.
Now they reappear, stumbling back to
Stonetop, thinking that only hours or
days have passed.
		Someone the PCs care about steps
onto the Fae paths and doesn’t return.

When a follower loses their way and no one
saves them, their fate is in the GM’s hands.

479

The Whitefang Mountains
The Whitefang Mountains form the northern border of the World’s End, running
from the Huffel Peaks (page 236) in the
northwest, all along the Great Wood (page
200) and the North Manmarch (page
282), to the Dread River (page 86) and
beyond in the east. Beyond them lie nothing
but the Frozen Wastes (page 172).
The mountains are tall and jagged, steep
and forbidding. Yet they hide almost
indescribable beauty: waterfalls, deep pine
forests, sweeping meadows. They also hide
a surprising number of settlements. Barrier Pass (page 26) is closest to Stonetop,
but many more lie beyond.

Hooks
		Storms around Tor’s Fist grow stronger;
the colors shift to red and yellow
		 A band of kyakaara (page 493) slips
out of the mountains and is eyeing
Stonetop
		 A southern merchant is intent on
finding a mountain village, to trade for
ivory and fine wool
		Mountain apes (page 494) hold an
artifact/scrap of lore in their hoard
		 An apprentice shantyara (page 495)
needs help retrieving her dead master’s
sword from the bhoka (page 490)
who killed him

Impressions
Always
		Snow-covered peaks, like mighty teeth
		 Wind: howling, whispering, gusting
		 Monkeys screeching and chittering in
the valleys, leaping through the trees
		 Prayer-flags fluttering in the wind
		 The elation of reaching a summit; the
despair of realizing it was just a ridge,
and you’re only half-way
Spring
		Slopes and vales bright with rhododendron: purple, red, and orange
		 Trickle and rush of snowmelt

480

Summer
		Lush green below; stern, colorless
peaks above
		 Birdsong echoing and echoing
Autumn
		Meadows turned brown and red
and gold
		 Mist filling the valleys, clouds gliding
by above
Winter
		Snow cloaking the valleys, choking passes
		 Tears freezing, sweat soaking you under
warm clothes

The Whitefang Mountains

Tor’s Fist
Almost straight north of Stonetop, this
lonely mountain juts above its neighbors.
It’s not the tallest peak in the Whitefangs,
but it’s tall enough. When summer storms
roll up from the south and crash against
the mountains, the worst of them swirl
around Tor’s Fist. They’ll lash the peak
with lightning, and in the aftermath, green
and purple lights dance around the crown.
What’s up there?
Clearly Tor’s Fist holds some mystery, but
its nature is for you to decide. Perhaps the
mountain’s peak holds…
…		 the tomb of Stormcatcher (page 332),
or her son, or her lover;
…		 a cleft, from which the Stone (page
12) itself was carved;
…		 one of the last thirteen Rime Lords
(page 316), still in stasis, protected by
powerful tulpas (page 324);
…		 a potent storm-spirit, corrupted by the
Things Below (page 432), but bound
there by ancient Rime Lord magic;
…		 the domain of a powerful, temperamental Fae (page 94);
…		 a shattered portal (page 306), torn
open and ruined;
…		 a people of great stature, who worship
Tor (page 452);
…		 the last of the Tempest Lords (page
398), grumpy and aloof;
…		 some combination of the above; or
…		 something else entirely.

Chart a Course
To reach the summit of Tor’s Fist:
		 You’ll need to wait until late spring or
summer to have any chance
		 First you must travel to the Foothills
(page 144) or Barrier Pass (page 26)
		 From there, you must travel to the base
of Tor’s Fist
		 It’ll take at least 5 days (from the
Foothills) or 6 days (from Barrier
Pass), and a corresponding amount
of supplies
		 You need a knowledgeable guide or
accurate map
		 You need to bring warm clothes,
climbing gear, and fuel for cookfires
		 The terrain is treacherous; you
risk injury
		 The way will be grueling; you risk
exhausting yourselves/your resources
		 The way is perilous, filled
with dangers
		 From the base of Tor’s Fist, you can
attempt to reach the summit
		 It’ll take at least 5 days, lest you get
altitude sickness (page 489)
		 You need to bring a skilled
mountaineer
		 The climb is treacherous and grueling;
you risk injury, exhaustion, or both
		 You risk drawing the attention of
whatever it is that’s up there
		 You must brave the final ascent: a
sheer climb in heavy winds

Mountain folk
The Whitefangs host a surprising number
of settlements. Most are small farming valleys, even more isolated than Stonetop. But
there are also a number of fortress-monasteries, like Barrier Pass (page 26).
Something interesting: The mountain
folk’s ancestors were disciples of the Rime
Lords (page 316), and Rime Lord teachings still influence the culture. But time,
isolation, and competing philosophies have
led to vastly different local traditions. An
idea or practice that is lauded in one village
might be ridiculed in the next. And in the
village beyond that? It might get you killed.
Something useful: Communities occasionally trade with their nearest neighbors,
and folk in one settlement are familiar with
the ways of the next—at least well enough
to avoid offense or violence. But if you
seek a guide or informant, find one who’s
actually been there, lest you have your ears
filled with exaggerated nonsense.

Names
Nepalese- and Tibetan-inspired: Aandha,
Araayin, Bisheej, Choden, Dawa, Dhaar,
Dorji, Duga, Fayurma, Hopan, Jamya, Jyat,
Keshhay, Ksali, Kundari, Kunza, Lhamo,
Mitis, Nobu, Nyado, Passan, Pratar, Puyari,
Radhaa, Ravar, Sajadr, Sonam, Supar, Tani,
Tashi, Tenzi, Tseri, Wanchu, Yonta.

The Whitefang Mountains

Questions
		 Who among you have traveled here
before? When and why?
		 Who do you know (in Gordin’s Delve,
perhaps) who hails from the Whitefangs?
		 Why do you think storms swirl around
Tor’s Fist? What do you think those
strange lights are?
		 Did you once live in a farm valley, or a
fortress-monastery?
		 What drove you to leave?
		 What behavior in Stonetop is strictly
taboo where you once lived?
		 What practice is common where you
once lived, but utterly baffling to folk
in Stonetop?
		 How is (are) the language(s) of the
mountain folk different from those of
Stonetop?
		 Do they worship ___ up there? If so,
how is that worship different than in
Stonetop?

484

Farm valleys
The valleys of the Whitefangs can be
surprisingly pleasant. Streams flow almost
year-round, freezing over but still running
throughout winter. Below the tree line, tall
skinny conifers grow in abundance. Many
of these vales hold steadings.
Steading names
Amchatala, Bhatand, Bhungadi, Dhulgad,
Dhurankat, Ghumjun, Heledhan, Khutan,
Methala, Naungadi, Potyata, Sakokoch,
Thulalu, Thundind.
Size hamlet (~25 to 75 souls) or village
(~150 to 350 souls)
Population -1 to +1
Prosperity -1 to +1
		 Trade with...
... other farm valleys (food, spouses)
... mammoth herders (ivory, meat,
dark ice, see page 322)

barley, lentils
		 Livestock: goats, sheep, milk, meat
		 Fine wool
		Timber
		Hunting/fishing
		 Trades: weaving, dyeing, bronzesmithing, etc.
Defenses -1 for a hamlet, +0 for
a village
		 Some spears, slings, bows
		 Some bronze tools/weapons
		 Protection from a nearby
fortress-monastery

The Whitefang Mountains

Fortress-monasteries
At least a dozen fortress-monasteries remain, scattered throughout the Whitefang
Mountains. Barrier Pass (page 26) is
one of the largest and most powerful. Each
is dedicated to preserving the teachings of
the Rime Lords (page 316)—or at least,
preserving their version of them!
Size hamlet (~25 to 75 souls)
Population +0
Prosperity +0
		 Trade with nearby farm valleys
(food, livestock, wool)
Resources
		 Tithes from nearby farm valleys
		 Terraced gardens (barley, lentils)
		 Livestock (goats, sheep, milk, meat)
		 Fine wool
		 Parchment, vellum, ink
		 Trades: bronze-smithing, weaving,
dyeing, tanning, scribing, etc.
		 Rime Lord artifacts
Defenses +2
		Guardians (page 33)
		 At least one adept (page 328)
		 One or more tulpas (page 324)
		 Weapons/armor of bronze, maybe
dark ice (page 322)
		 Defensible position
A typical fortress-monastery has 25-50
residents, split between monks (page 33)
and guardians (page 33), plus a handful
of layfolk. Most receive food and material
support from a nearby farm valley or two.
In turn, guardians patrol the region and
monks (ostensibly) guide, teach, and serve
as an example for villagers.

Dangers
Hazards
		 Getting lost (maze of mountains,
canyons, passes, and valleys)
		 Sheer rock face to climb, and a reason
to climb it

paths, wet stone, scree, thin waterlogged soil, mud, crumbling rock)

forward and risk it, or backtrack and
lose half a day?
		Mudslides and rockslides (page 376)
Harsh mountain weather
		 Baking sun, dry air
		Fog banks/low clouds, blocking vision
and soaking everything
		 Wind: drowning out voices, threatening
to toss you aside
		 Rain and wind rolling up from below
		 Rain and sleet: pelting you, soaking
you, threatening to wash you away
		 Thunder or lightning: making you
flinch/jerk/jolt, triggering rockslides
		 Snow: hiding a drop, burying a pass,
making trails impassible
		 Brutal cold, leading
to hypothermia &
frostbite (next page)

488

The Whitefang Mountains

Avalanche
					 Everyone who lives in these mountains learns to spot the signs.
Instinct to gather mass, to bury all in its path
ä Have one cascade down a distant
slope, majestic and terrifying
ä Point out the snowpack, looming on
the slopes above
ä Note how a storm adds mass to
the snowpack
ä Point out the dimples as sun/warmth
undermine its structure
When an avalanche starts above them,
start the following:
l		 A chunk of snow gives way
l		 It gains mass, momentum; it’ll be here

in a matter of minutes

l		 It rushes towards them, imminent
l		 They’re buried under snow and ice

(d8+2 damage, forceful, ignores armor)

l		 They’re trapped, disoriented, suffering

hypothermia & frostbite (see below)

Hypothermia & frostbite
					 It gets cold in the mountains. Very,
very cold.
ä		 Start them shivering, despite fire/
warm clothing
ä		 Test their endurance on even
simple tasks
ä		 Have them lose their footing/grip
ä		 Inflict a debility
ä		 Have frostbite set in (d6 damage,
ignores armor)
ä		 Have frostbite get worse (+messy)
ä		 Compel them to do something foolish
(go to sleep, shed clothing, etc.)

Altitude sickness
					 Dangerously thin air starts a couple
thousand feet below the snowline, and
gets worse the higher you go. Locals are
accustomed to the thin air, but even they
must be careful.
ä		 Test their endurance to keep going, or
just to breathe
ä		 Inflict a debility
ä		 Reveal that they’ve missed something
ä		 Have them collapse, unable to continue
ä		 Fill their lungs with fluid, swell their
brains, have them roll for Death’s Door
Something interesting: Disciples of the
Rime Lords practice techniques to thrive
in the thin air, but they take months or
years to master.
Something useful: One can acclimate safely by ascending no more than ~1,000 feet
each day, and even then, extreme exertion
can be dangerous. A tea steeped from purple
drakewort can alleviate symptoms, but an
overdose can cause death by suffocation.
Monsters
		Predators: cave bears (page 205),
﻿cougars (page 206), and ﻿wolves
(page 209)
		Spirits of the wild (page 356)
		Wraiths (page 79) of those who died
of starvation and want, or corrupted by
the Howling Curse (page 492)
		Tulpas (page 324), free-roaming or
bound to something nearby
People
		Guardians, monks, or novices from
Barrier Pass (page 26)
		An adept (page 328)
		A false adept (page 329), or
their minions

The Whitefang Mountains

Bhoka
					Solitary, large, cautious, magical,
corrupted
HP 16; Armor 1 (hide)
Damage talons 1d10+3 (hand, close, grabby,
forceful)
Special qualities blinded by full daylight
or holy light, takes +1d4 damage from
bronze
Instinct see below
ä Slink through trees and shadows
These hulking brutes dwell in dark places:
caves, north-facing slopes, and deep mountain forests. They’re hairy things, hunched
and wild, only vaguely looking like people.
They’d be little more than dangerous
beasts, easily avoided, if they weren’t driven
by an obsessive vice—and if they didn’t
wield the wild elements themselves.

Something interesting: The people of the
Whitefangs fear bhoka and are well aware
of the dangers they pose, but they never
hunt them. They seem content to drive
them off or appease them.
Something useful: The monks claim that
bhoka descended from Rime Lords (page
316) who were corrupted by the Things
Below (page 412). They cannot abide the
sound of the Rime Lords’ meditative chants,
recoiling and fleeing from anyone who
performs such a chant without faltering.
Guardian
					Group, organized, hardy, skilled
HP 10; Armor 2 (hides, shields)
Damage bronze or dark ice-tipped
weapons 1d8 (tags by weapon)
Instinct to protect their charges
ä Use the terrain to their advantage
